topics.title: Compilers - The Translation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Compilers - The Translation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **compiler** is a systematic software program that translates source code written in a high-level programming language (HLL) into a lower-level language—typically machine code, assembly language, or an intermediate representation (IR)—while preserving the semantic meaning, logical structure, and intended behavior of the original program. This translation process is the central focus of compiler design and forms the foundation of modern software engineering.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the **translation process** refers to the structured, multi-phase pipeline through which a source program is systematically converted into an executable target program, often augmented by pre-processing, assembling, linking, and loading operations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> A compiler is a translator whose source language is a high-level programming language and whose target language is typically a low-level language such as assembly or machine code. The translation process is decomposed into well-defined phases: **Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Intermediate Code Generation → Optimization → Code Generation**, with auxiliary support from a **Symbol Table** and an **Error Handler**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Translator

Imagine you have a brilliant book written in **Malayalam**, and you need to deliver its ideas to a French reader. What do you do?

1. You hire a **human translator** who first **reads sentence by sentence** (lexical analysis — breaking text into words).
2. The translator then **understands the grammar** — identifying subjects, verbs, and clauses (syntax analysis — building grammatical structure).
3. The translator checks whether the **meaning is faithful** — does "you are welcome" wrongly become "you are going"? (semantic analysis — ensuring logical correctness).
4. The translator may **rewrite** the text into a cleaner intermediate draft (intermediate code generation).
5. The translator then **polishes** the draft, removing redundancy and awkward phrasing (optimization).
6. Finally, the translator **writes the French version** (code generation).

A compiler does **exactly the same thing**, but with programming languages! It takes your English-like C code and translates it into the binary "French" that your CPU understands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Examinations:**
> Almost every Module 1 question tests your understanding of *what each phase does*, *what the input and output of each phase is*, and *how a compiler differs from an interpreter, assembler, and preprocessor*. Mastering this overview is the gateway to scoring high marks in the entire course.

### 1.3 The Complete Translation Process — High-Level View

The translation from source program to executable is **not** a single monolithic step. It is a coordinated pipeline:

$$\text{Source Program} \rightarrow \text{Preprocessor} \rightarrow \text{Compiler} \rightarrow \text{Assembly} \rightarrow \text{Assembler} \rightarrow \text{Object Code} \rightarrow \text{Linker/Loader} \rightarrow \text{Executable}$$

Each stage has a specific responsibility, and a holistic understanding requires studying all of them.

### 1.4 Key Terminology Table

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Source Program** | Original high-level language code (e.g., `.c`, `.java`) written by the programmer. |
| **Target Program** | Low-level output (assembly, machine code, bytecode) produced by the compiler. |
| **Token** | The smallest meaningful unit of the source program (keywords, identifiers, operators, constants). |
| **Lexeme** | The actual character sequence matched to a token pattern. |
| **Symbol Table** | A data structure that stores identifiers and their attributes (type, scope, memory location). |
| **Intermediate Representation (IR)** | A machine-independent abstract form of the source code used between analysis and synthesis. |
| **Front End** | Phases that depend only on the source language (lexical, syntax, semantic analysis). |
| **Back End** | Phases that depend on the target machine (code generation, optimization, target code emission). |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Translation Process — A Phase-Wise Deconstruction

The complete translation process is a **six-phase logical pipeline** supported by two global modules (Symbol Table and Error Handler). Let us dissect each phase rigorously.

#### **Phase 1: Lexical Analysis (Scanning)**
- **Role:** Reads the source program character by character and groups them into **tokens**.
- **Tool:** Lexical Analyzer (implemented using **Finite Automata** or tools like Lex/Flex).
- **Output:** A stream of `<token, lexeme>` pairs.
- **Removes:** Comments, whitespace, and unnecessary formatting.

> [!NOTE]
> Example: `position := initial + rate * 60` becomes:
> `<id, position> <assign_op, :=> <id, initial> <plus_op, +> <id, rate> <mult_op, *> <num, 60>`

#### **Phase 2: Syntax Analysis (Parsing)**
- **Role:** Takes the token stream and constructs a **parse tree** (or **syntax tree**) using **Context-Free Grammars (CFGs)**.
- **Tool:** Parser (LL, LR, SLR, LALR techniques).
- **Output:** Hierarchical tree structure that proves the program is grammatically correct.
- **Detects:** Syntax errors (missing semicolons, unmatched parentheses, illegal statements).

#### **Phase 3: Semantic Analysis**
- **Role:** Checks whether the parse tree makes *semantic* sense.
- **Key Activities:** Type checking, scope resolution, declaration checks, operator compatibility.
- **Output:** Annotated parse tree (syntax tree with type and scope information).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question:** "Why is semantic analysis necessary if syntax analysis has already checked the program?"
> **Answer:** Syntax analysis checks *structure*; semantic analysis checks *meaning*. For example, `int x = "hello";` is grammatically valid but semantically invalid (type mismatch).

#### **Phase 4: Intermediate Code Generation**
- **Role:** Translates the annotated tree into an **Intermediate Representation (IR)**.
- **Common Forms:** Three-Address Code (TAC), Static Single Assignment (SSA), Quadruples, Triples.
- **Example (TAC):**
> `t1 := 60 * rate`
> `t2 := initial + t1`
> `position := t2`

#### **Phase 5: Code Optimization**
- **Role:** Applies transformations to the IR to produce faster, smaller, or more efficient code.
- **Types:** Machine-independent (constant folding, dead code elimination, loop optimization) and machine-dependent (register allocation, peephole optimization).
- **Output:** Optimized IR (semantically equivalent, performance improved).

#### **Phase 6: Code Generation**
- **Role:** Maps the optimized IR into **target machine code** (assembly or relocatable binary).
- **Key Tasks:** Instruction selection, register allocation, instruction scheduling.
- **Output:** Target assembly or machine code.

### 2.2 Auxiliary Components

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Symbol Table Manager** | Maintains identifiers, their types, scopes, and memory addresses. Used by ALL phases. |
| **Error Handler** | Detects, reports, and (if possible) recovers from lexical, syntactic, and semantic errors. |

### 2.3 Pre-Processing, Assembly, Linking & Loading

The compiler alone is not enough — we need supporting tools:

| Stage | Tool | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| Preprocessing | Preprocessor (e.g., `cpp`) | Handles macros (`#define`), file inclusion (`#include`), conditional compilation. |
| Assembly | Assembler | Translates assembly language into relocatable machine code. |
| Linking | Linker | Combines multiple object files and library routines into a single executable. Resolves external references. |
| Loading | Loader | Copies the executable into memory, performs address relocation, and starts execution. |

### 2.4 Compiler vs Interpreter

| Feature | Compiler | Interpreter |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| **Translation** | Whole program at once | Line-by-line / statement-by-statement |
| **Execution Speed** | Faster (already compiled) | Slower (translates every time) |
| **Error Detection** | After full compilation | Immediately at the error statement |
| **Output** | Standalone executable file | No separate file produced |
| **Examples** | GCC, Clang, javac (to bytecode) | Python, Ruby, PHP engines |
| **Memory** | Generates intermediate files | Generally lower memory footprint |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hybrid models exist:** Java uses both — `javac` compiles to bytecode, and the **JVM** interprets/JIT-compiles the bytecode. This blends both paradigms.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Key Formula / Relationship | Application |
|---------|---------------------------|-------------|
| **Total Compilation Time (T)** | $T = T_{\text{lex}} + T_{\text{parse}} + T_{\text{sem}} + T_{\text{IR}} + T_{\text{opt}} + T_{\text{codegen}}$ | Where $T_{\text{phase}}$ is the time spent in each phase. |
| **Speedup Ratio** | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{\text{original}}}{T_{\text{optimized}}}$ | Used in code optimization evaluation. |
| **Tokenization Rate** | $\text{Rate} = \dfrac{N_{\text{tokens}}}{T_{\text{lex}}}$ (tokens/sec) | Lexer performance metric. |
| **Optimization Gain** | $\text{Gain} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{\text{optimized}}}{T_{\text{unoptimized}}}$ | Fractional improvement from optimization. |
| **Grouping Principle** | A compiler combines $k$ separate files of total size $S$ into one execution pipeline. | Used in **pass** calculations. |
| **Bootstrapping** | A compiler $C_{A \rightarrow B}$ for language A on machine M is written in language A itself. | Self-hosting compiler construction. |

> [!NOTE]
> The vertical bar symbol (used for set notation like $\vert x \vert$) must be written as `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Development:** Every IDE (VS Code, IntelliJ) uses compiler front-ends for **real-time error detection, autocomplete, and refactoring**.
- **Embedded Systems:** Compilers for ARM, AVR, and RISC-V architectures translate C/C++ into microcontroller-specific binaries.
- **Cybersecurity:** Static analyzers and vulnerability scanners leverage compiler infrastructure (LLVM, GCC plugins).
- **AI/ML:** Frameworks like **MLIR** (Multi-Level Intermediate Representation) extend compiler technology to optimize tensor computations.
- **Database Systems:** Query optimizers are conceptually similar to compiler optimizers — both transform IR into efficient execution plans.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Tracing a Statement Through Every Phase

Let us trace the following statement through the entire compilation pipeline:

$$\text{position} := \text{initial} + \text{rate} \times 60$$

This single-line trace will solidify your understanding of how the translation process works.

#### **Step 1: Lexical Analysis — Token Stream Generation**

The lexer reads characters left-to-right and groups them into tokens based on **regular expression patterns**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Input Characters: } p, o, s, i, t, i, o, n, \text{(space)}, :, =, \text{(space)}, i, n, i, t, i, a, l, \text{(space)}, +, \ldots
\end{aligned}
$$

The lexer matches these against patterns:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&[a-zA-Z]^+ \rightarrow \text{identifier} \\
&[0-9]^+ \rightarrow \text{number} \\
&:=, +, \times \rightarrow \text{operators}
\end{aligned}
$$

Resulting token stream:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{id}_1 \rightarrow &\ \text{position} \\
\text{assign\_op} \rightarrow &\ := \\
\text{id}_2 \rightarrow &\ \text{initial} \\
\text{plus\_op} \rightarrow &\ + \\
\text{id}_3 \rightarrow &\ \text{rate} \\
\text{mult\_op} \rightarrow &\ \times \\
\text{num} \rightarrow &\ 60
\end{aligned}
$$

#### **Step 2: Syntax Analysis — Parse Tree Construction**

The parser uses the **Context-Free Grammar rule**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{expression} &\rightarrow \text{expression} + \text{term} \mid \text{term} \\
\text{term} &\rightarrow \text{term} \times \text{factor} \mid \text{factor} \\
\text{factor} &\rightarrow \text{id} \mid \text{num}
\end{aligned}
$$

This builds the following hierarchy (simplified syntax tree):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{assignment} \\
&\quad \vert \\
&\quad \text{id}(\text{position}) \quad \text{expression} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \vert \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{expression} + \text{term} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \vert \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{term} \times \text{factor} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \vert \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{id}(\text{rate}) \quad \text{num}(60)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This parse tree proves the statement is **grammatically correct**. The plus and multiplication operators have correct precedence.

#### **Step 3: Semantic Analysis — Type & Scope Checking**

The semantic analyzer walks the tree and:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1: } & \text{position, initial, rate are declared? Look up Symbol Table.} \\
\text{Check 2: } & \text{rate is a float/int? } 60 \text{ is int? Multiplication produces int/float?} \\
\text{Check 3: } & \text{initial type matches the result of (rate * 60)?} \\
\text{Check 4: } & \text{position's declared type is compatible with the final value.}
\end{aligned}
$$

If all checks pass, the tree is **annotated with type information**.

#### **Step 4: Intermediate Code Generation — Three-Address Code**

The annotated tree is linearized into TAC (each instruction has at most three operands):

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= \text{inttoreal}(60) \\
t_2 &:= t_1 \times \text{rate} \\
t_3 &:= \text{initial} + t_2 \\
\text{position} &:= t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Notice the **implicit type conversion** (`inttoreal`) is explicit in the IR — this is the semantic analysis being preserved.

#### **Step 5: Code Optimization — Algebraic Simplification**

A simple optimization: $60$ is an integer constant and `rate` is a real number. We can precompute the constant folding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= 60.0 \quad \text{(constant folded from inttoreal(60))} \\
t_2 &:= t_1 \times \text{rate} \\
t_3 &:= \text{initial} + t_2 \\
\text{position} &:= t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

Further optimization — strength reduction and common subexpression elimination could apply in larger programs.

#### **Step 6: Code Generation — Target Assembly (x86-like)**

The optimized TAC is translated into assembly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{MOVF} \ t_1, \ \text{\#60.0} \quad\quad \text{; load constant into $t_1$} \\
&\text{MULF} \ t_2, \ t_1, \ \text{rate} \quad \text{; $t_2 = t_1 \times \text{rate}$} \\
&\text{ADDF} \ t_3, \ \text{initial}, \ t_2 \quad \text{; $t_3 = \text{initial} + t_2$} \\
&\text{STF} \ \text{position}, \ t_3 \quad\quad\quad \text{; store $t_3$ into position}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is then assembled into **machine code** by the assembler.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Phase-by-Phase Summary

| Phase | Input | Output | Major Technique | Error Detected |
|-------|-------|--------|-----------------|----------------|
| Lexical | Character stream | Token stream | Regular expressions, DFA | Lexical errors (illegal characters, unterminated strings) |
| Syntax | Token stream | Parse tree / AST | CFG, LL/LR parsing | Syntax errors (grammar violations) |
| Semantic | Parse tree | Annotated AST | Type systems, attribute grammars | Type mismatches, undeclared variables |
| IR Generation | Annotated AST | Intermediate code | Translation schemes | None (assumes previous phases succeeded) |
| Optimization | Intermediate code | Optimized IR | Data-flow analysis, transformations | None (preserves semantics) |
| Code Generation | Optimized IR | Assembly / machine code | Instruction selection, register allocation | Limited (target architecture issues) |

### 3.3 Pass Count Analysis (Derivation)

A **pass** is one complete traversal of the intermediate code (or its equivalent). Let $N$ be the number of phases and $P$ be the number of passes. For a typical compiler:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\min} &= 1 \quad \text{(single-pass compiler — limited optimization)} \\
P_{\max} &= N \quad \text{(multi-pass — one pass per phase, allows rich optimization)} \\
P_{\text{typical}} &= 2 \text{ to } 4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two-pass compilers** (e.g., early Pascal compilers) use:
- **Pass 1:** Front-end (lex, parse, semantic) + IR generation.
- **Pass 2:** Back-end (optimization + code generation).

**Single-pass compilers** (e.g., early C compilers) are faster to compile but produce less optimal code.

### 3.4 Bootstrapping Derivation (Conceptual)

The **bootstrapping problem**: To compile language $L$ for machine $M$, we need a compiler for $L$ on $M$. But to build one, we need $L$ working on $M$!

**Solution — Three-Step Bootstrapping:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} & \quad \text{Write compiler } C_{L \rightarrow M} \text{ in assembly language of } M. \\
\text{Step 2:} & \quad \text{Write a SUBSET compiler } C_{L_{\text{sub}} \rightarrow M} \text{ in language } L. \\
\text{Step 3:} & \quad \text{Use } C_{L_{\text{sub}} \rightarrow M} \text{ to compile the FULL compiler written in } L.
\end{aligned}
$$

This is how languages like **C, Rust, and Go** are self-hosted.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Translation Pipeline — Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the end-to-end flow from source program to executable:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source Program C File] --> preB[Preprocessor cpp]
    preB --> srcC[Preprocessed Source File]
    srcC --> compD[Compiler Front End]
    compD --> symTabA[Symbol Table]
    compD --> errHandA[Error Handler]
    compD --> assmE[Assembly Code]
    assmE --> assmF[Assembler]
    assmF --> objG[Relocatable Object File]
    objG --> linkH[Linker]
    libK[Library Object Files] --> linkH
    linkH --> exeI[Executable Binary]
    exeI --> loadJ[Loader]
    loadJ --> memL[Memory Image Ready to Execute]
```

### 4.2 Detailed Compiler Phase Diagram

This diagram shows the **six logical phases** plus the global symbol table and error handler:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    input1[Source Code Character Stream] --> phase1A[Phase 1 Lexical Analyzer]
    phase1A --> tokenOut[Token Stream]
    tokenOut --> phase2A[Phase 2 Syntax Analyzer Parser]
    phase2A --> parseTree[Parse Tree AST]
    parseTree --> phase3A[Phase 3 Semantic Analyzer]
    phase3A --> annTree[Annotated AST with Types]
    annTree --> phase4A[Phase 4 Intermediate Code Generator]
    phase4A --> irOut[Intermediate Representation IR]
    irOut --> phase5A[Phase 5 Code Optimizer]
    phase5A --> optIR[Optimized IR]
    optIR --> phase6A[Phase 6 Code Generator]
    phase6A --> targetOut[Target Machine Code]
    symTabB[Symbol Table Manager] -.-> phase1A
    symTabB -.-> phase2A
    symTabB -.-> phase3A
    symTabB -.-> phase4A
    symTabB -.-> phase5A
    symTabB -.-> phase6A
    errHandB[Error Handler] -.-> phase1A
    errHandB -.-> phase2A
    errHandB -.-> phase3A
```

### 4.3 Compiler vs Interpreter — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph COMPILE[Compiler Pipeline]
        c1[Source Code] --> c2[Full Translation Step]
        c2 --> c3[Executable File]
        c3 --> c4[Direct Execution on Hardware]
    end
    subgraph INTERPRET[Interpreter Pipeline]
        i1[Source Code] --> i2[Read One Statement]
        i2 --> i3[Translate Statement]
        i3 --> i4[Execute Statement Immediately]
        i4 --> i5[Loop Until Program Ends]
    end
```

### 4.4 Front-End vs Back-End Partitioning (Modular Compiler Design)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRONT[Compiler Front End Source Dependent]
        f1[Lexical Analysis] --> f2[Syntax Analysis]
        f2 --> f3[Semantic Analysis]
        f3 --> f4[IR Generation]
    end
    subgraph BACK[Compiler Back End Target Dependent]
        b1[Optimization] --> b2[Code Generation]
        b2 --> b3[Target Machine Code]
    end
    FRONT --> BACK
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a block diagram of a compiler, ALWAYS include the **Symbol Table** and **Error Handler** boxes, drawn with dashed lines connecting them to ALL six phases. Examiners award extra marks for completeness.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

#### **Question 1: Define a compiler. List the six phases of a compiler. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Model Answer:**

A compiler is a software program that translates a source program written in a high-level programming language into an equivalent target program in a low-level language (assembly or machine code) while preserving the semantic meaning of the original.

The **six phases of compilation** are:

1. **Lexical Analysis** — converts the source character stream into tokens.
2. **Syntax Analysis** — builds a parse tree from the token stream using grammar rules.
3. **Semantic Analysis** — performs type checking, scope resolution, and other meaning-based checks.
4. **Intermediate Code Generation** — produces a machine-independent IR.
5. **Code Optimization** — applies transformations to improve performance.
6. **Code Generation** — translates the optimized IR into target machine code.

These phases are supported by two global modules: the **Symbol Table Manager** and the **Error Handler**.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for listing phases with one-line descriptions.)*

---

#### **Question 2: Differentiate between a compiler and an interpreter. Give one example of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Compiler | Interpreter |
|---------|----------|-------------|
| Translation Mode | Translates the **entire** program at once | Translates and executes **line-by-line** |
| Output | Produces a **standalone executable** | **No** standalone output is produced |
| Execution Speed | Faster (no translation during run) | Slower (translates every run) |
| Error Detection | Reports all errors after full compilation | Stops at the **first error** |
| Example | **GCC** (for C), **javac** | **Python interpreter**, **Ruby engine** |

*(3 marks: 2 for tabular differences, 1 for examples.)*

---

### **PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice Pattern)**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Explaining the Translation Process with Phases and Auxiliary Modules `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the different phases of a compiler, with a neat block diagram showing the symbol table and error handler.

**Model Solution:**

The compilation process is logically divided into **six phases**, with two auxiliary modules operating across all phases.

**1. Lexical Analysis:**
Reads the source program character by character, groups characters into **lexemes**, and produces **tokens** of the form `<token-name, attribute-value>`. Uses regular expressions and finite automata. Example: `sum := a + b` produces `<id, sum>`, `<assign_op, :=>`, `<id, a>`, `<plus_op, +>`, `<id, b>`.

**2. Syntax Analysis:**
Takes the token stream and constructs a **parse tree** (or **abstract syntax tree**) that reflects the grammatical structure. Uses context-free grammars and parsing techniques (LL, LR, SLR). Detects syntax errors like missing semicolons or mismatched parentheses.

**3. Semantic Analysis:**
Performs type checking, ensures operands are compatible, verifies variable declarations match usages, and resolves scope. For example, it catches errors like `"hello" + 5` where string and integer cannot be added. Produces an **annotated syntax tree**.

**4. Intermediate Code Generation:**
Translates the annotated tree into a machine-independent representation. The most common form is **Three-Address Code (TAC)** with at most one operator per instruction and at most three operands. For example: `t1 := a + b; t2 := t1 * c`.

**5. Code Optimization:**
Applies transformations to the IR to improve runtime, memory usage, or both. Techniques include **constant folding**, **dead code elimination**, **loop unrolling**, **strength reduction**, and **common subexpression elimination**. The output is semantically equivalent to the input IR.

**6. Code Generation:**
Translates the optimized IR into target **machine code** or **assembly**. Performs instruction selection, register allocation, and instruction scheduling. The output is relocatable object code.

**Auxiliary Modules:**
- **Symbol Table:** Stores identifiers with their attributes (name, type, scope, memory location, line number). Updated and queried by all phases.
- **Error Handler:** Detects, reports, and may attempt to recover from lexical, syntactic, and semantic errors.

**Block Diagram:**

```
+----------+     +-----------+     +-----------+     +-----------+     +-----------+     +----------+     +-----------+
| Source   | --> |  Lexical  | --> |  Syntax   | --> | Semantic  | --> | IR Code   | --> | Code Opt | --> | Code Gen |
| Program  |     |  Analyzer |     |  Analyzer |     |  Analyzer |     | Generator |     | Optimizer|     |  Module  |
+----------+     +-----------+     +-----------+     +-----------+     +-----------+     +----------+     +-----------+
                      |               |                |                |                |              |
                      v               v                v                v                v              v
                  +---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
                  |                          SYMBOL TABLE MANAGER                              |
                  +---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
                                              ^
                                              |
                  +---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
                  |                            ERROR HANDLER                                   |
                  +---------------------------------------------------------------------------+
```

*(7 marks: 1 mark for intro, 5 marks for phase explanations, 1 mark for diagram.)*

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare the working of a compiler, an interpreter, an assembler, and a preprocessor. Mention the input and output of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Tool | Input | Output | Function |
|------|-------|--------|----------|
| **Preprocessor** | Source file with directives (`#include`, `#define`) | Pure source file (macro-expanded) | Handles macros, file inclusion, conditional compilation. Runs BEFORE the compiler. |
| **Compiler** | Preprocessed high-level source code | Assembly code (or IR) | Performs the six-phase translation from HLL to assembly. |
| **Assembler** | Assembly code | Relocatable machine code (object file) | Translates mnemonics (MOV, ADD) into binary opcodes and resolves local labels. |
| **Interpreter** | High-level source code (line by line) | Direct execution — no separate file | Translates and executes each statement immediately; no standalone executable produced. |

**Key Conceptual Differences:**

- The **preprocessor** works at the *source level* using textual substitution — it does NOT understand the language syntax.
- The **compiler** performs *semantic translation* and produces an intermediate or target file.
- The **assembler** is a special-purpose compiler where the source and target languages are at the same abstraction level (low level).
- The **interpreter** does NOT produce a separate output file — translation and execution are fused.

**Conclusion:** All four are translators, but they operate at different abstraction levels. The preprocessor and assembler handle textual/binary-level transformations, while the compiler and interpreter handle semantic translation. The interpreter is unique in that it merges translation with execution.

*(7 marks: 4 marks for table, 3 marks for conceptual discussion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Compiler Architecture and Translation Trace `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Draw the architecture of a compiler showing the front-end and back-end phases. Explain the difference between front-end and back-end with examples.

**Model Solution:**

**Compiler Architecture:**

The compiler is logically divided into two major partitions: the **front end** (analysis phases) and the **back end** (synthesis phases). The middle stage holds the **Intermediate Representation (IR)**.

```
+-----------------------------------+    +--------------+    +-----------------------------------+
|           FRONT END               |    |              |    |           BACK END                |
|  (Source Language Dependent)      | -->|  INTERMEDIATE|--> |  (Target Machine Dependent)       |
|                                   |    |REPRESENTATION|    |                                   |
|  - Lexical Analyzer               |    |              |    |  - Code Optimizer                 |
|  - Syntax Analyzer                |    |   (TAC,      |    |  - Code Generator                 |
|  - Semantic Analyzer              |    |    Quadruples|    |  - Target Machine Code            |
|  - IR Generator                   |    |    SSA, etc.)|    |                                   |
+-----------------------------------+    +--------------+    +-----------------------------------+
              |                                                       ^
              v                                                       |
        +-----------------------------------+                         |
        |       SYMBOL TABLE               |-------------------------+
        |       ERROR HANDLER              |
        +-----------------------------------+
```

**Difference Between Front End and Back End:**

| Aspect | Front End | Back End |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| **Dependency** | Depends on the **source language** only | Depends on the **target machine** only |
| **Phases** | Lexical, syntax, semantic analysis, IR generation | Optimization, code generation |
| **Input** | Source program | Intermediate Representation |
| **Output** | Intermediate Representation | Target machine code |
| **Reusability** | Can be reused across different targets | Can be reused across different source languages (if IR is shared) |
| **Examples** | GCC's `cc1` (parser for C), Clang's Sema module | GCC's machine descriptions, LLVM's x86 backend |

**Significance:**
This **decoupled design** enables the construction of **multi-language, multi-target compilers**. For example, GCC uses the same C/C++/Fortran front ends with different back ends (x86, ARM, RISC-V) by sharing a common IR (GIMPLE). Similarly, the same back end can serve multiple front ends through a common IR — this is the principle behind **LLVM**.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for diagram, 4 marks for explanation.)*

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Consider the source statement: `total = base + bonus * 10;`. Show the output of the lexical analyzer, the corresponding parse tree, and the three-address code (TAC) generated.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Lexical Analyzer Output:**

The lexer scans the input and produces the following token stream:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\langle \text{id}, \text{total} \rangle \\
&\langle \text{assign\_op}, = \rangle \\
&\langle \text{id}, \text{base} \rangle \\
&\langle \text{plus\_op}, + \rangle \\
&\langle \text{id}, \text{bonus} \rangle \\
&\langle \text{mult\_op}, * \rangle \\
&\langle \text{num}, 10 \rangle \\
&\langle \text{semicolon}, ; \rangle
\end{aligned}
$$

*(1 mark for token stream.)*

**Step 2 — Parse Tree (Simplified Abstract Syntax Tree):**

The parser applies precedence rules (multiplication binds tighter than addition):

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Assignment} \\
&\quad /\quad\backslash \\
&\text{id(total)} \quad \text{Expression} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad /\quad\backslash \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad \text{Expression} \quad \text{Term} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\mid \quad\quad\quad\quad /\quad\backslash \\
&\quad\quad\quad \text{Term} \quad \text{plus} \quad \text{Term} \quad \text{Factor} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\quad\mid \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\mid \quad\quad\quad\quad\mid \\
&\quad\quad \text{Factor} \quad \text{id(base)} \quad \text{Factor} \quad \text{num(10)} \\
&\quad\quad\quad\mid \\
&\quad\quad\text{id(bonus)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This AST correctly captures that `bonus * 10` is evaluated **before** the addition.

*(2 marks for tree.)*

**Step 3 — Three-Address Code (TAC):**

The IR generator linearizes the AST. Multiplication is performed first (using operator precedence), then addition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= \text{bonus} * 10 \\
t_2 &:= \text{base} + t_1 \\
\text{total} &:= t_2
\end{aligned}
$$

Each TAC instruction has **at most three operands** and **at most one operator** on the right-hand side — a key property of the IR.

*(2 marks for TAC.)*

**Step 4 — After Optimization (Bonus, Optional):**

If `10` is an integer constant and `bonus` is a real variable, the optimizer may insert an implicit type conversion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= \text{inttoreal}(10) \\
t_2 &:= \text{bonus} * t_1 \\
t_3 &:= \text{base} + t_2 \\
\text{total} &:= t_3
\end{aligned}
$$

*(2 marks for any optimization discussion.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Symbol Table and Error Handler:** When drawing the compiler block diagram, ALWAYS include the two auxiliary modules connected to all phases. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if omitted.
> 2. **Confusing Lexeme and Token:** A *token* is the category (e.g., `id`, `num`); a *lexeme* is the actual character sequence (e.g., `position`, `60`). Mixing these up costs marks.
> 3. **Ignoring Semantic Analysis:** Many students answer only up to syntax analysis. KTU Module 1 questions specifically test whether you include semantic analysis as a SEPARATE phase.
> 4. **Mixing up Passes and Phases:** A *pass* = a complete read of the input; a *phase* = a logical transformation. A single pass may contain multiple phases.
> 5. **Confusing Compiler Output:** The compiler's direct output is **assembly** (or IR). The **assembler** converts that into machine code. Many students incorrectly say the compiler produces an executable.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Compilers: The Translation Process**

- A **compiler** translates a high-level language to a low-level language while preserving semantics.
- The **translation process** is a 6-phase pipeline: **Lex → Parse → Semantic → IR → Optimize → Code Gen**.
- **Lexical Analysis** produces tokens using regular expressions and finite automata.
- **Syntax Analysis** produces a parse tree using context-free grammars (LL, LR parsers).
- **Semantic Analysis** checks types, scope, and declaration consistency.
- **IR (Intermediate Representation)** is machine-independent; common forms: TAC, SSA, Quadruples.
- **Optimization** improves performance without changing semantics.
- **Code Generation** produces target assembly or machine code.
- The **Symbol Table** stores identifier information and is used by ALL phases.
- The **Error Handler** detects and reports errors across all phases.
- **Preprocessor → Compiler → Assembler → Linker/Loader** is the complete chain.
- **Compiler** = whole-program translation; **Interpreter** = line-by-line translation.
- **Passes** = number of complete input traversals; **Phases** = logical transformations.
- **Bootstrapping** = self-hosting compiler construction.
- **Front end** = source-dependent; **Back end** = target-dependent; **IR** is the bridge.
- Modern compilers (GCC, LLVM) reuse front ends and back ends via shared IRs for multi-language, multi-target support.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Major Data Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Major Data Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Major Data Structures in Compiler Design — Core Definition & Intuition

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **compiler** is a software system that translates source code from one programming language (source language) to another (target language, typically machine code or an intermediate representation). To perform this translation systematically, the compiler passes the source program through several well-defined **phases**: *Lexical Analysis, Syntax Analysis, Semantic Analysis, Intermediate Code Generation, Optimization,* and *Code Generation*. Each phase consumes input from the previous phase, processes it, and produces output that serves as the input to the next phase.

For these phases to communicate, store, retrieve, and manipulate information efficiently, the compiler relies on a carefully chosen set of **major data structures**. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PECMT527 – Introduction to Compiler Design*, the term **"Major Data Structures"** specifically refers to the canonical set of storage and organizational structures that are created, maintained, and queried across the various phases of compilation. These include:

- **Tokens** (the atomic output of the lexical analyzer)
- **Symbol Table** (a central repository of identifiers and their attributes)
- **Parse Tree** (the concrete syntactic representation)
- **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** (the simplified semantic representation)
- **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** (for optimization)
- **Three-Address Code (TAC)** (the canonical intermediate representation)
- Auxiliary structures: **Hash Tables, Linked Lists, Stacks, and Graphs (Control Flow Graph)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1, the focus is on *recognizing* the data structures, *understanding* their role, and *witnessing* how they are constructed. The internal algorithms (e.g., Aho-Corasick string matching, SLR parsing tables) belong to later modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Compiler as a "Multilingual Post Office"

Imagine a large international post office that receives handwritten letters in **English** (the *source language*) and must deliver typewritten telegrams in **French** (the *target language*). The post office cannot simply throw the letter at a translator — it must go through **processing stations**:

1. **Sorting Room (Lexical Analysis):** The letter is broken into individual *words* and *punctuation marks*. Each token is stamped with a category. These stamped tokens are stored in **envelopes (Tokens)** with a *lexeme* and a *token name*.
2. **Grammar Inspector (Syntax Analysis):** An inspector reads the tokens and arranges them into a **tree-shaped diagram (Parse Tree)** to confirm the sentence obeys the rules of English grammar.
3. **Meaning Checker (Semantic Analysis):** Another inspector walks through the tree, looking up each proper noun in a **giant ledger (Symbol Table)** to verify meaning and type consistency.
4. **Summarizer (Intermediate Code Generation):** The verified tree is flattened into a **list of simple three-step instructions (Three-Address Code)**.
5. **Editor (Optimization):** The instructions are reorganized on a **flowchart (DAG/Control Flow Graph)** to find redundancies.
6. **Translator (Code Generation):** Each simple instruction is finally rewritten into **French telegrams (Target Code)**.

In this analogy, every "form" used inside the post office — envelopes, trees, ledgers, flowcharts — is a **data structure** used by the compiler. None of them are part of the *program being compiled*; they are part of the *compilation machinery itself*.

## 1.3 Summary of the Major Data Structures

| # | Data Structure | Primary Phase | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Token** | Lexical Analysis | Atomic symbol: *token-name* + *attribute-value* |
| 2 | **Symbol Table** | All phases (especially Semantic) | Stores identifiers, their attributes (type, scope, memory location) |
| 3 | **Parse Tree** | Syntax Analysis | Concrete hierarchical representation of source program |
| 4 | **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** | Syntax/Semantic Analysis | Compressed parse tree showing only essential syntactic constructs |
| 5 | **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** | Optimization | Identifies common sub-expressions for redundancy removal |
| 6 | **Three-Address Code (TAC)** | Intermediate Code Generation | Linear IR with at most three operands per instruction |
| 7 | **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** | Optimization | Graph of basic blocks connected by control flow edges |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU 2024 ESE answers, always state the **phase** in which a data structure is created AND the **phase(s)** in which it is consumed. Showing this bidirectional flow earns full marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Token — The Lexical Atom

A **token** is a tuple consisting of a *token-name* and an optional *attribute-value*.

**Token Tuple:**

$$\text{Token} = \langle \text{token-name}, \text{attribute-value} \rangle$$

- **token-name** is an abstract symbol representing the kind of lexical unit (e.g., `identifier`, `number`, `+`, `if`).
- **attribute-value** points to the entry in the symbol table for the corresponding lexeme.

**Example:** For the source fragment `count = count + 5;`, the lexer produces the stream:

| Token-Name | Attribute-Value | Lexeme |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | pointer to symbol-table entry for `count` | count |
| `=` | — | = |
| `id` | pointer to symbol-table entry for `count` | count |
| `+` | — | + |
| `num` | pointer to literal-table entry for `5` | 5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Patterns (regular expressions) and lexemes are *not* the same as tokens. A **pattern** is the *rule*; a **lexeme** is the *matched text*; a **token** is the *classified category*.

## 2.2 Symbol Table — The Central Ledger

The **symbol table** is a data structure that maps each *identifier* (variable, function, class) in the source program to a collection of *attributes* (name, type, scope, memory address, line of declaration, etc.).

### 2.2.1 Standard Symbol-Table Entry Schema

| Field | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `lexeme` | The textual name | `counter` |
| `token-type` | Category of identifier | `variable` |
| `data-type` | Type information | `int` |
| `scope` | Region of visibility | `{block_2}` |
| `memory-location` | Compile-time address | `0x2004` |
| `line-number` | First declaration line | `12` |
| `size` | Storage size in bytes | `4` |
| `parameter-list` | For functions | `(int, float)` |
| `return-type` | For functions | `void` |

### 2.2.2 Implementation Strategies

Three standard implementations are recognized by KTU:

1. **Unordered (Linear) List**
   - Insertion: $O(1)$ — append at end.
   - Lookup: $O(n)$ — sequential scan.
   - **Use:** tiny compilers, toy examples.

2. **Self-Organizing List (Move-to-Front)**
   - Frequently accessed entries migrate to the head.
   - Lookup: $O(n)$ worst case, but near $O(1)$ for hot symbols.

3. **Hash Table (Standard)**
   - Uses a hash function $h(\text{lexeme}) \rightarrow \text{index}$.
   - Average lookup: $O(1)$.
   - Worst case: $O(n)$ (due to collisions).
   - **Use:** production compilers such as GCC, Clang.

**Hash Function Examples:**

$$\text{Mid-Square: } h(k) = \left\lfloor \frac{k^2}{10^{m-1}} \right\rfloor \bmod \; n$$

$$\text{Division: } h(k) = k \bmod \; n$$

$$\text{Folding: } h(k) = \text{(fold groups of digits and sum)} \bmod \; n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Collision Resolution** is critical. **Chaining** (linked lists from each bucket) is preferred over **Open Addressing** when the load factor $\alpha = n/m$ approaches **1**, where $n$ is the number of keys and $m$ is the number of buckets.

## 2.3 Parse Tree — The Concrete Syntax Tree

A **parse tree** (also called a *concrete syntax tree* or *derivation tree*) is a rooted, ordered tree that depicts the complete syntactic derivation of a source program from the start symbol of a context-free grammar.

### 2.3.1 Construction Rule (Recursive)

For a production of the form $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_k$:

- Create a node labeled $A$ (the parent).
- Create $k$ child nodes labeled $X_1, X_2, \dots, X_k$ from left to right.

### 2.3.2 Worked Grammar Example

Consider the arithmetic grammar:

$$E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$$
$$T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$$
$$F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$$

For the input string `id + id * id`, the parse tree has the start symbol `E` at the root and terminals at the leaves. The **in-order traversal** of this tree reproduces the source string.

## 2.4 Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) — The Pruned Tree

An **AST** is a parse tree with redundant punctuation and chaining nodes removed. It captures only the *essential* structural and semantic content.

### 2.4.1 Key Differences (AST vs. Parse Tree)

| Property | Parse Tree | AST |
|---|---|---|
| Node count | High (includes all grammar symbols) | Low (only essential operators/operands) |
| Chain nodes | Preserved | Collapsed |
| Parentheses | Appear as nodes | Implicit in tree structure |
| Used for | Grammar validation | Semantic analysis, code generation |
| Size | $O(\text{input length})$ scaled by grammar | $O(\text{input length})$ linear |

### 2.4.2 AST Node Types for Expressions

- **Operator nodes:** binary ($+, -, *, /$), unary ($-$, `NOT`).
- **Operand nodes:** identifier, constant literal.
- **Statement nodes:** assignment, conditional, loop, return, compound.

For the expression `id₁ + id₂ * id₃`, the AST is:

```
        (+)
       /   \
     id₁    (*)
           /   \
         id₂   id₃
```

The parse tree, by contrast, would also contain nodes for `E → T`, `T → F`, `F → (E)` derivations that simply chain without operation.

## 2.5 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)

A **DAG** is a directed graph with no cycles, where a node may have multiple parents. It is constructed from an AST by **merging common sub-expressions** that compute the same value.

### 2.5.1 Construction Algorithm (Value-Number Method)

1. Maintain a hash map from operator + operand descriptors to existing nodes.
2. For each sub-expression, search the map:
   - If found, reuse the existing node (avoiding duplication).
   - If not, create a new node and insert it.

### 2.5.2 Why a DAG?

The DAG enables *common sub-expression elimination*, a fundamental local optimization:

$$\text{For statement } a[i] = a[i] + 1,$$

the address of `a[i]` is computed twice in the AST but only once in the DAG, halving the address-calculation cost.

## 2.6 Three-Address Code (TAC)

**TAC** is an intermediate representation in which each instruction has at most **three operands** and typically follows one of four forms:

| Form | Template | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `x = y op z` | Binary operation |
| 2 | `x = op y` | Unary operation |
| 3 | `x = y` | Copy / move |
| 4 | `goto L` / `if x goto L` / `if x relop y goto L` | Control flow |

### 2.6.1 Implementation of TAC

The TAC program is usually stored as a list of quadruples `(op, arg1, arg2, result)` or triples `(op, arg1, arg2)`, or as **Static Single Assignment (SSA)** form.

## 2.7 Control Flow Graph (CFG)

A **CFG** is a directed graph $G = (N, E)$ where:
- $N$ = set of *basic blocks* (maximal straight-line instruction sequences).
- $E$ = set of edges $(B_i, B_j)$ indicating possible control transfer.

The CFG is the foundation for almost all *global* optimizations: dead-code elimination, constant propagation, loop-invariant code motion.

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Token definition | $\text{Token} = \langle \text{name}, \text{attribute} \rangle$ |
| 2 | Hash table — average lookup | $O(1)$ |
| 3 | Hash table — worst lookup | $O(n)$ |
| 4 | Load factor | $\alpha = n / m$ |
| 5 | Linear list — lookup | $O(n)$ |
| 6 | Parse tree — in-order traversal | Recovers the source string |
| 7 | AST — size | $O(\vert \text{input} \vert)$ |
| 8 | TAC — operands per instruction | $\le 3$ |
| 9 | DAG — node count vs AST | $\le$ AST count |
| 10 | CFG — node type | Basic Block |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Application:** Production compilers (GCC, LLVM, Clang, MSVC) use *all* of these structures. LLVM's IR is essentially TAC extended with SSA; GCC's *GIMPLE* is a tree-based IR. Recognizing these structures is the foundation of *compiler engineering*, a multi-billion-dollar industry segment powering every device running compiled code.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Constructions & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference

| Data Structure | Construction Input | Output / Stored Form | Key Operation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Token | Source character stream | `(name, attribute)` tuple | Lexical matching via DFA |
| Symbol Table | Identifier lexeme + type/scope info | Hash-keyed record | `insert`, `lookup`, `modify` |
| Parse Tree | Token stream | Rooted ordered tree | `derivation` using grammar |
| AST | Parse tree or token stream | Pruned tree | `fold`, `visit` |
| DAG | AST (or TAC) | DAG with merged common sub-expressions | `hash` operator+operands |
| TAC | AST / DAG | List of 3-operand instructions | `emit(instruction)` |
| CFG | TAC with leader identification | Graph of basic blocks | `partition` into leaders |

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Building a Symbol Table

Consider the C-like program:

```
int x;
float y;
{
    int x;
    x = 1;
    y = 2.5;
}
x = 10;
```

**Step 1:** Insert global `x` of type `int`, scope `global`.
**Step 2:** Insert global `y` of type `float`, scope `global`.
**Step 3:** On entering the inner block, push a new scope.
**Step 4:** Insert inner `x` of type `int`, scope `block_1`. The symbol table now has **two** entries for `x`, with the inner one shadowing the outer.
**Step 5:** Assignment `x = 1` updates inner `x`.
**Step 6:** Assignment `y = 2.5` updates global `y` (no inner `y` exists).
**Step 7:** On leaving the inner block, pop the scope; inner `x` is removed.
**Step 8:** Assignment `x = 10` now updates the global `x`.

**Resulting Hash-Bucketed Symbol Table (conceptual):**

| Bucket | Lexeme | Type | Scope | Address | Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `h("x")` | x | int | global | 0x1000 | 4 |
| `h("x")` (shadow) | x | int | block_1 | 0x2000 | 4 |
| `h("y")` | y | float | global | 0x1004 | 4 |

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Constructing a Parse Tree

**Grammar:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\
T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\
F &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Input:** `id + id * id`

**Step-by-step leftmost derivation:**

1. $E \Rightarrow E + T$ (apply $E \rightarrow E + T$)
2. $\Rightarrow T + T$ (apply $E \rightarrow T$)
3. $\Rightarrow F + T$ (apply $T \rightarrow F$)
4. $\Rightarrow \text{id} + T$ (apply $F \rightarrow \text{id}$)
5. $\Rightarrow \text{id} + T * F$ (apply $T \rightarrow T * F$)
6. $\Rightarrow \text{id} + F * F$ (apply $T \rightarrow F$)
7. $\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F$ (apply $F \rightarrow \text{id}$)
8. $\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ (apply $F \rightarrow \text{id}$)

**Equivalent parse tree (textual rendering):**

```
                E
              / | \
             E  +  T
             |    /|\
             T   T *  F
             |   |   |
             F   F   id
             |   |
             id  id
```

> [!NOTE]
> The parse tree contains **intermediate non-terminal nodes** (`E`, `T`, `F`) that serve only to chain derivations. The AST removes them.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — AST vs DAG Construction

**Input Expression:** `a + a * (b - c) + (b - c) * d`

### 3.4.1 AST (with redundant sub-trees)

```
            (+)
           /   \
         (+)    (*)
        /   \   /  \
       a    (*) (b-c)
            / \
           a   (-)
              / \
             b   c
```

Notice: the sub-expression `(b - c)` appears **twice** as two separate sub-trees.

### 3.4.2 DAG (with common sub-expression merged)

```
            n1(+)        <-- root
           /   \
         n2(+)  n3(*)
        /   \   /   \
       a    n3*  d
            / \
        n4(-)  (the merged (b-c) node)
        / \
       b   c
```

**The DAG replaces 7 AST nodes with 5 DAG nodes** by reusing the `b - c` computation.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — Three-Address Code Generation

**Source:** `a + b * c`

**Step 1 — Parse Tree / AST:**

```
        (+)
       /   \
      a    (*)
          /   \
         b     c
```

**Step 2 — TAC Generation (post-order traversal):**

The compiler emits TAC in *post-order* to ensure operands are computed before the operator that consumes them.

| Line | TAC Instruction | Comment |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `t1 = b * c` | compute right operand first |
| 2 | `t2 = a + t1` | then the addition |
| 3 | `result = t2` | assign final result |

**Generalized rule:**

$$\text{For node } n \text{ with children } n_1, n_2 \text{ labeled with operator } \text{op},$$

$$\text{emit: } t = n_1.\text{place} \; \text{op} \; n_2.\text{place}$$

$$\text{then } n.\text{place} = t$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake is to perform a *pre-order* traversal of the AST when generating TAC, which attempts to use operands before they are computed. Always use **post-order**.

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Building a Control Flow Graph

**TAC program:**

| Line | Instruction |
|---|---|
| 1 | `t1 = a + b` |
| 2 | `t2 = t1 * 2` |
| 3 | `if t2 > 100 goto L1` |
| 4 | `t3 = t2 - 50` |
| 5 | `goto L2` |
| 6 | `L1: t4 = t2 + 10` |
| 7 | `L2: print t4` |

**Step 1 — Identify leaders:**
- Line 1 (first instruction)
- Line 3 (target of conditional jump — actually the *target* of a branch is a leader, but the branch itself is also a leader)
- Line 4 (instruction immediately after a conditional jump)
- Line 6 (label `L1`)
- Line 7 (label `L2`)

**Step 2 — Partition into basic blocks:**

- **B1:** Lines 1–3
- **B2:** Lines 4–5
- **B3:** Line 6
- **B4:** Line 7

**Step 3 — Draw CFG edges:**

- B1 → B2 (fall-through after `if false`)
- B1 → B3 (jump to L1 on `if true`)
- B2 → B4 (jump to L2)
- B3 → B4 (fall-through)

**Resulting CFG:**

```
        B1
       /  \
      v    v
     B2    B3
      \    /
       v  v
        B4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** CFGs power every global optimization in production compilers. For instance, *dataflow analysis* on this CFG can prove that `t4` is unconditionally defined along the path B1→B3→B4, allowing the optimizer to remove `t3` from B2 if it is provably dead.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Data Structures Across Compiler Phases

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the relationship between the **major data structures** and the **compiler phases** that produce and consume them.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Source[Source Code Character Stream]
    Lexer[Lexical Analyzer]
    Tokens[Token Stream - name attribute tuples]
    SymTab[Symbol Table - Hash Table]
    Parser[Syntax Analyzer]
    ParseTree[Parse Tree - Concrete Syntax Tree]
    AST[Abstract Syntax Tree]
    SemAn[Semantic Analyzer]
    IRGen[Intermediate Code Generator]
    TAC[Three Address Code]
    Opt[Optimizer]
    DAG[Directed Acyclic Graph]
    CFG[Control Flow Graph]
    CodeGen[Code Generator]
    Target[Target Code]

    Source --> Lexer
    Lexer --> Tokens
    Lexer -.-> SymTab
    Tokens --> Parser
    Parser --> ParseTree
    ParseTree --> AST
    AST --> SemAn
    SemAn -.-> SymTab
    SemAn --> IRGen
    IRGen --> TAC
    TAC --> Opt
    Opt --> DAG
    Opt --> CFG
    DAG --> CodeGen
    CFG --> CodeGen
    CodeGen --> Target
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Solid arrows represent *data flow* (a structure feeding the next phase). Dotted arrows represent *query/update* relationships with the symbol table. The symbol table is **shared** by the lexer, parser, and semantic analyzer — it is the only data structure that survives the entire compilation.

## 4.2 Token-Stream Construction Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Src["count = count + 5;"]
    Lx[Lexical Analyzer]
    T1["id, attr→count"]
    T2["=", attr→null"]
    T3["id, attr→count"]
    T4["+, attr→null"]
    T5["num, attr→5"]
    T6[";, attr→null"]
    St[Symbol Table]

    Src --> Lx
    Lx --> T1
    Lx --> T2
    Lx --> T3
    Lx --> T4
    Lx --> T5
    Lx --> T6
    Lx -.-> St
    T1 -.-> St
    T3 -.-> St
    T5 -.-> St
```

## 4.3 Symbol-Table Hash Bucket Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H["h(lexeme) → bucket index"]
    B0["Bucket 0"]
    B1["Bucket 1"]
    B2["Bucket 2"]
    B3["Bucket 3"]
    N1["x → int, global"]
    N2["y → float, global"]
    N3["counter → int, block_1"]
    N4["temp1 → int, expr"]

    H --> B0
    H --> B1
    H --> B2
    H --> B3
    B1 --- N1
    B1 --- N3
    B2 --- N2
    B3 --- N4
```

## 4.4 AST Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Parse Tree with chain nodes]
    A2[Remove chain nodes E to T, T to F]
    A3[Collapse single-child derivations]
    A4[AST with operator and operand nodes only]

    A1 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> A4
```

## 4.5 TAC Emission Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Post-order Traversal of AST]
    P2[Emit binary TAC x = y op z]
    P3[Emit unary TAC x = op y]
    P4[Emit copy TAC x = y]
    P5[Final TAC Instruction List]

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P1 --> P4
    P2 --> P5
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visualization Note:** If the Mermaid diagrams above do not render in your PDF viewer, refer to the textual descriptions in Section 3. Each diagram corresponds to a specific construction rule that is examinable in KTU 2024 ESE.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a "Token" in the context of lexical analysis. Illustrate with a suitable example. [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

A token is the atomic output produced by the lexical analyzer (lexer). Formally, a token is a tuple consisting of a *token-name* (an abstract symbol indicating the category of the lexical unit) and an optional *attribute-value* (which points to the symbol-table entry for the corresponding lexeme).

$$\text{Token} = \langle \text{token-name}, \text{attribute-value} \rangle$$

**Example:** For the C statement `total = price * quantity;`, the lexer produces the token stream:

| Token-Name | Attribute-Value | Lexeme |
|---|---|---|
| `id` | sym_tab_ptr(`total`) | total |
| `=` | — | = |
| `id` | sym_tab_ptr(`price`) | price |
| `*` | — | * |
| `id` | sym_tab_ptr(`quantity`) | quantity |
| `;` | — | ; |

The token-name categorizes the lexeme, while the attribute-value provides the semantic linkage to the symbol table.

---

### Q2. Differentiate between a Parse Tree and an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST). [3 Marks]
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model Question]**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Parse Tree | Abstract Syntax Tree |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Rooted, ordered tree showing the complete grammatical derivation from the start symbol. | Pruned parse tree containing only essential operators and operands. |
| **Nodes Included** | All grammar symbols (terminals + non-terminals). | Only operator and operand nodes. |
| **Chain Nodes** | Preserved (e.g., `E → T → F → id`). | Collapsed. |
| **Size** | Larger, often $2\times$ to $3\times$ the AST. | Compact, linear in input length. |
| **Purpose** | Validate grammatical correctness; used by parsers. | Drive semantic analysis, IR generation, optimization. |
| **Example** | For `a + b`, includes nodes for `E`, `T`, `F`, `+`, `a`, `b`. | Root `+` with children `a` and `b`. |

The AST is *derived from* the parse tree by removing chain nodes that have a single child and grouping the same operator's operands.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Verified):** Each Part-B question offers internal choice. You must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Each option has two sub-parts, typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [Choose this OR Question B]

**(a) Explain the structure and operations of a Symbol Table used in a compiler. Discuss the implementation using Hash Tables with collision resolution by chaining. [7 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Symbol Table** is a central data structure used by the compiler to store information about identifiers (variable names, function names, class names, etc.) appearing in the source program. It supports three principal operations:

1. **insert(name, attributes)** — adds a new identifier.
2. **lookup(name)** — retrieves the attributes of an identifier.
3. **modify(name, attributes)** — updates the attributes of an existing identifier.

**Structure of a Symbol-Table Entry:**

| Field | Description |
|---|---|
| `lexeme` | The identifier text (e.g., `counter`). |
| `type` | Data type (e.g., `int`, `float`, `function`). |
| `scope` | Region of visibility (e.g., `global`, `block_3`). |
| `memory-location` | Compile-time address assigned to the identifier. |
| `size` | Storage requirement in bytes. |
| `line-number` | Line at which the identifier is first declared. |
| `parameter-list` | For functions, the list of formal parameters and their types. |
| `return-type` | For functions, the return type. |

**Implementation Using Hash Tables with Chaining:**

A hash table is an array of $m$ *buckets* indexed by a hash function $h(\text{lexeme})$. The most common hash function is the **division method**:

$$h(k) = k \bmod \; m$$

where $k$ is a numeric code (e.g., sum of character codes) of the lexeme.

Because two different lexemes can hash to the same bucket (a **collision**), we resolve collisions by **chaining**: each bucket stores a pointer to a linked list of all entries that hashed to that bucket.

**Operations in a Hashed Symbol Table:**

- **insert:** Compute $h(\text{lexeme})$, walk the chain at that bucket, append a new node if the lexeme is not already present. Cost: $O(1)$ average, $O(n)$ worst case.
- **lookup:** Compute $h$, walk the chain comparing lexemes. Cost: $O(1)$ average with a good hash function and low load factor $\alpha = n/m \le 1$.
- **modify:** Lookup, then update fields in-place. Cost: same as lookup.

**Load Factor Analysis:**

The expected chain length is $\alpha = n/m$. For $m \approx 1.27 \cdot n$, the average chain length is about **1.27**, giving near-constant-time operations.

**Closing Remark:** Production compilers (e.g., GCC) use sophisticated hash functions like **FNV-1a** or **MurmurHash3** and may combine hashing with **balanced binary search trees** (e.g., red-black trees) inside buckets to guarantee $O(\log n)$ worst-case lookup.

---

**(b) Construct the Abstract Syntax Tree (AST) and the equivalent Three-Address Code (TAC) for the expression: `a * (b + c) - d / e`. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Construct the AST:**

The AST has the *highest-precedence* operator at the deepest level. Following standard precedence (`/` and `*` over `+`, left-to-right associativity):

```
        (-)
       /   \
     (*)   (/)
    /  \   / \
   a   (+)  d   e
       / \
      b   c
```

**Step 2 — Generate Three-Address Code using Post-Order Traversal:**

Post-order ensures operands are computed before the operator that uses them.

| # | TAC Instruction | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `t1 = b + c` | Innermost sub-expression `b + c` |
| 2 | `t2 = a * t1` | Multiply `a` with `(b + c)` |
| 3 | `t3 = d / e` | Compute `d / e` |
| 4 | `t4 = t2 - t3` | Subtract `d / e` from `a * (b + c)` |

**Final TAC:**

```
t1 = b + c
t2 = a * t1
t3 = d / e
t4 = t2 - t3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The variables `t1, t2, t3, t4` are **compiler-generated temporaries**. Each TAC instruction has at most three operands — *two sources* and *one destination* — satisfying the canonical 3-address form `x = y op z`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) from an expression. How does it help in compiler optimization? [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** is a graphical data structure used as an intermediate representation in compilers. Unlike a tree, a node in a DAG may have **multiple parents**, allowing the same sub-expression to be **shared** rather than duplicated.

**Construction Algorithm (Value-Number Method):**

The compiler maintains a hash table of *value numbers*, where each value number represents a unique sub-expression.

1. Visit the AST in post-order.
2. For each sub-expression `(op, left, right)`:
   - Look up the existing node in the hash table.
   - If a node with the same operator and operand value-numbers already exists, **reuse it**.
   - Otherwise, create a new node and assign it a new value number.
3. The result is a DAG where duplicated sub-expressions are merged.

**Worked Example:**

Source expression:

$$a + a * (b - c) + (b - c) * d$$

**AST (with duplicate `(b - c)` sub-trees):**

```
                  (+)
                /      \
              (+)      (*)
             /   \    /    \
            a   (*)  (b-c)  d
                / \
               a  (-)
                 / \
                b   c
```

**DAG (with `(b - c)` merged):**

```
               n1  (+)         [root]
              /   \
           n2(+)  n3(*)
          /   \   / \
         a   n3*  d
             / \
        n4(-)
         / \
        b   c
```

Here, the computation `b - c` is represented by **a single node** `n4` even though it is used twice in the source.

**Role in Compiler Optimization — Common Sub-Expression Elimination (CSE):**

The DAG enables **local common sub-expression elimination** automatically. Because the merged sub-expression is computed only once at runtime, the compiler can:

- Eliminate redundant address calculations (e.g., `a[i]` used twice in `a[i] = a[i] + 1`).
- Reduce register pressure by reusing values.
- Lower the instruction count, leading to faster target code.

**Conclusion:** The DAG is the principal *local* optimization structure; for *global* optimization, the compiler uses the **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** combined with **dataflow analysis**.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of a Control Flow Graph (CFG). Given a Three-Address Code segment, construct its CFG. [7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

A **Control Flow Graph (CFG)** is a directed graph $G = (N, E)$ where:
- Each node $n \in N$ is a **basic block** — a maximal sequence of consecutive TAC instructions in which flow of control enters at the beginning and leaves at the end without any internal branches.
- Each edge $e = (n_i, n_j) \in E$ represents a possible transfer of control from block $n_i$ to block $n_j$.

**Construction Procedure:**

1. **Identify leaders** — the first instructions of basic blocks:
   - The first instruction of the program.
   - The target of any branch (conditional or unconditional).
   - The instruction immediately following any branch.
2. **Form a basic block** starting at each leader and continuing until the next leader.
3. **Draw edges** between blocks based on:
   - Sequential fall-through (if the last instruction is not an unconditional jump).
   - Explicit branch targets.
   - Jumps out of the middle of a block.

**Given TAC Program:**

| Line | Instruction |
|---|---|
| 1 | `t1 = a + b` |
| 2 | `t2 = t1 * 2` |
| 3 | `if t2 > 100 goto L1` |
| 4 | `t3 = t2 - 50` |
| 5 | `goto L2` |
| 6 | `L1: t4 = t2 + 10` |
| 7 | `L2: print t4` |

**Step 1 — Identify Leaders:**
- Line 1 (first instruction)
- Line 3 (target of conditional branch)
- Line 4 (instruction after a conditional branch)
- Line 6 (label `L1`)
- Line 7 (label `L2`)

**Step 2 — Form Basic Blocks:**
- **B1:** Lines 1–3 (`t1 = a + b; t2 = t1 * 2; if t2 > 100 goto L1`)
- **B2:** Lines 4–5 (`t3 = t2 - 50; goto L2`)
- **B3:** Line 6 (`L1: t4 = t2 + 10`)
- **B4:** Line 7 (`L2: print t4`)

**Step 3 — Draw CFG Edges:**

| Edge | Reason |
|---|---|
| B1 → B2 | Fall-through (the `if` branch was not taken) |
| B1 → B3 | Conditional jump target `L1` |
| B2 → B4 | Unconditional `goto L2` |
| B3 → B4 | Fall-through after `L1` block |

**Resulting CFG:**

```
            ┌──────┐
            │  B1  │
            └──┬─┬─┘
        false  │ │ true
               ▼ ▼
         ┌──────┐   ┌──────┐
         │  B2  │   │  B3  │
         └──┬───┘   └──┬───┘
            │          │
            ▼          ▼
            └────┬─────┘
                 ▼
              ┌──────┐
              │  B4  │
              └──────┘
```

**Engineering Significance:** CFGs are the substrate for all global optimizations: *dead-code elimination, constant propagation, loop-invariant code motion,* and *global common sub-expression elimination.* Tools like **LLVM's `opt`** consume CFGs as LLVM IR and apply dozens of such passes.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Verified from Past KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Confusing "lexeme" with "token":** Students often write the lexeme (`count`) in place of the token (`<id, ptr_to_count>`). The lexeme is the matched *text*; the token is the *classified category* with its attribute. Marks deducted for the difference.
> 2. **Forgetting the "attribute-value" half of the Token tuple:** Always present a token as a pair, not a single symbol.
> 3. **Drawing a Parse Tree when an AST is asked (and vice versa):** Read the question twice. Parse trees include all non-terminal chain nodes; ASTs do not.
> 4. **Wrong traversal order for TAC generation:** Use **post-order** on the AST. Pre-order causes use-before-compute errors.
> 5. **Missing leaders when constructing a CFG:** A common mistake is to mark only the *targets* of branches as leaders. You must also mark the **instruction immediately after** a branch as a leader (it begins a new basic block reachable by fall-through).
> 6. **Omitting scope information in the symbol table:** For nested programs, you *must* mention scope stacks or chained symbol tables. Single-scope symbol tables are incomplete.
> 7. **No hash-collision strategy:** Any hash-table design without collision resolution is incomplete. Always state the strategy (chaining / open addressing / rehashing).

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Token** = `⟨token-name, attribute-value⟩`; output of the lexer.
- **Pattern** = the *rule* (regular expression); **Lexeme** = the matched *text*; **Token** = the *classified category*.
- **Symbol Table** is the *only* data structure that spans multiple phases. Implemented as a **hash table with chaining** for $O(1)$ average lookup.
- **Parse Tree** = full derivation tree from start symbol; contains *all* non-terminals.
- **AST** = parse tree with chain nodes removed; *operator* and *operand* nodes only.
- **DAG** = AST with common sub-expressions merged; used for **common sub-expression elimination**.
- **TAC** = list of instructions of the form `x = y op z` (≤3 operands); used as the canonical **intermediate representation**.
- **CFG** = graph of basic blocks; built by identifying *leaders* and *fall-through edges*.
- **Post-order** traversal of AST is the correct method for TAC emission.
- **Load factor** $\alpha = n/m$ governs hash-table performance; keep $\alpha \le 1$ for chaining.
- **Production compiler mapping:** GCC → GIMPLE (TAC-like); LLVM → LLVM IR (SSA-form TAC); Clang → AST-based frontend.
- **KTU-Mandated Vocabulary:** When asked to "list the major data structures," always list: *Token, Symbol Table, Parse Tree, AST, DAG, TAC, CFG* — all seven.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Study Tip:** Practice drawing the *parse tree* first, then the *AST* (with chain nodes removed), then the *DAG* (with common sub-expressions merged), and finally emit the *TAC* via post-order traversal. This four-step ladder appears verbatim in KTU 2024 ESE questions and mastering it guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Other Issues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Other Issues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Other Issues" in Compiler Design?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECMT527 – Introduction to Compiler Design**, Module 1 introduces the foundational anatomy of a compiler. Beyond the six classical phases (Lexical, Syntax, Semantic, Intermediate Code Generation, Code Optimization, and Target Code Generation), there exist several **auxiliary, cross-cutting concerns** that bind the compiler together. Collectively, these are termed **"Other Issues"**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> *Other Issues* refer to the auxiliary mechanisms and supporting infrastructure required for a compiler to function reliably, namely: **Error Detection & Recovery, Symbol Table Management, Bootstrapping, Cross-Compilation, T-Diagrams, and Compiler Construction Tools (LEX & YACC)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Construction Site Metaphor

Imagine you are building a skyscraper (the **target program**). The architect draws the plan (the **source code**), and the contractor (the **compiler**) converts this plan into the actual building.

- **Phases of Compilation** = The actual construction workers (masons, electricians, plumbers).
- **Symbol Table** = The *central registry* kept at the site office where every brick, beam, and material is logged with its location, type, and supplier.
- **Error Detection & Recovery** = The *quality control inspector* who catches a wrong measurement and tells the worker how to fix it *without tearing down the entire floor*.
- **Bootstrapping** = A *small crane* that builds a *bigger crane*, which then builds the *entire building*.
- **Cross-Compilation** = A Kerala-based architect using a Japanese manual to follow Japanese construction standards to build in India.
- **T-Diagrams** = The *label* pasted on each crane showing: *what it can lift, what it speaks, and what it produces*.
- **LEX & YACC** = The *power tools* (automatic brick-layer and automatic steel-binder) that automate repetitive construction tasks.

## 1.3 Core Components of "Other Issues"

| # | Sub-Topic | One-Line Purpose |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | Error Detection & Recovery | Identify, report, and recover from faults at every phase. |
| 2 | Symbol Table | Central data structure storing identifiers and attributes. |
| 3 | Bootstrapping | Building a compiler for language L using language L itself. |
| 4 | Cross-Compilation | Compiling on Machine A to produce code for Machine B. |
| 5 | T-Diagrams | Pictorial notation for representing compilers and their ports. |
| 6 | LEX & YACC | Automatic tools to generate Lexical and Syntax Analyzers. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study "Other Issues"?** These topics frequently appear as **2-mark and 7-mark questions** in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). The PECMT527 syllabus explicitly lists them under Module 1, making them mandatory for both university exam success and practical compiler construction.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Error Detection and Recovery

### 2.1.1 Concept
A compiler must not halt at the first error. Instead, it must:
1. **Detect** the error.
2. **Report** it clearly (line number, error type).
3. **Recover** and continue to find further errors (to save programmer time).

### 2.1.2 Classification of Errors

| Error Type | Detected By | Example |
|------------|-------------|---------|
| Lexical | Lexical Analyzer | `123abc` (illegal token) |
| Syntactic | Syntax Analyzer | `int x = ;` (missing expression) |
| Semantic | Semantic Analyzer | Type mismatch: `int a; a = "hello";` |
| Logical | Not detected by compiler | Infinite loop, off-by-one |

### 2.1.3 Error Recovery Strategies

- **Panic Mode Recovery**: Discard input symbols until a synchronizing token (like `;` or `}`) is found. *Simplest, most widely used.*
- **Phrase Level Recovery**: Local correction — insert/delete a single symbol.
- **Error Productions**: Augment grammar with rules that generate erroneous constructs.
- **Global Correction**: Find the *minimum-cost* correction (theoretically best, but expensive).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU high-yield point: **Panic Mode** is the most common strategy used in practice and is the expected answer for "briefly explain error recovery" questions.

## 2.2 Symbol Table

### 2.2.1 Definition
A **Symbol Table** is a compile-time data structure that maps each *identifier* in the source program to its *attributes* (type, scope, memory address, etc.).

### 2.2.2 Symbol Table Entry — Typical Attributes

For a variable `x` declared as `int x;` inside function `foo()`:

| Attribute | Example Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Name | `x` |
| Type | `int` |
| Scope | Local to `foo()` |
| Memory Address | Stack offset, e.g., `-4(%rbp)` |
| Line Number | `Line 5` |
| Size | `4 bytes` |

### 2.2.3 Operations on Symbol Table

The symbol table supports exactly **6 core operations**:
1. `insert(name, attributes)` — Add a new entry.
2. `lookup(name)` — Find an entry.
3. `modify(name, attributes)` — Update an existing entry.
4. `delete(name)` — Remove an entry (typically at scope exit).
5. `openScope()` — Push a new scope on stack.
6. `closeScope()` — Pop a scope.

### 2.2.4 Implementation Strategies

- **Linear List** — Simple, slow lookup $O(n)$.
- **Hash Table** — Fast lookup $O(1)$ on average.
- **Binary Search Tree** — $O(\log n)$ lookup, ordered traversal.
- **Stack-based Scope Nesting** — Tracks nested blocks.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU high-yield fact: The symbol table is **shared across all phases** of the compiler and is the *primary communication channel* between the front-end phases.

## 2.3 Bootstrapping

### 2.3.1 The Problem
Suppose we want a compiler for language **L** running on machine **M**. But initially, we only have an interpreter or assembler for machine M — no compiler exists in L.

### 2.3.2 The Bootstrap Process
The technique of progressively building a compiler for a new language using previous versions is called **Bootstrapping**.

**Three Stages of Bootstrapping:**
1. Write a *small subset compiler* $S_1$ for L in language L itself, targeting machine M.
2. Use $S_1$ to compile a *larger subset compiler* $S_2$ (written in L).
3. Repeat until the **full compiler** is built.

### 2.3.3 Advantages
- The compiler being written is tested on itself (a robust self-check).
- Removes dependency on other languages.
- Allows porting compilers to new machines efficiently.

## 2.4 Cross-Compilation
