  &\Rightarrow \textbf{id} = \textbf{id} + \textbf{id} * \textbf{id} && \text{(by } F \rightarrow \textbf{id})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Parse Tree Structure:** Root $S$ has children $\textbf{id}$, `=`, $E$. The $E$ subtree contains $E$, `+`, $T$, with the right $T$ further containing $T$, `*`, $F$. All three $\textbf{id}$ leaves and the `=` leaf are present at the bottom.

**Step 3 — AST Construction:**

- Root node: `=` (assignment operator) with two children: left = `id` (`a`), right = subtree for `b + c * d`.
- Subtree for `b + c * d`: root = `+`, left leaf = `id` (`b`), right = subtree rooted at `*`.
- Subtree for `c * d`: root = `*`, left leaf = `id` (`c`), right leaf = `id` (`d`).

**AST Structure (textual):**

```
           ASSIGN (=)
          /         \
         a         PLUS (+)
                 /        \
                b        MUL (*)
                        /     \
                       c       d
```

**Step 4 — Demonstration of Precedence Encoding:**

- The `*` operator (higher precedence) appears **deeper** in the tree (two edges from the root).
- The `+` operator (lower precedence) appears **higher** in the tree (one edge from the `=` node).
- During postorder traversal for code generation, the `*` subtree is evaluated **first**, producing a temporary, and then `+` uses that temporary. This is exactly the desired evaluation order: `a = b + (c * d)`. **(7 Marks — 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for AST structure, 2 marks for precedence justification)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Parse Tree Yield with AST Traversal (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often claim that the inorder traversal of an AST yields the original source string. This is **false** as a definitional property. The inorder yield of a parse tree is the input string by definition; for an AST, inorder traversal only recovers the input if operator precedences are *implied* by convention. **Always state the property carefully.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Parenthesis / Bracket / Semicolon Nodes (–1 mark):** When drawing a parse tree, students often omit the `=`, `(`, `)`, or `;` nodes. These are **terminal symbols** and **must** appear as leaves. AST construction is the only place where they are dropped.
>
> 3. **Drawing AST with Non-terminals (–2 marks):** If the AST still shows $E, T, F$ as internal nodes, it is **incorrect**. The AST must show operator symbols (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `=`, etc.) as internal nodes.
>
> 4. **Wrong Tree Depth for Precedence (–1 to –2 marks):** Higher-precedence operators must be **deeper** in the AST. If `*` appears higher than `+`, the precedence is inverted and the answer is wrong.
>
> 5. **Omitting Justification in 14-mark Questions (–2 to –3 marks):** KTU board examiners specifically award marks for *explanation*. A correct tree alone is not enough — you **must** write 4–6 lines justifying why AST is preferred over parse tree.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Start Symbol Label at the Root (–1 mark):** The root of a parse tree is always the **start symbol** ($E$, $S$, or program non-terminal), never an operator.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Parse Trees and ASTs (Module 1, KTU 2024)**

- [ ] A **Parse Tree** = full derivation tree; root = start symbol; internal nodes = non-terminals; leaves = terminals.
- [ ] The **yield** of a parse tree (inorder leaf traversal) **equals** the input string — this is a *defining* property.
- [ ] An **AST** is a **condensed** tree with only operators (internal) and operands (leaves); redundant non-terminals, parentheses, and punctuation are removed.
- [ ] **Precedence** is encoded by **tree depth** in the AST (higher precedence = deeper in the tree).
- [ ] **Associativity** is encoded by **child position** in the AST (left-assoc = left subtree built first; right-assoc = right subtree built first).
- [ ] An **ambiguous grammar** produces **two or more** parse trees for the same input string. ASTs are inherently unambiguous.
- [ ] AST is preferred over Parse Tree in **semantic analysis, IR generation, and optimization** because it is **smaller, faster to traverse, and semantically meaningful**.
- [ ] ASTs are built using **Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)** — each grammar production is augmented with a *semantic action* that constructs an AST node at reduction time.
- [ ] A **DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph)** is a further optimization of AST where **common subexpressions are shared** (e.g., `(a+b)` used twice in the same expression becomes a single node with two parents).
- [ ] The **standard CFG** for arithmetic expressions is the three-level precedence grammar: $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$, $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \textbf{id}$ — **memorize this**; it appears in 80% of KTU parse tree questions.
- [ ] Real-world applications of AST: **GCC, LLVM, V8 (Chrome JavaScript), Babel, TypeScript, ESLint, SonarQube, Prettier, clang-format, IntelliJ refactoring**.
- [ ] Compiler pipeline position: **Lexical Analysis → Parsing (produces Parse Tree) → AST Construction → Semantic Analysis → IR Generation → Optimization → Code Generation**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ambiguity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ambiguity # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Ambiguity in a Context-Free Grammar (CFG):** > A grammar **G** is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string **w ∈ L(G)** (i.e., a string derivable from the grammar) for which **more than one distinct parse tree** can be constructed. Equivalently, the string admits **more than one leftmost derivation** OR **more than one rightmost derivation**. Mathematically, a grammar $G = (V, T, P, S)$ is ambiguous if: $$\exists \, w \in L(G) \text{ such that } \vert \{ \text{parse trees for } w \} \vert \geq 2$$ Conversely, a grammar is **unambiguous** if every string in the language has **exactly one** parse tree. > [!NOTE] > **Key Syllabus Highlight:** The concept of ambiguity is foundational in the **Syntax Analysis Phase** of compilation. An ambiguous grammar **cannot be used directly by a top-down (LL) or bottom-up (LR) parser** because the parser needs a unique structure to drive semantic actions (e.g., code generation, type checking). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two Interpretations" Problem Imagine you tell a friend: *"I saw the man with the telescope."* Did you **use the telescope** to see the man? Or did you see **a man who was holding a telescope**? The English sentence is **linguistically ambiguous** — the same string of words admits two valid grammatical structures. In compiler design, the input program is the "sentence," and the grammar rules are the "English grammar rules." If the grammar permits more than one way to group tokens, the compiler cannot decide **which order to evaluate expressions** or **which `if` an `else` belongs to** — leading to semantic inconsistency. > [!TIP] > **Real-world consequence:** Java's famous *"dangling else"* problem (resolved by specifying that `else` binds to the nearest unmatched `if`) is a direct outcome of ambiguity in early grammar specifications. ## 1.3 The Two Equivalent Criteria A grammar is ambiguous if **any** of the following equivalent conditions hold for some string $w$: | Criterion # | Condition | |:-----------:|:----------| | 1 | $w$ has **≥ 2 distinct parse trees** rooted at the start symbol | | 2 | $w$ has **≥ 2 distinct leftmost derivations** | | 3 | $w$ has **≥ 2 distinct rightmost derivations** | The equivalence between **Criteria 2 & 3** holds because each parse tree corresponds to exactly one leftmost AND exactly one rightmost derivation (and vice versa), following unique leftmost/rightmost substitution orders at every step. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 Why Ambiguity Arises — Structural Roots Ambiguity is **not** a property of a programming language — it is a property of the **specific grammar chosen** to describe that language. The same language can often be described by both an ambiguous and an unambiguous grammar. The structural roots of ambiguity in arithmetic expression grammars are: 1. **Absence of Operator Precedence** — When the grammar does not encode that `*` binds tighter than `+`, both groupings become valid. 2. **Absence of Associativity Rules** — When the grammar does not specify whether `a - b - c` is `(a-b)-c` or `a-(b-c)`. 3. **Unresolved Control-Flow Constructs** — The classic `if-then-else` ambiguity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Ambiguity is generally an **undesirable property** in the syntax-analysis phase because most practical parsers (LL, LR, LALR) **require unambiguous grammars** to operate deterministically. The compiler designer must therefore **transform** an ambiguous grammar into an equivalent unambiguous one. ## 2.2 Detection Strategy There is **no general algorithmic procedure** to test whether an arbitrary CFG is ambiguous (this is an undecidable problem). However, for specific small grammars, ambiguity is demonstrated by: - Constructing **two distinct parse trees** for the same string, **OR** - Exhibiting **two distinct leftmost derivations** for the same string. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Frequently Asked:** *Is there an algorithm to detect ambiguity?* > **Answer:** No, in general, ambiguity detection is **undecidable**. It can only be demonstrated for specific strings, not proven absent for arbitrary grammars. ## 2.3 Inherent Ambiguity A context-free language **L** is called **inherently ambiguous** if **every** grammar that generates $L$ is ambiguous. In such cases, the language itself carries the ambiguity — no clever grammar rewriting can eliminate it. > Classic example: $L = \{ a^n b^n c^m d^m \mid n \geq 1, m \geq 1 \} \cup \{ a^n b^m c^m d^n \mid n \geq 1, m \geq 1 \}$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula / Decision Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Formula | KTU Significance | |:--------|:---------------|:-----------------| | Ambiguity Test | Find $w$ with $\geq 2$ parse trees | Demonstrative, not algorithmic | | Removal via Precedence | Introduce separate non-terminals per precedence level | Enforces `*` > `+` binding | | Removal via Associativity | Force **left-recursive** or **right-recursive** rules for the same operator | Enforces left/right grouping | | Dangling Else | Match `else` to **nearest unmatched `if`** | Required by C, C++, Java, C# specs | | Inherent Ambiguity | Exists if **no** unambiguous grammar generates $L$ | Undecidable in general | | Top-Down Parser Compatibility | Requires **unambiguous** + **left-factored** + **no left recursion** | Direct KTU Module-1 / Module-2 link | | Bottom-Up Parser Compatibility | Requires **unambiguous** grammar; LR(1) resolves shifts | Direct KTU Module-3 link | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Resolving ambiguity is **not just an academic exercise** — it is critical in: - **Compiler Construction:** Defines the exact evaluation order of expressions. - **Natural Language Processing (NLP):** Probabilistic grammars (PCFGs) handle ambiguity via likelihoods. - **Hardware Description Languages (HDL):** Verilog/VHDL grammars must be unambiguous to generate deterministic hardware. - **Database Query Languages (SQL):** Operator precedence & associativity in SQL follow unambiguous grammar rules. ## 2.6 Summary of Removal Techniques | # | Technique | When Applicable | |:-:|:----------|:----------------| | 1 | **Operator Precedence Splitting** — distinct non-terminals per precedence tier | Arithmetic/logical expression grammars | | 2 | **Left/Right Associativity Enforcement** — left- or right-recursive production | Operators of equal precedence | | 3 | **Rewriting Dangling-Else Rules** — separate matched/unmatched `if` non-terminals | `if-then-else` constructs | | 4 | **Left Factoring** — extract common prefixes | Often combined with ambiguity removal | | 5 | **Eliminating Left Recursion** — convert to right recursion | Required for top-down parsers | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Canonical Ambiguous Grammar — Arithmetic Expressions Consider the KTU-favourite classical ambiguous grammar for arithmetic expressions: $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$ Let the input string be: $$w = \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ We will demonstrate ambiguity by constructing **two distinct parse trees** for $w$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation Path A — `*` Binds Tighter (Correct Precedence) This is the **intended** parse tree where multiplication binds tighter than addition, i.e., $E \Rightarrow E + (E * E)$. **Step A.1:** Start the derivation from the start symbol $E$. $$E \Rightarrow E + E$$ *(Logic: We want the `+` to be the outermost operation, so we pick the production $E \rightarrow E + E$. The first $E$ will derive `id`, the second $E` will derive `id * id`.)* **Step A.2:** Replace the **left** $E$ with $\text{id}$. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E$$ *(Logic: The leftmost non-terminal is rewritten. We use the production $E \rightarrow \text{id}$.)* **Step A.3:** Replace the **right** $E$ with $E * E$ to enforce that `*` is grouped first. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E$$ *(Logic: We pick $E \rightarrow E * E$ for the rightmost non-terminal, reflecting the higher precedence of `*` over `+`.)* **Step A.4:** Rewrite the leftmost $E$ in `E * E` (which is the second non-terminal overall) with $\text{id}$. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E$$ *(Logic: Leftmost derivation rule — always expand the leftmost non-terminal first.)* **Step A.5:** Rewrite the remaining $E$ with $\text{id}$. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ *(Logic: Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the only remaining non-terminal.)* **Final leftmost derivation (Path A):** $$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ **This represents the structure: $E \Rightarrow (E + (E * E))$ — meaning $\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})$** --- ### 3.3 Derivation Path B — `+` Binds Tighter (Wrong Precedence) This produces the **incorrect** parse tree where `+` binds tighter, i.e., $E \Rightarrow (E + E) * E$. **Step B.1:** Start the derivation. We want `*` to be the outermost operation, so pick $E \rightarrow E * E$ first. $$E \Rightarrow E * E$$ *(Logic: The first `id` will come from the left $E$, and the structure `id + id` will come from the right $E$, then multiplied by the trailing `id`.)* **Step B.2:** Replace the **left** $E$ with $E + E$ to force `+` inside. $$E \Rightarrow E + E * E$$ *(Logic: Using $E \rightarrow E + E$ for the leftmost non-terminal.)* **Step B.3:** Replace the leftmost $E$ (first position) with $\text{id}$. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E$$ *(Logic: Apply $E \rightarrow \text{id}$ to the leftmost non-terminal.)* **Step B.4:** Replace the middle $E$ with $\text{id}$. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E$$ *(Logic: Leftmost non-terminal now is the one between `+` and `*`.)* **Step B.5:** Replace the rightmost $E$ with $\text{id}$. $$E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ *(Logic: Final application of $E \rightarrow \text{id}$.)* **Final leftmost derivation (Path B):** $$E \Rightarrow E * E \Rightarrow E + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$$ **This represents the structure: $E \Rightarrow ((E + E) * E)$ — meaning $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$** --- ### 3.4 Ambiguity Confirmed Both derivations produce the **identical string** $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ but yield **two semantically different structures**: | Path | Structural Meaning | Evaluation Result (assuming id = 2) | |:----:|:-------------------|:-----------------------------------:| | **A** | $\text{id} + (\text{id} * \text{id})$ | $2 + (2 * 2) = 2 + 4 = 6$ | | **B** | $(\text{id} + \text{id}) * \text{id}$ | $(2 + 2) * 2 = 4 * 2 = 8$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The grammar is **ambiguous** because the same string admits two distinct parse trees (and two distinct leftmost derivations). --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Removing Ambiguity We construct an **equivalent unambiguous grammar** by introducing one non-terminal per precedence level and enforcing **left-associativity** via **left-recursive** rules. ### Original (Ambiguous) Grammar: $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$ ### Transformed (Unambiguous) Grammar: $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\ T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id} \end{aligned} $$ ### Why this works — Layer-by-Layer Logic | Layer | Non-Terminal | Role | Associativity | Precedence | |:-----:|:-------------|:-----|:--------------|:-----------| | 1 (Lowest) | $E$ | Sums (`+`, `-`) | Left-associative | Lowest | | 2 (Middle) | $T$ | Products (`*`, `/`) | Left-associative | Higher than $E$ | | 3 (Highest) | $F$ | Atomic operands (`id`, `(...)`) | N/A | Highest | > [!NOTE] > **Logic of precedence:** Since $E$ can only derive a $T$ (which is "atomic" with respect to sums), and $T$ can only derive a $F$, the `*` operator (inside $T$) is **forced** to be grouped before any surrounding `+` (in $E$). > [!NOTE] > **Logic of associativity:** The rule $E \rightarrow E + T$ is **left-recursive**, meaning the operator combines with the **left** operand first. This forces $\text{id} - \text{id} - \text{id}$ to parse as $(\text{id} - \text{id}) - \text{id}$, matching standard arithmetic convention. ### Verification: Re-derive $\text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}$ with the New Grammar $$ \begin{aligned} E &\Rightarrow E + T \quad \text{(outermost is `+`)} \\ &\Rightarrow T + T \quad \text{(left $E \rightarrow T$)} \\ &\Rightarrow F + T \quad \text{(left $T \rightarrow F$)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{id} + T \quad \text{(left $F \rightarrow \text{id}$)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{id} + T * F \quad \text{(middle $T \rightarrow T * F$)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{id} + F * F \quad \text{(left $T \rightarrow F$)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F \quad \text{(left $F \rightarrow \text{id}$)} \\ &\Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id} \quad \text{(right $F \rightarrow \text{id}$)} \end{aligned} $$ **Unique parse tree** — ambiguity is eliminated. ## 3.6 The Dangling-Else Ambiguity ### Ambiguous Grammar (simplified): $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow \text{if } C \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } C \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \mid \text{other} \end{aligned} $$ ### Problem String: $$\text{if } C_1 \text{ then if } C_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2$$ This string has **two** parse trees: - **Tree 1:** `else` binds to **outer** `if` — i.e., `if C1 then (if C2 then S1 else S2)`. - **Tree 2:** `else` binds to **inner** `if` — i.e., `if C1 then (if C2 then S1) else S2`. ### Unambiguous Rewrite (Convention: `else` matches nearest unmatched `if`): $$ \begin{aligned} S &\rightarrow \text{Matched} \mid \text{Unmatched} \\ \text{Matched} &\rightarrow \text{if } C \text{ then Matched else Matched} \mid \text{other} \\ \text{Unmatched} &\rightarrow \text{if } C \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } C \text{ then Matched else Unmatched} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **KTU Insight:** Modern languages (C, C++, Java, C#, JavaScript) resolve this ambiguity by **language specification rule** — "an `else` is associated with the closest preceding `if` that does not already have an `else`" — which corresponds exactly to the **Unmatched** non-terminal in the unambiguous grammar above. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Two Parse Trees for `id + id * id` (Demonstrating Ambiguity) ```mermaid graph TD rootA["E"] --> sumA["E"] rootA --> plusA["+"] rootA --> prodA["E"] sumA --> leaf1A["id"] prodA --> prodLeftA["E"] prodA --> starA["*"] prodA --> prodRightA["E"] prodLeftA --> leaf2A["id"] prodRightA --> leaf3A["id"] classDef correct fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef wrong fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class rootA,sumA,prodA,plusA,prodLeftA,prodRightA,starA,leaf1A,leaf2A,leaf3A correct ``` **Tree A — Correct Precedence:** `id + (id * id)` — multiplication grouped inside addition. ```mermaid graph TD rootB["E"] --> prodB["E"] rootB --> starB["*"] rootB --> rightB["E"] prodB --> sumB["E"] prodB --> plusB["+"] prodB --> leaf1B["id"] sumB --> leaf2B["id"] rightB --> leaf3B["id"] classDef wrong fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef correct fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class rootB,prodB,sumB,rightB,starB,plusB,leaf1B,leaf2B,leaf3B wrong ``` **Tree B — Incorrect Precedence:** `(id + id) * id` — addition grouped inside multiplication. > [!NOTE] > Both trees yield the **same string** `id + id * id` but **different evaluation semantics**. This is the textbook hallmark of an ambiguous grammar. ## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Ambiguity Resolution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Ambiguous Grammar G"] --> B{"Ambiguity
Detected?"} B -- "Yes" --> C["Apply Precedence
Splitting"] C --> D["Apply Associativity
Enforcement"] D --> E["Unambiguous
Grammar G'"] B -- "No" --> F["Use Grammar Directly"] E --> G["LL Parser
Top-Down"] E --> H["LR Parser
Bottom-Up"] F --> G F --> H G --> I["Deterministic
Parse Tree"] H --> I classDef input fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef output fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class A input class B,C,D process class E,F,G,H,I output ``` **Flow Interpretation:** | Stage | Function | |:------|:---------| | **Ambiguous Grammar G** | Original specification (often natural & concise but unusable by parsers) | | **Ambiguity Detected?** | Manual or tool-assisted identification (no general algorithm exists) | | **Precedence Splitting** | Introduce a non-terminal per operator-precedence level | | **Associativity Enforcement** | Use left/right recursion to fix grouping order | | **Unambiguous Grammar G'** | Equivalent language, but single parse tree per string | | **Parser Input** | Feeds both LL and LR parser families | ## 4.3 Unambiguous Grammar — Precedence Tiers as a Layered Stack ```mermaid graph TD E["E (Lowest Precedence)"] -->|E -> E + T| Elow["E"] Elow --> T1["T"] E -->|E -> T| Thigh1["T"] T1["T (Mid Precedence)"] -->|T -> T * F| Tlow["T"] Tlow --> F1["F"] T1 -->|T -> F| Fhigh1["F"] F2["F (Highest - Atoms)"] -->|F -> id| idNode["id"] F2 -->|F -> ( E )| parenNode["( E )"] classDef low fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef high fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 class E,Elow,Thigh1 low class T1,Tlow,Fhigh1 mid class F2,idNode,parenNode high ``` **Layer Interpretation:** | Layer | Symbol | Contains Operators | Associativity | |:------|:-------|:-------------------|:--------------| | Top (lowest prec.) | $E$ | `+`, `-` | Left | | Middle | $T$ | `*`, `/` | Left | | Bottom (highest prec.) | $F$ | Atoms: `id`, `(...)` | N/A | # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model] **Define ambiguity in a context-free grammar. State one necessary condition for a grammar to be unambiguous.** **Model Answer:** > A context-free grammar $G$ is said to be **ambiguous** if there exists at least one string $w \in L(G)$ that can be derived using **more than one distinct parse tree**, or equivalently, by more than one leftmost (or rightmost) derivation. > > A grammar is **unambiguous** if and only if **every string in the language it generates has exactly one parse tree** rooted at the start symbol. > > *One necessary condition for unambiguity: for every string $w \in L(G)$, the number of distinct leftmost derivations of $w$ must be exactly one.* [3 marks] --- ### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model] **State whether the following grammar is ambiguous. Justify with a one-line example:** $$S \rightarrow aS \mid aSb \mid \varepsilon$$ **Model Answer:** > The given grammar is **unambiguous**. Every string $a^n b^m$ in $L(G)$ has a unique parse tree because the number of $a$'s generated before switching to $b$-generation is uniquely determined by the structure: $S$ can only switch from $aS$ to $aSb$ at one specific point, enforcing a unique count of $a$'s preceding each $b$. [3 marks] --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Show that the following grammar is ambiguous by constructing two distinct parse trees for the string `id + id + id`: $$E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$$ **(7 marks)** **(b)** Rewrite the grammar to make it unambiguous, clearly explaining how precedence and associativity are enforced. Show the unique parse tree for `id + id + id`. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q A (a):** > **Two Distinct Parse Trees for** $w = \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$ > > **Tree 1: Left-associative grouping —** $(\text{id} + \text{id}) + \text{id}$ > > - Root: $E$ expands to $E + E$ > - Left $E$ expands to $E + E$ again, then both inner $E$'s to `id` > - Right $E$ expands to `id` > > This gives the leftmost derivation: > > $$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow E + E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$$ > > **Tree 2: Right-associative grouping —** $\text{id} + (\text{id} + \text{id})$ > > - Root: $E$ expands to $E + E$ > - Left $E$ expands to `id` > - Right $E$ expands to $E + E$, then both inner $E$'s to `id` > > This gives the leftmost derivation: > > $$E \Rightarrow E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + E + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + E \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$$ > > **Conclusion:** Two different parse trees produce the same string — **grammar is ambiguous**. [7 marks] --- #### **Model Solution — Q A (b):** > **Unambiguous Rewrite:** > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > E &\rightarrow E + T \mid T \\ > T &\rightarrow T * F \mid F \\ > F &\rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > **Precedence enforcement:** The non-terminals form a **three-tier hierarchy** — $E$ (lowest) → $T$ (mid) → $F$ (highest). Since $E$ can only invoke $T$, and $T$ can only invoke $F$, the `*` operator (handled inside $T$) is structurally forced to be grouped **before** any `+` operator (handled in $E$). This correctly implements standard precedence: `*` binds tighter than `+`. > > **Associativity enforcement:** The production $E \rightarrow E + T$ is **left-recursive**, which forces the `+` operator to combine with its **left** operand first. Thus, $\text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$ is parsed as $(\text{id} + \text{id}) + \text{id}$, matching conventional arithmetic. > > **Unique parse tree for** $\text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$: > > The only valid leftmost derivation is: > > $$E \Rightarrow E + T \Rightarrow E + T + T \Rightarrow T + T + T \Rightarrow F + T + T \Rightarrow \text{id} + T + T \Rightarrow \text{id} + F + T \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + T \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + F \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} + \text{id}$$ > > This corresponds to the structure $(\text{id} + \text{id}) + \text{id}$ — unique. [7 marks] --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Explain the **dangling-else ambiguity** with a suitable example. Construct the two distinct parse trees for the ambiguous string. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Rewrite the `if-then-else` grammar to make it unambiguous. Apply the resulting grammar to derive the correct parse tree for your chosen example. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Q B (a):** > **Dangling-Else Ambiguity:** > > Consider the grammar: > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > S &\rightarrow \text{if } C \text{ then } S \\ > &\quad \vert \ \text{if } C \text{ then } S \text{ else } S \\ > &\quad \vert \ \text{other} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > For the input string: > > $$w = \text{if } C_1 \text{ then if } C_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2$$ > > Two parse trees exist: > > **Tree 1** — `else` binds to **outer** `if`: > > $S \Rightarrow \text{if } C_1 \text{ then } (\text{if } C_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2)$ — here the inner `if-then-else` is a complete `S`. > > **Tree 2** — `else` binds to **inner** `if`: > > $S \Rightarrow \text{if } C_1 \text{ then } (\text{if } C_2 \text{ then } S_1) \text{ else } S_2$ — here the inner `if` is just a `then`-only statement, leaving the `else` to attach to the outer. > > **Conclusion:** The single `else` in the input is "dangling" — it can pair with either of the two `if`s, producing semantically different programs. [7 marks] --- #### **Model Solution — Q B (b):** > **Unambiguous Rewrite (separating matched from unmatched `if`s):** > > $$ > \begin{aligned} > S &\rightarrow \text{Matched} \mid \text{Unmatched} \\ > \text{Matched} &\rightarrow \text{if } C \text{ then Matched else Matched} \mid \text{other} \\ > \text{Unmatched} &\rightarrow \text{if } C \text{ then } S \mid \text{if } C \text{ then Matched else Unmatched} > \end{aligned} > $$ > > **Semantic Convention:** An `else` is matched with the **nearest preceding unmatched `if`**. The grammar enforces this by: > > - A `then`-only `if` is permitted **only** inside an `Unmatched` statement (i.e., it is the closest `if` available to consume a future `else`). > - A `Matched` statement always has both `then` and `else` branches complete, so it cannot "steal" a future `else`. > > **Application to the example:** > > For $w = \text{if } C_1 \text{ then if } C_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2$, the unique parse is: > > $S \Rightarrow \text{Unmatched} \Rightarrow \text{if } C_1 \text{ then Matched else Unmatched}$ > > where the inner part is a **Matched** statement: $\text{if } C_2 \text{ then } S_1 \text{ else } S_2$. > > The `else` is unambiguously bound to the **inner** `if` (the nearest unmatched one). [7 marks] --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Ambiguity Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the "more than one parse tree" condition** — students often write only "more than one derivation." Always explicitly state **parse trees**, then mention the **equivalent leftmost/rightmost** form. > 2. **Confusing *grammar* ambiguity with *language* ambiguity** — A language can be inherently ambiguous even if some grammar for it is not. Examiners check for this distinction. > 3. **Skipping the left-recursive form when rewriting** — A common error is using right recursion ($E \rightarrow T + E$), which enforces right-associativity. For standard arithmetic, **left-associativity is expected** → use $E \rightarrow E + T$. > 4. **Not drawing the parse tree in part (a)** — even if the derivation is written, KTU valuators expect a **clear tree diagram** with leaf and internal nodes labelled. Marks are lost when only derivations are shown. > 5. **Forgetting to show that the *rewritten* grammar generates the same language** — a brief note (e.g., "this grammar accepts the same set of strings, as each `id` or parenthesised expression is still derivable") earns full marks. > 6. **In dangling-else questions**, not explicitly stating the **language-design rule** (nearest unmatched `if`) costs at least 1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Ambiguity** > > - **Definition:** A grammar is **ambiguous** if ∃ a string with **≥ 2 parse trees** (equivalently, ≥ 2 leftmost or rightmost derivations). > - **Equivalent tests:** Multiple parse trees ⇔ multiple leftmost derivations ⇔ multiple rightmost derivations. > - **Unambiguity requirement:** All standard parsers (LL, LR, LALR) require an **unambiguous** grammar to function deterministically. > - **Arithmetic grammar classic:** $E \rightarrow E + E \mid E * E \mid (E) \mid \text{id}$ is **ambiguous** because it lacks both precedence and associativity. > - **Removal strategy #1 — Precedence:** Use one non-terminal per precedence level. Lower-tier non-terminals invoke higher-tier ones, never the reverse. > - **Removal strategy #2 — Associativity:** Use **left-recursion** ($E \rightarrow E + T$) for left-associative operators, **right-recursion** ($E \rightarrow T + E$) for right-associative ones. Standard arithmetic uses **left**-associativity. > - **Standard unambiguous arithmetic grammar (KTU-favourite):** $E \rightarrow E + T \mid T$; $T \rightarrow T * F \mid F$; $F \rightarrow (E) \mid \text{id}$. > - **Dangling-else problem:** Caused by the `if C then S` vs. `if C then S else S` ambiguity. Resolution: split into **Matched** (always has `else`) and **Unmatched** (may or may not) non-terminals. `else` binds to the **nearest unmatched `if`**. > - **Inherent ambiguity:** A language is inherently ambiguous if **every** grammar generating it is ambiguous. **Detection is undecidable** in general. > - **No general algorithm:** There is **no algorithm** to test CFG ambiguity for arbitrary grammars — it is an undecidable problem. > - **KTU exam tip:** Always demonstrate ambiguity by **exhibiting two distinct parse trees**, not just two derivations. The diagram is what earns full marks. > - **Real-world impact:** Ambiguity directly determines **evaluation order** of expressions and **control-flow binding** in compiled programs — incorrect resolution changes program semantics.

topics.title: Top-Down Parsing - Recursive Descent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Top-Down Parsing - Recursive Descent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Top-Down Parsing** is a class of syntax analysis techniques in which the parse tree is constructed starting from the **root node** (the start symbol of the grammar) and proceeds downward toward the **leaves** (the terminal symbols of the input string). The parser essentially attempts to derive the input string by applying production rules in a leftmost fashion, predicting which production to apply at each step.

**Recursive Descent Parsing (RDP)** is a specific, widely-used form of **top-down parsing** in which a separate **recursive procedure** is written for **each non-terminal** of the grammar. Each procedure is responsible for recognizing all possible derivations of the corresponding non-terminal symbol in the input stream. When a procedure is invoked, it inspects the next input token (the *lookahead*) and, based on a deterministic decision, either consumes the token or calls another non-terminal's procedure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim-style):**
> *Recursive Descent Parsing is a top-down parsing method where each non-terminal of the grammar is associated with a recursive procedure, and the parser builds the parse tree by recursively expanding non-terminals starting from the start symbol until the entire input string is matched.*

There are two main variants of Recursive Descent Parsing:

| Variant | Description | Determinism |
|---|---|---|
| **Backtracking Recursive Descent** | Tries all productions one-by-one; rolls back on failure | Non-deterministic, slow |
| **Predictive Recursive Descent** | Uses lookahead to deterministically choose **one** production | Deterministic, **O(n)** time |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, the focus is strictly on **Predictive Recursive Descent Parsing**, which is the practical, efficient form used in real compilers.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **tour guide in a huge museum** 🏛️. You start at the **main entrance** (the start symbol `S`). At every room you enter, a **signboard** (the lookahead token) tells you which of several possible doors (productions) to take next. Each door leads to another room (a non-terminal) or directly to an exhibit (a terminal).

- **Top-Down = walking from the entrance inward** (root → leaves).
- **Recursive Descent = each type of room has its own dedicated guide** (procedure) who knows exactly where to lead you next.
- **Predictive Parsing = the signboard is clear and unambiguous**, so you never have to backtrack to a previous room.

If the signboard is ambiguous (e.g., two doors look the same), the guides get confused — this is exactly what happens with grammars that have a **common prefix** in their productions, which is why we apply **left factoring** before writing recursive descent code.

---

### 1.3 Key Characteristics (KTU Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Pre-requisites for Recursive Descent Parsing:**
> 1. The grammar must be **free of left recursion** (direct + indirect).
> 2. The grammar must be **left-factored** (no common prefixes in alternatives).
> 3. The grammar must allow construction of a **predictive parsing table** with **no multiply-defined entries** (i.e., the grammar must be in the class **LL(1)**).

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Lookahead `k`**: For pure recursive descent, the standard lookahead is **k = 1** token.
- **Grammar Class**: **LL(1)** — *Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 token of lookahead*.
- **Time Complexity**: **O(n)** where `n` is the length of the input string.
- **Space Complexity**: **O(d)** where `d` is the depth of the parse tree (recursion stack).
- **Maximum call stack depth**: Determined by the *longest chain of non-terminals* in any production.

---

### 1.5 Block Diagram — Position of Recursive Descent in Compiler Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> Recursive Descent Parsing sits in the **Front-End → Syntax Analysis Phase** of a compiler, sitting on top of the **Lexical Analyzer (scanner)** and feeding the **Semantic Analyzer / Intermediate Code Generator** below it.

The flow is:

```
Source Code → Lexical Analyzer → Tokens → Recursive Descent Parser → Parse Tree (or Syntax Error)
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A Recursive Descent Parser is a collection of **mutually recursive procedures**, one for each non-terminal. The execution of the parser can be broken down into the following structured steps:

**Step 1 — Preprocessing the Grammar (Offline Step):**
- Eliminate **left recursion** using the standard algorithm.
- Apply **left factoring** to remove common prefixes.
- Compute **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for every non-terminal.
- Construct the **LL(1) parsing table** `M[A, a]`.

**Step 2 — Invocation of `procedure S()`:**
- The parser begins by calling the procedure corresponding to the **start symbol `S`**.
- The input pointer points to the first token of the source program.

**Step 3 — Lookahead Inspection:**
- Inside each procedure, the current input token `a` is read (without consuming it).
- The parsing table entry `M[A, a]` is consulted to decide which production `A → α` to apply.

**Step 4 — Production Expansion:**
- For each symbol in the RHS `α` of the chosen production:
  - If the symbol is a **terminal** `t` and matches the current input token, **consume** the token and advance the input pointer.
  - If the symbol is a **non-terminal** `B`, **recursively call** the procedure `procedure B()`.
  - If the symbol is `ε` (epsilon), do nothing — return to the caller.

**Step 5 — Termination:**
- If the entire input is consumed and the parser returns to the start procedure successfully, the input is **syntactically valid**.
- If at any point `M[A, a]` is **empty** (error entry), the parser reports a **syntax error** and may invoke an **error-recovery routine** (panic-mode recovery using synchronizing tokens from `FOLLOW(A)`).

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it is Needed |
|---|---|
| Grammar preprocessing | Recursive descent **cannot** handle left-recursive grammars because it would cause infinite recursion. |
| Lookahead inspection | Without lookahead, two productions with the same prefix would be indistinguishable. |
| Mutual recursion | Each non-terminal may appear in the RHS of others, requiring **mutual** (not just self) recursion. |
| Error entries in table | The empty entries in `M` are the **synchronization points** for panic-mode error recovery. |
| `ε` handling | Epsilon productions allow the parser to "match nothing" — crucial for optional constructs. |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FIRST(α)** | If `X` is terminal: `FIRST(X) = {X}`. If `X → ε`: add `ε`. If `X → Y₁Y₂…Yₖ`: add `FIRST(Y₁) − {ε}`; if `ε ∈ FIRST(Y₁)`, then add `FIRST(Y₂) − {ε}`, and so on. | $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ | Set of terminals that can begin a string derived from `α`. |
| **FOLLOW(A)** | (1) Place `$` in `FOLLOW(S)`. (2) If `A → αBβ`: `FIRST(β) − {ε}` ⊆ `FOLLOW(B)`. (3) If `A → αB` or `A → αBβ` with `ε ∈ FIRST(β)`: `FOLLOW(A)` ⊆ `FOLLOW(B)`. | $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals that can immediately follow `A` in a sentential form. |
| **Parsing Table Entry** | `M[A, a] = A → α` if `a ∈ FIRST(α)`, or if `ε ∈ FIRST(α)` and `a ∈ FOLLOW(A)`. | $M[A, a]$ | Defines which production to apply. |
| **LL(1) Condition** | For every non-terminal `A` with productions `A → α₁ ∣ α₂ ∣ … ∣ αₙ`: `FIRST(αᵢ) ∩ FIRST(αⱼ) = ∅` for `i ≠ j`, and at most one `αᵢ` can derive `ε`. | $\text{Grammar is } LL(1)$ | Ensures unique table entry per cell. |
| **Left Recursion Elimination** | `A → Aα ∣ β`  ⟹  `A → βA'` and `A' → αA' ∣ ε`. | — | Removes infinite recursion. |
| **Left Factoring** | `A → αβ₁ ∣ αβ₂`  ⟹  `A → αA'` and `A' → β₁ ∣ β₂`. | — | Enables unique lookahead decision. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Cells:** The vertical pipe `|` is **forbidden** in KTU answer-sheet markdown tables. Always write $\vert$ or $\mid$ inside LaTeX expressions to avoid table breakage.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Recursive Descent Parsing is used in production compilers and tools, including:

- **GCC** (in early stages for specific languages)
- **Clang** (LLVM front-end) for parsing C/C++/Objective-C
- **ANTLR** (ANother Tool for Language Recognition) — generates recursive-descent parsers from grammar specifications
- **JavaCC** — Java Compiler Compiler, a popular RDP generator
- **Parser combinator libraries** in functional languages (Parsec in Haskell, nom in Rust)
- **Hand-written parsers** in many scripting language interpreters (Python, Lua)

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Recursive descent is favored in **domain-specific languages (DSLs)** and **expression evaluators** in CAD/CAM software, configuration-file parsers in embedded firmware, and command-line argument parsers in CLI tools. Its main advantage is **readability** — the code mirrors the grammar almost one-to-one.

---

### 2.5 Algorithm: The Predictive Parsing Algorithm

```
Input:  A string w, a parsing table M, start symbol S
Output: If w ∈ L(G), a leftmost derivation; otherwise, an error

ip ← pointer to first symbol of w$
push S onto an initially empty stack
repeat
    X ← top of stack
    a ← symbol pointed to by ip
    if X is a terminal or X = $ then
        if X = a then
            pop X
            advance ip
        else
            ERROR()
    else  // X is a non-terminal
        if M[X, a] = X → Y₁Y₂…Yₖ then
            pop X
            push Yₖ, Yₖ₋₁, …, Y₁ onto stack  // Y₁ on top
            output production X → Y₁Y₂…Yₖ
        else
            ERROR()
until X = $   // stack empty and input consumed
```

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Building a Recursive Descent Parser for Arithmetic Expressions

Consider the following classic grammar for arithmetic expressions with `+` and `*`:

$$
E \rightarrow E + T \mid T
$$
$$
T \rightarrow T * F \mid F
$$
$$
F \rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id}
$$

This grammar has **immediate left recursion** in `E` and `T`. We **cannot** directly build a recursive descent parser for it.

---

#### Step A — Eliminate Left Recursion

Apply the standard left-recursion elimination:

For `E`:

$$
E \rightarrow T\,E'
$$
$$
E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E' \mid \varepsilon
$$

For `T`:

$$
T \rightarrow F\,T'
$$
$$
T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T' \mid \varepsilon
$$

`F` has no left recursion and stays the same:

$$
F \rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id}
$$

**Resulting Grammar `G'` (left-recursion-free):**

$$
E \rightarrow T\,E'
$$
$$
E' \rightarrow +\,T\,E' \mid \varepsilon
$$
$$
T \rightarrow F\,T'
$$
$$
T' \rightarrow *\,F\,T' \mid \varepsilon
$$
$$
F \rightarrow ( E ) \mid \text{id}
$$

---

#### Step B — Compute FIRST Sets

We compute `FIRST` for every non-terminal by iterative fixed-point.

**Non-terminal `F`:**
Productions: `F → ( E )` and `F → id`.
Both start with terminals.
$$
\text{FIRST}(F) = \{ (,\ \text{id} \}
$$

**Non-terminal `T`:**
Productions: `T → F T'`. The first symbol is `F`, so:
$$
\text{FIRST}(T) = \text{FIRST}(F) = \{ (,\ \text{id} \}
$$

**Non-terminal `E`:**
Productions: `E → T E'`. The first symbol is `T`, so:
$$
\text{FIRST}(E) = \text{FIRST}(T) = \{ (,\ \text{id} \}
$$

**Non-terminal `E'`:**
Productions: `E' → + T E'` and `E' → ε`. The first starts with `+`, the second is epsilon.
$$
\text{FIRST}(E') = \{ +,\ \varepsilon \}
$$

**Non-terminal `T'`:**
Productions: `T' → * F T'` and `T' → ε`. The first starts with `*`, the second is epsilon.
$$
\text{FIRST}(T') = \{ *,\ \varepsilon \}
$$

**Summary Table of FIRST Sets:**

| Non-terminal | FIRST |
|---|---|
| `E`  | `{ (, id }` |
| `E'` | `{ +, ε }` |
| `T`  | `{ (, id }` |
| `T'` | `{ *, ε }` |
| `F`  | `{ (, id }` |

---

#### Step C — Compute FOLLOW Sets

We apply the three rules iteratively until no change.

**Initialize:**
- `FOLLOW(E) = { $ }`  (start symbol)
- All other `FOLLOW` sets empty.

**Rule application:**

**Pass 1:**

1. Production `E → T E'`: For non-terminal `E'`, the symbol after it is end-of-production. So `FOLLOW(E) ⊆ FOLLOW(E')`.
   - `FOLLOW(E') = { $ }`

2. Production `E' → + T E'`: For non-terminal `T`, the symbol after it is `E'`. So `FIRST(E') − {ε} ⊆ FOLLOW(T)`.
   - `FIRST(E') − {ε} = { + }`. So `FOLLOW(T) = { + }`.

3. Production `T → F T'`: For non-terminal `F`, the symbol after it is `T'`. So `FIRST(T') − {ε} ⊆ FOLLOW(F)`.
   - `FIRST(T') − {ε} = { * }`. So `FOLLOW(F) = { * }`.

4. Production `T' → * F T'`: For non-terminal `F`, the symbol after it is `T'`. So `FIRST(T') − {ε} ⊆ FOLLOW(F)`.
   - Already `*` ∈ `FOLLOW(F)`. No change.

5. Production `F → ( E )`: For non-terminal `E`, the symbol after it is `)`. So add `)` to `FOLLOW(E)`.
   - `FOLLOW(E) = { $, ) }`.

**Pass 2 (recheck for ε-productions):**

6. Production `E' → + T E'`: After `T` is `E'`. Since `ε ∈ FIRST(E')`, we must also add `FOLLOW(E')` to `FOLLOW(T)`.
   - `FOLLOW(E') = { $ }`. So `FOLLOW(T) = { +, $ }`.

7. Production `T' → * F T'`: After `F` is `T'`. Since `ε ∈ FIRST(T')`, we must also add `FOLLOW(T')` to `FOLLOW(F)`.
   - `FOLLOW(T')` is still being computed — currently empty. No change.

**Pass 3:**

8. Production `T → F T'`: After `F` is `T'`. Since `ε ∈ FIRST(T')`, we must also add `FOLLOW(T')` to `FOLLOW(F)`.
   - We need `FOLLOW(T')` first.

9. To compute `FOLLOW(T')`: Look at productions where `T'` appears on RHS:
   - `E' → + T E'`: `T'` is not on RHS. (Skip.)
   - `T → F T'`: After `T'` is end-of-production. So `FOLLOW(T) ⊆ FOLLOW(T')`.
     - `FOLLOW(T) = { +, $ }`. So `FOLLOW(T') = { +, $ }`.

10. Revisit step 8: `FOLLOW(F) = { *, + }` after adding `FOLLOW(T') = { +, $ }` minus nothing → `FOLLOW(F) = { *, +, $ }`.

11. Revisit `FOLLOW(T')`: Any other production with `T'` on RHS?
    - `T' → * F T'`: After inner `T'` is end-of-production. So `FOLLOW(T') ⊆ FOLLOW(T')`. No change.

**Pass 4 (no changes):** Terminate.

**Summary Table of FOLLOW Sets:**

| Non-terminal | FOLLOW |
|---|---|
| `E`  | `{ $, ) }` |
| `E'` | `{ $, ) }` |
| `T`  | `{ +, $, ) }` |
| `T'` | `{ +, $, ) }` |
| `F`  | `{ *, +, $, ) }` |

---

#### Step D — Build the LL(1) Parsing Table `M`

For each production `A → α`, compute `FIRST(α)` and place `A → α` in `M[A, a]` for each `a ∈ FIRST(α) − {ε}`. If `ε ∈ FIRST(α)`, then for each `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)`, place `A → α` in `M[A, b]`.

**Production 1: `E → T E'`**
- `FIRST(T E') = FIRST(T) = { (, id }` (no ε in `FIRST(T)`).
- Place `E → T E'` in cells: `M[E, (]`, `M[E, id]`.

**Production 2: `E' → + T E'`**
- `FIRST(+ T E') = { + }`.
- Place `E' → + T E'` in cell: `M[E', +]`.

**Production 3: `E' → ε`**
- `FIRST(ε) = { ε }`. Use `FOLLOW(E') = { $, ) }`.
- Place `E' → ε` in cells: `M[E', $]`, `M[E', )]`.

**Production 4: `T → F T'`**
- `FIRST(F T') = FIRST(F) = { (, id }`.
- Place `T → F T'` in cells: `M[T, (]`, `M[T, id]`.

**Production 5: `T' → * F T'`**
- `FIRST(* F T') = { * }`.
- Place `T' → * F T'` in cell: `M[T', *]`.

**Production 6: `T' → ε`**
- Use `FOLLOW(T') = { +, $, ) }`.
- Place `T' → ε` in cells: `M[T', +]`, `M[T', $]`, `M[T', )]`.

**Production 7: `F → ( E )`**
- `FIRST(( E )) = { ( }`.
- Place `F → ( E )` in cell: `M[F, (]`.

**Production 8: `F → id`**
- `FIRST(id) = { id }`.
- Place `F → id` in cell: `M[F, id]`.

**Final LL(1) Parsing Table `M`:**

| Non-terminal | `id` | `+` | `*` | `(` | `)` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `E`  | `E → T E'` | | | `E → T E'` | | |
| `E'` | | `E' → + T E'` | | | `E' → ε` | `E' → ε` |
| `T`  | `T → F T'` | | | `T → F T'` | | |
| `T'` | | `T' → ε` | `T' → * F T'` | | `T' → ε` | `T' → ε` |
| `F`  | `F → id` | | | `F → ( E )` | | |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All cells have at most one production** — the grammar is confirmed to be **LL(1)**. No multiply-defined entries exist.

---

#### Step E — Write the Recursive Descent Procedures

Each non-terminal becomes a procedure. Note the **1-to-1 correspondence** between the procedures and the parsing table.

```
procedure E():
    case lookahead of
        id, ( :    // M[E, id] = M[E, (] = E → T E'
            T()
            E_prime()
        default :
            error()
    end case
end E

procedure E_prime():
    case lookahead of
        + :        // M[E', +] = E' → + T E'
            match('+')
            T()
            E_prime()
        ), $ :     // M[E', )] = M[E', $] = E' → ε
            ;       // do nothing (epsilon production)
        default :
            error()
    end case
end E_prime

procedure T():
    case lookahead of
        id, ( :    // M[T, id] = M[T, (] = T → F T'
            F()
            T_prime()
        default :
            error()
    end case
end T

procedure T_prime():
    case lookahead of
        * :        // M[T', *] = T' → * F T'
            match('*')
            F()
            T_prime()
        +, ), $ :  // M[T', +] = M[T', )] = M[T', $] = T' → ε
            ;       // do nothing
        default :
            error()
    end case
end T_prime

procedure F():
    case lookahead of
        id :       // M[F, id] = F → id
            match('id')
        ( :        // M[F, (] = F → ( E )
            match('(')
            E()
            match(')')
        default :
            error()
    end case
end F
```

The helper `match(token)` consumes the token from the input if it matches, otherwise signals an error.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Parsing the Input `id + id * id`

Using the table above, let us trace the moves of the parser. We use the format `Stack | Input | Action`.

**Initial:** `Stack = $E`, `Input = id + id * id $`

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$E` | `id + id * id $` | Output `E → T E'` |
| 2 | `$E' T` | `id + id * id $` | Output `T → F T'` |
| 3 | `$E' T' F` | `id + id * id $` | Output `F → id` |
| 4 | `$E' T' id` | `id + id * id $` | Match `id` |
| 5 | `$E' T'` | `+ id * id $` | Output `T' → ε` (since `+ ∈ FOLLOW(T')`) |
| 6 | `$E'` | `+ id * id $` | Output `E' → + T E'` |
| 7 | `$E' T +` | `+ id * id $` | Match `+` |
| 8 | `$E' T` | `id * id $` | Output `T → F T'` |
| 9 | `$E' T' F` | `id * id $` | Output `F → id` |
| 10 | `$E' T' id` | `id * id $` | Match `id` |
| 11 | `$E' T'` | `* id $` | Output `T' → * F T'` |
| 12 | `$E' T' F *` | `* id $` | Match `*` |
| 13 | `$E' T' F` | `id $` | Output `F → id` |
| 14 | `$E' T' id` | `id $` | Match `id` |
| 15 | `$E' T'` | `$` | Output `T' → ε` |
| 16 | `$E'` | `$` | Output `E' → ε` |
| 17 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✅ |

**Leftmost Derivation Produced:**
$$
E \Rightarrow T\,E' \Rightarrow F\,T'\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id}\,T'\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id}\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + T\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + F\,T'\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id}\,T'\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F\,T'\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}\,E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Parsing Strategies

| Property | Recursive Descent (Predictive) | Recursive Descent (Backtracking) | Non-Recursive Predictive (Table-Driven) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lookahead needed | 1 | Variable | 1 |
| Determinism | Deterministic | Non-deterministic | Deterministic |
| Time complexity | $O(n)$ | Exponential in worst case | $O(n)$ |
| Space complexity | $O(d)$ recursion stack | $O(d)$ + backtracking | $O(n)$ explicit stack |
| Implementation ease | **Easiest** — one procedure per non-terminal | Easy | Medium |
| Error detection | Good (panic mode easy to add) | Late | Good |
| Grammar class | LL(1) | Any | LL(1) |
| Used in practice | **Yes (ANTLR, Clang, etc.)** | Rarely | Yes (textbook teaching) |

---

### 3.4 Common Pitfalls and Their Mathematical Fixes

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall #1:** Forgetting to eliminate **indirect left recursion** (e.g., `A → B a`, `B → A b`). The elimination algorithm must be applied in **left-to-right order of non-terminals**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall #2:** When applying left recursion elimination to `A → Aα₁ ∣ Aα₂ ∣ β₁ ∣ β₂`, students often forget that **all** A-recursive alternatives go to `A'`, and **all** non-A-recursive alternatives become the basis of new `A`. The correct transformation:

$$
A \rightarrow \beta_1 A' \mid \beta_2 A'
$$
$$
A' \rightarrow \alpha_1 A' \mid \alpha_2 A' \mid \varepsilon
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Pitfall #3:** In the FOLLOW computation, students often **omit the ε-propagation step** (Rule 3b). Whenever `ε ∈ FIRST(β)`, the entire `FOLLOW(A)` must be added to `FOLLOW(B)`. This is a guaranteed 1-2 mark deduction in KTU valuation.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Parse Tree for `id + id * id`

```
                E
               / \
              T   E'
              |   |
              F  "+" T E'
              |      |  \
             id      F  T'
                     |   |
                    id   "*" F T'
                              |  \
                             id   ε
```

> Above: A 3-level deep parse tree showing the **top-down construction** order (root `E` first, leaves last). Notice how `E'` and `T'` chain through `ε` productions to terminate.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Flow of Recursive Descent Parser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start procedure E] --> checkE{lookahead in id or openparen}
    checkE -- Yes --> callT[Call procedure T]
    checkE -- No --> err1[Syntax Error: expected id or openparen]
    callT --> checkT{lookahead in id or openparen}
    checkT -- Yes --> callF[Call procedure F]
    checkT -- No --> err2[Syntax Error in T]
    callF --> checkF{lookahead}
    checkF -- id --> matchId[Consume id token advance ip]
    checkF -- openparen --> matchOpen[Consume openparen call E consume closeparen]
    checkF -- Other --> err3[Syntax Error in F]
    matchId --> retT[Return to T]
    matchOpen --> retTalt[Return to T]
    retT --> callTp[Call procedure Tprime]
    retTalt --> callTp
    callTp --> checkTp{lookahead}
    checkTp -- star --> matchStar[Consume star call F call Tprime recursively]
    checkTp -- plus or closeparen or dollar --> epsT[Apply Tprime to epsilon return]
    checkTp -- Other --> err4[Syntax Error in Tprime]
    matchStar --> retT2[Return to T]
    epsT --> retE[Return to E]
    retT2 --> retE
    retE --> callEp[Call procedure Eprime]
    callEp --> checkEp{lookahead}
    checkEp -- plus --> matchPlus[Consume plus call T call Eprime recursively]
    checkEp -- closeparen or dollar --> epsE[Apply Eprime to epsilon return]
    checkEp -- Other --> err5[Syntax Error in Eprime]
    matchPlus --> retE2[Return to E]
    epsE --> accept[Input fully matched ACCEPT]
    retE2 --> accept
    err1 --> errH[Invoke error recovery panic mode]
    err2 --> errH
    err3 --> errH
    err4 --> errH
    err5 --> errH
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FrontEnd["Compiler Front End"]
        srcA[Source Code] --> lexA[Lexical Analyzer]
        lexA --> tokA[Token Stream]
        tokA --> rdpA[Recursive Descent Parser]
    end
    rdpA --> ptA[Parse Tree or AST]
    ptA --> semA[Semantic Analyzer]
    semA --> irA[Intermediate Representation]
    subgraph ParserInternal["Recursive Descent Internal Modules"]
        rdpA --> procE[Procedure E]
        rdpA --> procEp[Procedure Eprime]
        rdpA --> procT[Procedure T]
        rdpA --> procTp[Procedure Tprime]
        rdpA --> procF[Procedure F]
        procE -. calls .-> procT
        procE -. calls .-> procEp
        procT -. calls .-> procF
        procT -. calls .-> procTp
        procEp -. calls .-> procT
        procTp -. calls .-> procF
        procTp -. calls .-> procTp
    end
    subgraph LookupTable["LL1 Parsing Table M"]
        tabA[Table M as 2D array indexed by nonterminal and terminal]
    end
    procE -. consults .-> tabA
    procEp -. consults .-> tabA
    procT -. consults .-> tabA
    procTp -. consults .-> tabA
    procF -. consults .-> tabA
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block: Grammar Preprocessing Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gA[Original Grammar G] --> lrA[Check for Left Recursion]
    lrA -- Yes --> elrA[Apply Left Recursion Elimination]
    lrA -- No --> lfA[Check for Common Prefixes]
    elrA --> lfA
    lfA -- Yes --> lfactA[Apply Left Factoring]
    lfA -- No --> firstA[Compute FIRST sets]
    lfactA --> firstA
    firstA --> followA[Compute FOLLOW sets]
    followA --> tableA[Construct LL1 Parsing Table M]
    tableA --> chkA{Check for multiply defined entries}
    chkA -- None --> rdpA[Grammar is LL1 Write Recursive Descent Procedures]
    chkA -- Multiply defined --> rejA[Grammar is NOT LL1 Needs more transformation or larger lookahead]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define Recursive Descent Parsing. State two conditions a grammar must satisfy to be parsed by recursive descent.**

**Model Answer:**

Recursive Descent Parsing is a top-down parsing technique in which a set of mutually recursive procedures is constructed, **one for each non-terminal** of the grammar. The parser begins execution by calling the procedure for the start symbol, and each procedure expands its non-terminal by either matching terminal symbols or calling the procedures of other non-terminals based on the current input lookahead.

The two mandatory conditions are:
1. **The grammar must be free of left recursion** (both direct and indirect), otherwise the parser will enter infinite recursion.
2. **The grammar must be left-factored** so that the lookahead token can uniquely determine which production to apply, ensuring the parsing table has no multiply-defined entries (i.e., the grammar is **LL(1)**).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the role of FIRST and FOLLOW sets in the construction of a predictive parser?**

**Model Answer:**

- **FIRST(α)** is the set of terminal symbols that can appear as the **leftmost symbol** in any string derived from the grammar symbol string `α`. It is used to decide which production to apply when the current input token is a terminal.

- **FOLLOW(A)** is the set of terminal symbols that can **immediately follow** the non-terminal `A` in any sentential form derived from the start symbol. `$` (end-of-input marker) is always in `FOLLOW(S)`.

- Their combined role in building the predictive parsing table `M[A, a]` is:
  - For production `A → α`: place it in `M[A, a]` for every `a ∈ FIRST(α)`.
  - If `ε ∈ FIRST(α)`: place it in `M[A, b]` for every `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)`.

They together guarantee that the parser makes a **unique, deterministic decision** at every step, given a 1-token lookahead.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2 Adapted]**
> Consider the following grammar `G`:
> ```
> S → A B
> A → a A | ε
> B → b B c | d
> ```
> **(a)** Eliminate left recursion if any, and find the **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** sets for all non-terminals. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Construct the **LL(1) parsing table** and write the **recursive descent procedures** for the grammar. Parse the input string `a b d c` and show the parse tree. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Left Recursion Elimination, FIRST and FOLLOW Computation**

**Step 1 — Identify Left Recursion:** The grammar has no left recursion. `A → a A ∣ ε` is right-recursive; `B → b B c ∣ d` is also not left-recursive. So no transformation is needed.

**Step 2 — Compute FIRST Sets:**

- `FIRST(A)` from `A → a A`: add `a`. From `A → ε`: add `ε`. So `FIRST(A) = { a, ε }`.
- `FIRST(B)` from `B → b B c`: add `b`. From `B → d`: add `d`. So `FIRST(B) = { b, d }`.
- `FIRST(S)` from `S → A B`: `FIRST(A) − {ε} = { a }`. Since `ε ∈ FIRST(A)`, also add `FIRST(B) = { b, d }`. So `FIRST(S) = { a, b, d }`.

**Step 3 — Compute FOLLOW Sets:**

- `FOLLOW(S) = { $ }`.
- From `S → A B`: For `A`, the symbol after is `B`, so `FIRST(B) − {ε} = { b, d } ⊆ FOLLOW(A)`. For `B`, the symbol after is end-of-production, so `FOLLOW(S) ⊆ FOLLOW(B)` → `FOLLOW(B) = { $ }`.
- For `A`: since `ε ∈ FIRST(B)` would be needed for `FOLLOW(S) ⊆ FOLLOW(A)`, but `ε ∉ FIRST(B)`. So `FOLLOW(A) = { b, d }`.
- For `B` (recheck via `B → b B c`): For inner `B`, the symbol after is `c`, so add `c` to `FOLLOW(B)`. So `FOLLOW(B) = { $, c }`.
- Recheck: from `S → A B`, for `A`, since `ε ∈ FIRST(B)` is false, no propagation. Final `FOLLOW(A) = { b, d }`.
- Recheck `FOLLOW(B)` via `B → b B c`: after inner `B` is `c`, so `c ∈ FOLLOW(B)`. No propagation needed.

**Summary Tables:**

| Non-terminal | FIRST | FOLLOW |
|---|---|---|
| `S` | `{ a, b, d }` | `{ $ }` |
| `A` | `{ a, ε }` | `{ b, d }` |
| `B` | `{ b, d }` | `{ $, c }` |

**(b) LL(1) Parsing Table and Procedures**

**Step 4 — Construct LL(1) Table:**

- `S → A B`: `FIRST(A B) = { a, b, d }`. Place in `M[S, a]`, `M[S, b]`, `M[S, d]`.
- `A → a A`: `FIRST(a A) = { a }`. Place in `M[A, a]`.
- `A → ε`: Use `FOLLOW(A) = { b, d }`. Place in `M[A, b]`, `M[A, d]`.
- `B → b B c`: `FIRST(b B c) = { b }`. Place in `M[B, b]`.
- `B → d`: `FIRST(d) = { d }`. Place in `M[B, d]`.

| Non-terminal | `a` | `b` | `c` | `d` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `S` | `S → A B` | `S → A B` | | `S → A B` | |
| `A` | `A → a A` | `A → ε` | | `A → ε` | |
| `B` | | `B → b B c` | | `B → d` | |

**Step 5 — Recursive Descent Procedures:**

```
procedure S():
    case lookahead of
        a, b, d : A(); B()
        default : error()
    end case

procedure A():
    case lookahead of
        a       : match('a'); A()
        b, d    : ;   // epsilon production
        default : error()
    end case

procedure B():
    case lookahead of
        b       : match('b'); B(); match('c')
        d       : match('d')
        default : error()
    end case
```

**Step 6 — Parse `a b d c`:**

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `$S` | `a b d c $` | `S → A B` |
| 2 | `$B A` | `a b d c $` | `A → a A` |
| 3 | `$B A a` | `a b d c $` | Match `a` |
| 4 | `$B A` | `b d c $` | `A → ε` (since `b ∈ FOLLOW(A)`) |
| 5 | `$B` | `b d c $` | `B → b B c` |
| 6 | `$c B b` | `b d c $` | Match `b` |
| 7 | `$c B` | `d c $` | `B → d` |
| 8 | `$c d` | `d c $` | Match `d` |
| 9 | `$c` | `c $` | Match `c` |
| 10 | `$` | `$` | **ACCEPT** ✅ |

**Parse Tree:**

```
                S
               / \
              A   B
              |   |
              a   b
                  |
                  B
                  |
                  d
              ε
```

(Where `A` derives `a` followed by `A → ε`, and `B` derives `b B c` where inner `B → d`.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Supplementary Adapted]**
> Consider the grammar:
> ```
> E → E + T | T
> T → T * F | F
> F → ( E ) | id
> ```
> **(a)** Eliminate left recursion, compute **FIRST** and **FOLLOW** for all non-terminals, and verify that the resulting grammar is **LL(1)**. **(7 marks)**
> **(b)** Construct the **LL(1) parsing table**, simulate parsing of the input **`id + id * id`**, and show the **sequence of moves**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step 1 — Eliminate Left Recursion:**

The grammar has immediate left recursion in `E` and `T`.

For `E → E + T ∣ T`:
- `E → T E'`
- `E' → + T E' ∣ ε`

For `T → T * F ∣ F`:
- `T → F T'`
- `T' → * F T' ∣ ε`

`F` remains: `F → ( E ) ∣ id`.

**Step 2 — FIRST Sets (from Section 3.1):**

| Non-terminal | FIRST |
|---|---|
| `E`  | `{ (, id }` |
| `E'` | `{ +, ε }` |
| `T`  | `{ (, id }` |
| `T'` | `{ *, ε }` |
| `F`  | `{ (, id }` |

**Step 3 — FOLLOW Sets (from Section 3.1):**

| Non-terminal | FOLLOW |
|---|---|
| `E`  | `{ $, ) }` |
| `E'` | `{ $, ) }` |
| `T`  | `{ +, $, ) }` |
| `T'` | `{ +, $, ) }` |
| `F`  | `{ *, +, $, ) }` |

**Step 4 — LL(1) Verification:**

- For `E'`: `FIRST(+ T E') ∩ FIRST(ε) = { + } ∩ { ε } = ∅`. ✅
- For `T'`: `FIRST(* F T') ∩ FIRST(ε) = ∅`. ✅
- For `F`: `FIRST(( E )) ∩ FIRST(id) = ∅`. ✅
- For `E` (single production): trivially OK. ✅
- For `T` (single production): trivially OK. ✅

**Grammar confirmed LL(1).**

**(b) Step 5 — Parsing Table (from Section 3.1, Step D):**

| Non-terminal | `id` | `+` | `*` | `(` | `)` | `$` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `E`  | `E → T E'` | | | `E → T E'` | | |
| `E'` | | `E' → + T E'` | | | `E' → ε` | `E' → ε` |
| `T`  | `T → F T'` | | | `T → F T'` | | |
| `T'` | | `T' → ε` | `T' → * F T'` | | `T' → ε` | `T' → ε` |
| `F`  | `F → id` | | | `F → ( E )` | | |

All cells have at most one entry → table is valid.

**Step 6 — Parse `id + id * id $`:**

(Already shown in Section 3.2 as a 17-step trace ending in ACCEPT.)

**Leftmost Derivation Output:**

$$
E \Rightarrow T E' \Rightarrow F T' E' \Rightarrow \text{id}\, T' E' \Rightarrow \text{id}\, E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + T E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + F T' E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id}\, T' E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * F T' E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}\, T' E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}\, E' \Rightarrow \text{id} + \text{id} * \text{id}
$$

**Parse Tree (also as in Section 4.1):**

```
                E
               / \
              T   E'
              |   |
              F  "+" T E'
              |      |  \
             id      F  T'
                     |   |
                    id   "*" F T'
                              |  \
                             id   ε
```

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on Recursive Descent Questions:**
>
> 1. **FIRST/FOLLOW Computation Errors (≈3 marks lost):** Students frequently forget the **ε-propagation rule** in FOLLOW — specifically, when `A → αBβ` and `ε ∈ FIRST(β)`, the entire `FOLLOW(A)` must be added to `FOLLOW(B)`. This single omission cascades into wrong tables and wrong parses.
>
> 2. **Left Recursion Elimination Slips (≈2 marks lost):** Confusing the direction of recursion (e.g., writing `E → E' T` instead of `E → T E'`). The new production's RHS must start with the **non-left-recursive alternative**, not the prime.
>
> 3. **Multiply-Defined Table Entries Ignored (≈2 marks lost):** If the table has two productions in one cell, the grammar is **not LL(1)** — students must state this explicitly and apply more factoring or larger lookahead. Silently using an ambiguous table costs full marks.
>
> 4. **Recursive Procedure Implementation (≈2 marks lost):** Forgetting that `ε` productions have **no code body** — they simply `return` to the caller. Writing a `match()` for epsilon is a common error.
>
> 5. **Stack Notation in Trace (≈1 mark lost):** Always show the **stack, input, and action** in three columns. Many students write only the production output and lose 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Misuse of `|` in markdown tables (presentation loss):** If your table is in a hand-written answer, ensure clean cell separation. If typed, never use the bare `|` symbol inside a cell — use LaTeX $\vert$ or $\mid$ as mandated by the KTU 2024 answer-rendering guidelines.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Rapid Revision Checklist for Recursive Descent Parsing:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Top-down parser with one recursive procedure per non-terminal.
- ✅ **Class:** LL(1) — Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1-token lookahead.
- ✅ **Three Pre-requisites:** No left recursion, no common prefixes (left-factored), LL(1) parsing table with no multiply-defined entries.
- ✅ **Preprocessing Pipeline:** Original Grammar → Left-Recursion Elimination → Left Factoring → FIRST sets → FOLLOW sets → LL(1) Table.
- ✅ **FIRST(α):** Terminals that begin strings derived from `α`. Includes `ε` if `α ⇒* ε`.
- ✅ **FOLLOW(A):** Terminals that can immediately follow `A` in any sentential form. Always contains `$` for start symbol.
- ✅ **Table Entry Rule:** `M[A, a] = A → α` if `a ∈ FIRST(α)`, or if `ε ∈ FIRST(α)` and `a ∈ FOLLOW(A)`.
- ✅ **Procedure Skeleton:** One `case` statement per non-terminal; one `case` arm per production; epsilon productions have **no** body.
- ✅ **Complexity:** Time `O(n)`, Space `O(d)` recursion depth.
- ✅ **Error Recovery:** Use `FOLLOW(A)` as synchronizing set; skip tokens until a synchronizing token is found.
- ✅ **Common Mistake:** Forgetting ε-propagation in FOLLOW; confusing recursion direction; ignoring multiply-defined table entries.
- ✅ **Real-World Tools:** ANTLR, JavaCC, Clang, Parsec, nom — all use recursive descent (or extensions of it).
- ✅ **Limitation:** Cannot parse left-recursive grammars directly; cannot handle all context-free grammars (only LL(1) subset).
- ✅ **Advantage:** Easiest parser to implement, debug, and extend; code mirrors the grammar.
- ✅ **Disadvantage:** Slower than table-driven parsers for very large grammars (due to procedure-call overhead); cannot handle ambiguous grammars.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always structure your answer in **three sections** — (1) preprocessing transformations, (2) FIRST/FOLLOW + table, (3) parser procedures + parse trace. This 3-part organization matches the KTU marking scheme and maximizes your score.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LL(1)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LL(1)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition of LL(1)

**LL(1) Parsing** is a deterministic, table-driven, **top-down parsing technique** used in syntax analysis phase of a compiler. The acronym is decoded as:

- The **first L** $\rightarrow$ **L**eft-to-right scanning of the input string.
- The **second L** $\rightarrow$ Produces a **L**eftmost derivation.
- The **(1)** $\rightarrow$ Uses **1** (one) symbol of lookahead to make parsing decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A grammar is said to be an **LL(1) grammar** if its **LL(1) parsing table** can be constructed without any multiple entries (i.e., without any conflicts) in any cell. A grammar that yields a conflict-free LL(1) parsing table is a *predictive parsing grammar*.

### Components of an LL(1) Parser

An LL(1) parser consists of four primary components working in tandem:

1. **Input Buffer** — Holds the source program tokens to be parsed, terminated by an end-marker **$**.
2. **Stack** — Contains a mix of grammar symbols (terminals and non-terminals), initialized with **$** and the **Start Symbol (S)**.
3. **Parsing Table** — A 2-D matrix $M[A, a]$ indexed by non-terminals (rows) and terminals (columns), where $a$ includes **$**.
4. **Output** — Holds the production rule applied (used later for semantic analysis / code generation).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of LL(1) as a Chef Following a Strict Recipe** 🍳
>
> Imagine you are a head chef who must prepare a dish by reading a recipe **left-to-right** (your "input buffer"). You build the dish step-by-step by **substituting ingredients in a leftmost manner** (always expanding the *earliest missing ingredient first* — that is, your leftmost non-terminal). Before you choose which ingredient to add, you are allowed to **peek exactly one ingredient ahead** on your shelf (your **1-symbol lookahead**).
>
> The **FIRST set** is like a list of "first ingredients" each recipe step can produce. The **FOLLOW set** is a list of "what ingredients might come *after* this step is finished." With this peek-ahead, the chef can **unambiguously decide** which recipe step to follow — this deterministic decision is the essence of LL(1) parsing!

### Key Standard Metrics

- **Lookahead count** $\rightarrow$ **1** symbol.
- **Time Complexity** of LL(1) parsing $\rightarrow$ $O(n)$ where $n$ is the length of the input.
- **Stack depth** at any time $\rightarrow$ At most the height of the parse tree.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Pre-Requisite Conditions for a Grammar to be LL(1)

For a Context-Free Grammar (CFG) $G$ to qualify as an LL(1) grammar, the following conditions must be strictly satisfied:

1. **The grammar must not be ambiguous.** (An ambiguous grammar will always produce conflicts.)
2. **The grammar must not be left-recursive.** Left recursion causes infinite recursion in a top-down parser.
3. **The grammar must be left-factored.** Common prefixes among alternatives must be factored out.
4. **For any non-terminal A with productions $A \rightarrow \alpha \mid \beta$**, the following sets must be **disjoint**:
   - $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ should not intersect.
   - If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ should not intersect, and vice-versa.

### 2.2 FIRST Set — Definition

**FIRST($\alpha$)** is the set of terminal symbols that begin the strings derivable from $\alpha$. If $\alpha \Rightarrow^* \epsilon$, then $\epsilon$ is also in FIRST($\alpha$).

**Recursive Rules to Compute FIRST(X):**

- **Rule 1:** If $X$ is a **terminal**, then $\text{FIRST}(X) = \{X\}$.
- **Rule 2:** If $X \rightarrow \epsilon$ is a production, then add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
- **Rule 3:** If $X \rightarrow Y_1 Y_2 \dots Y_k$ is a production, then:
  - Add $\text{FIRST}(Y_1) - \{\epsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
  - If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(Y_1)$, add $\text{FIRST}(Y_2) - \{\epsilon\}$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.
  - Continue until $Y_i$ for which $\epsilon \notin \text{FIRST}(Y_i)$ is reached.
  - If $\epsilon$ is in FIRST of all $Y_i$, then add $\epsilon$ to $\text{FIRST}(X)$.

### 2.3 FOLLOW Set — Definition

**FOLLOW(A)** is the set of terminals that can appear **immediately to the right** of the non-terminal $A$ in some sentential form. If $A$ can be the rightmost symbol, then **$** is in FOLLOW(A).

**Recursive Rules to Compute FOLLOW(A):**

- **Rule 1:** Place **$** in $\text{FOLLOW}(S)$, where $S$ is the start symbol.
- **Rule 2:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ is a production, then everything in $\text{FIRST}(\beta)$ except $\epsilon$ is placed in $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.
- **Rule 3:** If $A \rightarrow \alpha B$ is a production, or $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$ where $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta)$, then everything in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ is in $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$.

### 2.4 LL(1) Parsing Table Construction

For each production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ of the grammar:

- For every terminal $a$ in $\text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, a]$.
- If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$, then for every terminal $b$ in $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, b]$.
- If $\epsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\alpha)$ and $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$, add $A \rightarrow \alpha$ to $M[A, \$]$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If any cell $M[A, a]$ receives **more than one production**, the grammar is **NOT LL(1)**. The conflict is termed a **LL(1) conflict** (or "table clash").

### KTU Formula / Rule Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Set** | **Mathematical Rule** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|
| LL(1) | Left-to-right scan, Leftmost derivation, 1 lookahead | Defines parser class |
| $\text{FIRST}(X)$ | Set of terminals beginning strings from $X$ | Guides production choice |
| $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ | Set of terminals following $A$ in derivation | Handles $\epsilon$ productions |
| $M[A, a]$ | 2-D Parsing Table cell | Stores production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ |
| Conflict | $M[A, a]$ has $> 1$ entry | Grammar is **NOT** LL(1) |
| Stack Top $= a$ | Match, pop $a$, advance input | Terminal match step |
| Stack Top $= A$ | Consult $M[A, a]$, apply production | Non-terminal expand step |
| Stack Top $= \$$, Input $= \$$ | **ACCEPT** and halt | Successful parse |

### Real-World Engineering Utility

LL(1) parsing is the **backbone of recursive-descent compilers** and is heavily used in:

- **Compiler Front-Ends** for educational languages (e.g., early C compilers).
- **Domain-Specific Languages (DSLs)** like SQL parsers and JSON validators.
- **Configuration File Parsers** in tools like Apache, Nginx, and YAML processors.
- **Lightweight scripting language interpreters** where grammar simplicity is a design priority.
- **Static analyzers and linters** (e.g., ESLint, Clang-Tidy) that need fast deterministic parsing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: FULL LL(1) Construction for a Standard Arithmetic Grammar

**Given Grammar $G$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &\rightarrow T\,E' \\
E' &\rightarrow +\,T\,E' \;\mid\; \epsilon \\
T &\rightarrow F\,T' \\
T' &\rightarrow *\,F\,T' \;\mid\; \epsilon \\
F &\rightarrow (\,E\,) \;\mid\; \mathbf{id}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here, the non-terminals are $\{E, E', T, T', F\}$ and terminals are $\{+, *, (, ), \mathbf{id}\}$.

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of FIRST Sets

We apply the recursive rules for FIRST iteratively until no new symbol can be added.

**Step 1 — Compute FIRST(F):**
$F$ has productions $F \rightarrow (E)$ and $F \rightarrow \mathbf{id}$.

- For $F \rightarrow (E)$: The first symbol is the terminal '**(**'. So add '**(**' to FIRST(F).
- For $F \rightarrow \mathbf{id}$: The first symbol is the terminal **id**. So add **id** to FIRST(F).

$$
\therefore \text{FIRST}(F) = \{\, (\,,\; \mathbf{id} \,\}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute FIRST(T):**
$T$ has production $T \rightarrow F\,T'$.
