> 4. **Forgetting the `$` bottom-of-stack marker:** Every valid configuration begins with `$0` (state 0) on the stack. Omitting this in the initial configuration is a common error. **Penalty: 0.5 mark.**
> 5. **Not distinguishing handle from phrase:** A handle is *the* leftmost substring that can be reduced to derive the previous right-sentential form — not just *any* substring matching a RHS. **Penalty: 1-2 marks.**
> 6. **Mishandling the accept state:** The parser accepts only when the stack is `$S` and input is `$` — not just when input is consumed. **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Shift-Reduce Parser (Module 2)**

- [ ] **Shift-reduce parser** is a **bottom-up** parsing technique that uses a **stack + input buffer + parsing table** model.
- [ ] The four canonical actions are: **SHIFT, REDUCE, ACCEPT, ERROR** (in this priority order for typical tables).
- [ ] **Stack discipline:** Stack always holds alternating grammar symbols and parser states, e.g., `$0 a 5 S 3 ...`.
- [ ] **Initial configuration:** Stack = `0`, Input = `w$` where `w` is the token stream.
- [ ] **Acceptance:** Stack = `$S 1` (where `1` is the state containing `[S' → S·]`) and Input = `$`.
- [ ] **A handle** is a substring that matches the RHS of a production and represents one reverse step of a rightmost derivation.
- [ ] **Handle pruning** = the process of repeatedly finding and reducing handles, which is the heart of bottom-up parsing.
- [ ] **Viable prefixes** are prefixes of right-sentential forms that can legally appear on the stack.
- [ ] The set of viable prefixes forms a **regular language**, recognized by a **DFA built from LR(0) item sets**.
- [ ] **LR(0) items** are productions with a dot (·) marking a position in the RHS; each item represents a parsing state.
- [ ] **CLOSURE** operation adds items for non-terminals immediately after the dot.
- [ ] **GOTO** operation moves the dot past a symbol and closes the result.
- [ ] **Conflicts** make a grammar non-LR:
  - **Shift-Reduce Conflict:** same (state, terminal) cell needs both shift and reduce.
  - **Reduce-Reduce Conflict:** same (state, terminal) cell needs two different reduces.
- [ ] **Time complexity of shift-reduce parsing:** $O(n)$ for input of length $n$.
- [ ] **Parser table size:** proportional to (number of LR(0) states) × (number of grammar symbols + terminals).
- [ ] **Real-world usage:** GCC, Clang, YACC, Bison — all use LALR(1) shift-reduce parsers generated from yacc-style grammars.
- [ ] **Common memory aid:** "S.H.A.R.E." — **S**tack, **H**andle, **A**ctions (4), **R**ightmost derivation (reverse), **E**ngineering metric $O(n)$.
- [ ] **FOLLOW sets** are essential for SLR(1) table construction to decide when to reduce.
- [ ] **Stack size is bounded by input length** — practical for production compilers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LR(0) Items and LR(0) Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** LR(0) Items and LR(0) Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of an LR(0) Item

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LR(0) Item (KTU 2024 Definition):**
> An **LR(0) item** (also called a *configuration*) is a production of the grammar with a special dot (·) inserted at some position on the right-hand side. It represents a partial recognition of the right-hand side of a production during bottom-up parsing.

Formally, if $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$ is a production, then the items derived from it are:

$$A \rightarrow \cdot X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$$
$$A \rightarrow X_1 \cdot X_2 \dots X_n$$
$$\vdots$$
$$A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_{n-1} \cdot X_n$$
$$A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n \cdot$$

The total number of LR(0) items generated from a single production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ (where $\alpha$ has length $n$) is exactly $\mathbf{n+1}$.

## 1.2 The Augmented Grammar

To uniquely identify the parser's accept state, we **augment** the given grammar $G$ with a new start symbol $S'$. If $S$ is the original start symbol, the augmented grammar $G'$ contains the new production:

$$S' \rightarrow S$$

The item $S' \rightarrow S\cdot$ is treated as the unique **accepting** item. This guarantees exactly one accepting state in the DFA.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why augment?** Without augmentation, a grammar might have multiple productions for the start symbol, leading to ambiguity in the accept state. The augmented start symbol ensures the parser has one clear point of "grammar acceptance."

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Subway Station Model

Imagine the parser as a **traveler on a subway line**:
- The **productions** are subway lines (e.g., Line-A goes from station `S` to `CC`).
- The **dot (·)** is the **current position** of the traveler on the line.
- An **item set (state)** is a *station* — a snapshot of where the traveler could plausibly be, given what they have seen so far.
- The **GOTO transition** is moving one step along the line to the next station.

When the dot is at the far right (e.g., $C \rightarrow d\cdot$), the traveler has reached the end of the line and is ready to "announce" that the line is complete (a **reduce action**). When the dot is at the start (e.g., $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$), the traveler is waiting at the platform to board a train (a **shift action**).

## 1.4 The Two Fundamental Operations

Two operations drive the entire LR(0) automaton construction:

| Operation | Role in Parsing | When Applied |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------|
| **CLOSURE(I)** | Adds all items that could be validly "in progress" given the current items | Computes a *complete* state from a kernel |
| **GOTO(I, X)** | Advances the dot past symbol $X$, computing the next state | Models a shift or partial reduction |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Recall:** CLOSURE "completes" a state, while GOTO "advances" it. Together they form the **LR(0) Canonical Collection of Items**, which is the deterministic finite automaton (DFA) that drives the parser.

## 1.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

> **Mapped CO:** CO2 — *Apply shift-reduce parsing techniques (SLR, CLR, LALR) to construct parsers for context-free grammars.*
> **Bloom's Level:** L2 (Understand) → L3 (Apply)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CLOSURE Operation

The closure operation captures the intuition that, while the parser is *about to derive* a non-terminal $A$ (because the dot is just before $A$), it should simultaneously be ready to derive whatever $A$ can produce in the next step.

### 2.1.1 Formal Algorithm

**Input:** A set of items $I$ for grammar $G'$.
**Output:** CLOSURE($I$) — the closure of $I$.

```
function CLOSURE(I):
    repeat
        for each item [A → α·Bβ] in I do
            for each production B → γ in G' do
                if [B → ·γ] is NOT in I then
                    add [B → ·γ] to I
                end if
            end for
        end for
    until no new items can be added to I
    return I
```

### 2.1.2 The "Why" Behind Closure

If the parser is at a state where it expects to see a $B$ (i.e., the dot is before $B$), then $B$ could be the start of *any* of $B$'s productions. Therefore, the parser must be *ready* to derive any of those. This is the kernel→closure relationship:

$$[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta] \;\Longrightarrow\; [B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma] \quad \text{for every production } B \rightarrow \gamma$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A common mistake is to apply closure only *once*. It must be applied repeatedly (the `repeat…until` loop) because newly added items can themselves trigger further additions. This is a **frequent exam trap**.

## 2.2 The GOTO Operation

The GOTO operation models the parser consuming a symbol (terminal or non-terminal) and transitioning to a new state.

### 2.2.1 Formal Algorithm

**Input:** A set of items $I$ and a grammar symbol $X$.
**Output:** GOTO($I$, $X$).

```
function GOTO(I, X):
    J = empty set
    for each item [A → α·Xβ] in I do
        add [A → αX·β] to J
    end for
    return CLOSURE(J)
```

### 2.2.2 The "How" Behind GOTO

- **Step 1 — Collect:** Find all items where the dot is *immediately before* symbol $X$. Move the dot past $X$ in each.
- **Step 2 — Close:** Apply CLOSURE to the new set so that the resulting state is *complete* (i.e., it accounts for all possible future derivations).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** GOTO on a terminal = a **shift transition**. GOTO on a non-terminal = a **goto transition** (i.e., the parser reduced to a non-terminal and jumps to the corresponding state). This distinction is critical for filling the parsing action table vs. the goto table.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---------|----------------|-------|
| 1 | Number of items per production | If $\vert\alpha\vert = n$, items = $n+1$ | For production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ |
| 2 | Augmented start production | $S' \rightarrow S$ | Always added exactly once |
| 3 | CLOSURE trigger | Item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta]$ adds $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$ | For every production $B \rightarrow \gamma$ |
| 4 | GOTO trigger | Item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta]$ becomes $[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta]$ | $X$ is any grammar symbol |
| 5 | Reduce condition | Item has dot at the end: $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ | Apply production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ |
| 6 | Accept condition | Item $S' \rightarrow S\cdot$ and look-ahead = $\$$ | Triggers the unique accept action |
| 7 | Number of symbols to pop | $\vert \alpha \vert$ (length of RHS) | When reducing by $A \rightarrow \alpha$ |
| 8 | SLR reduce terminals | FOLLOW($A$) | Used only in SLR(1), not LR(0) |
| 9 | LR(0) reduce on | **ALL** terminal symbols | Pure LR(0) has no look-ahead |
| 10 | Conflict in LR(0) | shift–reduce OR reduce–reduce | Makes grammar not LR(0) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL DISTINCTION (Frequently Tested):** In **LR(0)**, the parser reduces by production $A \rightarrow \alpha$ on **every** terminal symbol (and $\$$) when the state contains $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$. It is only in **SLR(1)** that the reduction is restricted to FOLLOW($A$). This is one of the most common KTU exam traps.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

LR(0) parsing, while theoretically the simplest of the LR family, is the **conceptual foundation** for industrial-strength parsers like:
- **YACC / Bison** (used in GCC, the GNU C compiler)
- **ANTLR** (used in many Java/Python tools)
- **LLVM's table generator**

In practice, the LR(0) automaton skeleton is augmented with **look-ahead** to become SLR(1), CLR(1), or LALR(1) — all of which build on the same DFA structure covered in this module.

## 2.5 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Mapping

> **Mapped CO:** CO2 — *Apply shift-reduce parsing techniques (LR(0), SLR) to construct parsers for context-free grammars.*
> **Bloom's Level:** L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyze)

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Worked Example (KTU Standard Grammar)

Consider the following augmented grammar, which is the canonical example appearing in standard compiler textbooks (Aho, Sethi, Ullman) and frequently reused in KTU question papers:

$$S' \rightarrow S$$
$$S \rightarrow C\,C$$
$$C \rightarrow c\,C \mid d$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Grammar Setup:** Terminals = $\{c, d, \$\}$, Non-terminals = $\{S', S, C\}$, Start symbol = $S'$.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Generate All LR(0) Items

For each production, we insert the dot at every possible position:

| # | Production | Generated Items |
|---|------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $S' \rightarrow S$ | $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$ ; $S' \rightarrow S\cdot$ |
| 2 | $S \rightarrow CC$ | $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$ ; $S \rightarrow C \cdot C$ ; $S \rightarrow CC\cdot$ |
| 3 | $C \rightarrow cC$ | $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$ ; $C \rightarrow c \cdot C$ ; $C \rightarrow cC \cdot$ |
| 4 | $C \rightarrow d$ | $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ ; $C \rightarrow d\cdot$ |

**Total items generated:** $2 + 3 + 3 + 2 = \mathbf{10}$ items.

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Construct the Initial State $I_0$

We start with the kernel item $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$ and compute its closure.

**Iteration 1:**
- Item: $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$ — dot is before $S$, a non-terminal.
- For every production $S \rightarrow \gamma$, add $S \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ to $I_0$.
- New item added: $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$.

**Iteration 2:**
- Item: $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$ — dot is before $C$, a non-terminal.
- For every production $C \rightarrow \gamma$, add $C \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ to $I_0$.
- New items added: $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$ and $C \rightarrow \cdot d$.

**Iteration 3:**
- Items: $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$ and $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ — dot is before terminals $c$ and $d$.
- No new productions to add. **Closure complete.**

$$
I_0 = \begin{cases}
S' \rightarrow \cdot S \\
S \rightarrow \cdot CC \\
C \rightarrow \cdot cC \\
C \rightarrow \cdot d
\end{cases}
$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Compute GOTO Transitions from $I_0$

**GOTO($I_0$, $S$):** Find items with dot before $S$ in $I_0$: only $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$. Advance the dot:

$$I_1 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{S' \rightarrow S \cdot\}) = \{S' \rightarrow S \cdot\}$$

**GOTO($I_0$, $C$):** Find items with dot before $C$ in $I_0$: only $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$. Advance:

$$I_2 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{S \rightarrow C \cdot C\})$$

Applying closure — dot is now before $C$, so add $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$ and $C \rightarrow \cdot d$:

$$I_2 = \begin{cases}
S \rightarrow C \cdot C \\
C \rightarrow \cdot cC \\
C \rightarrow \cdot d
\end{cases}$$

**GOTO($I_0$, $c$):** Find items with dot before $c$ in $I_0$: only $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$. Advance:

$$I_3 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{C \rightarrow c \cdot C\})$$

Applying closure — dot is now before $C$, so add its productions:

$$I_3 = \begin{cases}
C \rightarrow c \cdot C \\
C \rightarrow \cdot cC \\
C \rightarrow \cdot d
\end{cases}$$

**GOTO($I_0$, $d$):** Find items with dot before $d$ in $I_0$: only $C \rightarrow \cdot d$. Advance:

$$I_4 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{C \rightarrow d \cdot\}) = \{C \rightarrow d \cdot\}$$

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Continue Construction: $I_2$ and $I_3$

**GOTO($I_2$, $C$):** Dot before $C$ in $S \rightarrow C \cdot C$ → advance:

$$I_5 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{S \rightarrow CC \cdot\}) = \{S \rightarrow CC \cdot\}$$

**GOTO($I_2$, $c$):** Dot before $c$ in $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$ → same as $I_3$.

**GOTO($I_2$, $d$):** Dot before $d$ in $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ → same as $I_4$.

**GOTO($I_3$, $C$):** Dot before $C$ in $C \rightarrow c \cdot C$ → advance:

$$I_6 = \text{CLOSURE}(\{C \rightarrow cC \cdot\}) = \{C \rightarrow cC \cdot\}$$

**GOTO($I_3$, $c$):** Same as $I_3$. **GOTO($I_3$, $d$):** Same as $I_4$.

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Final Canonical Collection

The complete set of 7 states is:

| State | Items |
|-------|-------|
| $I_0$ | $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$, $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$, $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$, $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ |
| $I_1$ | $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ |
| $I_2$ | $S \rightarrow C \cdot C$, $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$, $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ |
| $I_3$ | $C \rightarrow c \cdot C$, $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$, $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ |
| $I_4$ | $C \rightarrow d \cdot$ |
| $I_5$ | $S \rightarrow CC \cdot$ |
| $I_6$ | $C \rightarrow cC \cdot$ |

### 3.1.6 Step 6 — Build the LR(0) Parsing Table

We number the productions for reference:
- **(1)** $S \rightarrow CC$
- **(2)** $C \rightarrow cC$
- **(3)** $C \rightarrow d$

**Filling the ACTION table (left side: terminals $c$, $d$, $\$$):**

| State | $c$ | $d$ | $\$$ | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----|------|--------|
| 0 | s3 | s4 | — | $I_0$ shifts $c \to I_3$, $d \to I_4$ |
| 1 | — | — | **acc** | $I_1$ has $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ (accept) |
| 2 | s3 | s4 | — | $I_2$ shifts $c \to I_3$, $d \to I_4$ |
| 3 | s3 | s4 | — | $I_3$ shifts $c \to I_3$, $d \to I_4$ |
| 4 | **r3** | **r3** | **r3** | $I_4$ has $C \rightarrow d \cdot$ → reduce by (3) on **ALL** terminals |
| 5 | **r1** | **r1** | **r1** | $I_5$ has $S \rightarrow CC \cdot$ → reduce by (1) on **ALL** terminals |
| 6 | **r2** | **r2** | **r2** | $I_6$ has $C \rightarrow cC \cdot$ → reduce by (2) on **ALL** terminals |

**Filling the GOTO table (right side: non-terminals $S$, $C$):**

| State | $S$ | $C$ |
|-------|-----|-----|
| 0 | 1 | 2 |
| 1 | — | — |
| 2 | — | 5 |
| 3 | — | 6 |
| 4 | — | — |
| 5 | — | — |
| 6 | — | — |

### 3.1.7 Step 7 — Trace the Parsing of Input "ccd"

| Step | Stack | Input | Action | Justification |
|------|-------|-------|--------|---------------|
| 0 | `0` | `ccd$` | Shift to 3 | State 0, on $c$ → s3 |
| 1 | `0 c 3` | `cd$` | Shift to 3 | State 3, on $c$ → s3 |
| 2 | `0 c 3 c 3` | `d$` | Shift to 4 | State 3, on $d$ → s4 |
| 3 | `0 c 3 c 3 d 4` | `$` | Reduce by (3) $C \rightarrow d$ | State 4, on $\$$ → r3 |
| 4 | `0 c 3 c 3 C 6` | `$` | Reduce by (2) $C \rightarrow cC$ | State 6, on $\$$ → r2 |
| 5 | `0 c 3 C 6` | `$` | Reduce by (2) $C \rightarrow cC$ | State 6, on $\$$ → r2 |
| 6 | `0 C 2` | `$` | Reduce by (1) $S \rightarrow CC$ | State 5, on $\$$ → r1 |
| 7 | `0 S 1` | `$` | **Accept** | State 1, on $\$$ → acc |

> [!TIP]
> **Stack Convention:** The stack alternates between *symbols* and *states*. Each reduction pops $2 \cdot \vert\alpha\vert$ entries (symbol + state pairs), then pushes the non-terminal followed by GOTO(top_state, non-terminal).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Reduction Logic

The number of symbols popped during a reduction is governed by the formula:

$$\text{Pop count} = 2 \cdot \vert \alpha \vert$$

where $\alpha$ is the right-hand side of the production. After popping, exactly one non-terminal is pushed, followed by the new state obtained from GOTO.

| Production | $\vert \alpha \vert$ | Pop | Push | Production Number |
|------------|----------|-----|------|-------------------|
| $S \rightarrow CC$ | 2 | 4 entries | $S$ then new state | (1) |
| $C \rightarrow cC$ | 2 | 4 entries | $C$ then new state | (2) |
| $C \rightarrow d$ | 1 | 2 entries | $C$ then new state | (3) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why multiply by 2?** Each grammar symbol on the stack has a corresponding state *next to it* (the state reached after recognizing that symbol). Popping $\vert\alpha\vert$ symbols therefore also pops $\vert\alpha\vert$ states → total $2 \cdot \vert\alpha\vert$ entries.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LR(0) DFA — The Big Picture

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete LR(0) automaton for our example grammar, including all 7 states and their transitions.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    direction LR
    [*] --> state0

    state0: I0\nKernel: S'->.S, S->.CC\nClosure: C->.cC, C->.d
    state1: I1\nS'->S. (ACCEPT)
    state2: I2\nKernel: S->C.C\nClosure: C->.cC, C->.d
    state3: I3\nKernel: C->c.C\nClosure: C->.cC, C->.d
    state4: I4\nC->d. (REDUCE by C->d)
    state5: I5\nS->CC. (REDUCE by S->CC)
    state6: I6\nC->cC. (REDUCE by C->cC)

    state0 --> state1 : S (goto)
    state0 --> state2 : C (goto)
    state0 --> state3 : c (shift)
    state0 --> state4 : d (shift)

    state2 --> state5 : C (goto)
    state2 --> state3 : c (shift)
    state2 --> state4 : d (shift)

    state3 --> state6 : C (goto)
    state3 --> state3 : c (shift)
    state3 --> state4 : d (shift)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows are GOTO transitions. Labels on arrows indicate *what symbol caused* the transition. States that are circled in red (in a printed version) indicate *reduce-only* states.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LR(0) Parser

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Tokens] --> B[Token Buffer / Lexical Interface]
    B --> C{Parsing Engine\nState Machine}
    C -->|Shift Action| D[Push symbol+state\nto Stack]
    C -->|Reduce Action| E[Pop 2*alpha entries\nApply production]
    C -->|Accept| F[Output: Valid String]
    C -->|Error| G[Output: Syntax Error]

    H[LR Parsing Table\nACTION + GOTO] --> C

    E --> I[Push LHS non-terminal]
    I --> J[Lookup GOTO for new state]
    J --> D

    D --> K[Advance input pointer]
    K --> C

    style A fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#333
    style H fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#333
    style F fill:#D4EDDA,stroke:#333
    style G fill:#F8D7DA,stroke:#333
    style C fill:#FFE9B0,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Parsing Table Visual Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Table[LR 0 Parsing Table]
        direction TB
        subgraph ACTION[ACTION Table]
            A1[State | c | d | $]
            A2[0 | s3 | s4 | -]
            A3[1 | - | - | acc]
            A4[4 | r3 | r3 | r3]
            A5[5 | r1 | r1 | r1]
            A6[6 | r2 | r2 | r2]
        end
        subgraph GOTO[GOTO Table]
            G1[State | S | C]
            G2[0 | 1 | 2]
            G3[2 | - | 5]
            G4[3 | - | 6]
        end
    end

    Table --> ACTION
    Table --> GOTO
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When drawing the table in an exam, *always separate* the ACTION (terminals) and GOTO (non-terminals) sections. A common mistake is mixing them, which leads to deduction of marks.

## 4.4 Conceptual Map of LR(0) Construction Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Augment Grammar G to G'] --> P2[List All LR 0 Items]
    P2 --> P3[Compute CLOSURE of I0]
    P3 --> P4[Compute GOTO transitions]
    P4 --> P5{New states discovered?}
    P5 -->|Yes| P4
    P5 -->|No| P6[Build Parsing Tables]
    P6 --> P7[Simulate Parsing on Input]

    style P1 fill:#D4EDDA
    style P6 fill:#FFE9B0
    style P7 fill:#E8F4FD
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an LR(0) item. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> An **LR(0) item** is a production of a context-free grammar with a distinguished dot (·) inserted somewhere on the right-hand side. If a production is $A \rightarrow X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$, then an LR(0) item is a pair $(A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta)$ where $\alpha \beta = X_1 X_2 \dots X_n$.
>
> The dot represents the position up to which the parser has recognized input. If the production has RHS of length $n$, then there are exactly $n+1$ distinct LR(0) items derivable from it. **[3 marks]**

### Question 2: What is the role of the augmented production in LR(0) parsing? [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> The augmented production $S' \rightarrow S$ is added to the original grammar to **create a unique accepting item**, namely $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$. **[1 mark]**
>
> This guarantees that:
> - The parser has a single, unambiguous signal of successful acceptance. **[1 mark]**
> - The state containing $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ acts as the single final/accepting state of the LR(0) DFA. **[1 mark]**
>
> Without augmentation, a grammar with multiple start-symbol productions (e.g., $S \rightarrow a$ and $S \rightarrow b$) would have no unique accept state, breaking the deterministic structure of the parser.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Option 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Question:** *Consider the augmented grammar:*
$$S' \rightarrow S, \quad S \rightarrow CC, \quad C \rightarrow cC \mid d$$
*(a)* Construct the canonical collection of LR(0) items for the grammar. Show all CLOSURE and GOTO computations explicitly. **[7 marks]**
*(b)* Build the LR(0) parsing table. State whether the grammar is LR(0). Justify. **[7 marks]**

> **Solution:**

### Part (a) Solution — Collection of LR(0) Items [7 marks]

**Step 1: List all items (1 mark)**

From the four productions $S' \rightarrow S$, $S \rightarrow CC$, $C \rightarrow cC$, $C \rightarrow d$, we derive **10 items** (as listed in §3.1.1).

**Step 2: Construct $I_0$ (1 mark)**

Starting kernel: $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$. Applying CLOSURE repeatedly:
- $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$ → add $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$ (dot before non-terminal $S$)
- $S \rightarrow \cdot CC$ → add $C \rightarrow \cdot cC$ and $C \rightarrow \cdot d$ (dot before $C$)
- No further additions possible.

$$I_0 = \{S' \rightarrow \cdot S,\; S \rightarrow \cdot CC,\; C \rightarrow \cdot cC,\; C \rightarrow \cdot d\}$$

**Step 3: Compute GOTO from $I_0$ (2 marks)**

- **GOTO($I_0$, $S$):** $I_1 = \{S' \rightarrow S \cdot\}$
- **GOTO($I_0$, $C$):** $I_2 = \{S \rightarrow C \cdot C,\; C \rightarrow \cdot cC,\; C \rightarrow \cdot d\}$
- **GOTO($I_0$, $c$):** $I_3 = \{C \rightarrow c \cdot C,\; C \rightarrow \cdot cC,\; C \rightarrow \cdot d\}$
- **GOTO($I_0$, $d$):** $I_4 = \{C \rightarrow d \cdot\}$

**Step 4: Continue construction until fixed point (3 marks)**

- **GOTO($I_2$, $C$):** $I_5 = \{S \rightarrow CC \cdot\}$
- **GOTO($I_2$, $c$):** $I_3$ (already exists)
- **GOTO($I_2$, $d$):** $I_4$ (already exists)
- **GOTO($I_3$, $C$):** $I_6 = \{C \rightarrow cC \cdot\}$
- **GOTO($I_3$, $c$):** $I_3$ (already exists)
- **GOTO($I_3$, $d$):** $I_4$ (already exists)

All other GOTO transitions yield empty sets. The canonical collection has 7 states: $I_0$ through $I_6$.

### Part (b) Solution — Parsing Table & LR(0) Status [7 marks]

**Step 1: Number productions (1 mark)**
- (1) $S \rightarrow CC$
- (2) $C \rightarrow cC$
- (3) $C \rightarrow d$

**Step 2: Fill ACTION table (3 marks)** — Refer to §3.1.6 for the complete table.

**Step 3: Fill GOTO table (1 mark)** — Refer to §3.1.6 for the complete table.

**Step 4: Check for conflicts (2 marks)**

We inspect each state for shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts:
- $I_1$: $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ → no conflict (accept).
- $I_4$: $C \rightarrow d \cdot$ (single reduce) → no conflict.
- $I_5$: $S \rightarrow CC \cdot$ (single reduce) → no conflict.
- $I_6$: $C \rightarrow cC \cdot$ (single reduce) → no conflict.
- $I_0, I_2, I_3$: Only **shift** items, no reduce items present → no conflict.

**Conclusion:** The grammar is **LR(0)** because no state contains a shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflict.

---

### Question B (Option 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Question:** *For the augmented grammar:*
$$S' \rightarrow S, \quad S \rightarrow aA \mid b, \quad A \rightarrow cAd \mid c$$
*(a)* Construct the canonical collection of LR(0) items, showing the DFA. **[7 marks]**
*(b)* Build the LR(0) parsing table and demonstrate parsing of the input string `b`. **[7 marks]**

> **Solution:**

### Part (a) Solution — Collection of LR(0) Items [7 marks]

**Step 1: Generate items (1 mark)**

| Production | Items |
|------------|-------|
| $S' \rightarrow S$ | $S' \rightarrow \cdot S$ ; $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ |
| $S \rightarrow aA$ | $S \rightarrow \cdot aA$ ; $S \rightarrow a \cdot A$ ; $S \rightarrow aA \cdot$ |
| $S \rightarrow b$ | $S \rightarrow \cdot b$ ; $S \rightarrow b \cdot$ |
| $A \rightarrow cAd$ | $A \rightarrow \cdot cAd$ ; $A \rightarrow c \cdot Ad$ ; $A \rightarrow cA \cdot d$ ; $A \rightarrow cAd \cdot$ |
| $A \rightarrow c$ | $A \rightarrow \cdot c$ ; $A \rightarrow c \cdot$ |

**Step 2: Construct $I_0$ (1 mark)**

$$I_0 = \{S' \rightarrow \cdot S,\; S \rightarrow \cdot aA,\; S \rightarrow \cdot b\}$$

(No non-terminals before the dot besides $S$, and $S$ has productions $aA$ and $b$ — no further items added.)

**Step 3: Compute GOTO transitions (5 marks)**

- **GOTO($I_0$, $S$) = $I_1$**: $\{S' \rightarrow S \cdot\}$
- **GOTO($I_0$, $a$) = $I_2$**: $\{S \rightarrow a \cdot A\}$
  - Closure: dot before $A$ → add $A \rightarrow \cdot cAd$, $A \rightarrow \cdot c$.
  - $I_2 = \{S \rightarrow a \cdot A,\; A \rightarrow \cdot cAd,\; A \rightarrow \cdot c\}$
- **GOTO($I_0$, $b$) = $I_3$**: $\{S \rightarrow b \cdot\}$
- **GOTO($I_2$, $A$) = $I_4$**: $\{S \rightarrow aA \cdot\}$
- **GOTO($I_2$, $c$) = $I_5$**:
  - From $A \rightarrow \cdot cAd$ → $A \rightarrow c \cdot Ad$
  - From $A \rightarrow \cdot c$ → $A \rightarrow c \cdot$
  - Closure: dot before $A$ → add $A \rightarrow \cdot cAd$, $A \rightarrow \cdot c$
  - $I_5 = \{A \rightarrow c \cdot Ad,\; A \rightarrow c \cdot,\; A \rightarrow \cdot cAd,\; A \rightarrow \cdot c\}$
- **GOTO($I_5$, $A$) = $I_6$**: $\{A \rightarrow cA \cdot d\}$
  - Closure: no new additions.
  - $I_6 = \{A \rightarrow cA \cdot d\}$
- **GOTO($I_5$, $c$) = $I_5$** (self-loop)
- **GOTO($I_6$, $d$) = $I_7$**: $\{A \rightarrow cAd \cdot\}$

**Total states: 8** ($I_0$ through $I_7$).

### Part (b) Solution — Parsing Table & Trace [7 marks]

**Step 1: Number productions (1 mark)**
- (1) $S \rightarrow aA$
- (2) $S \rightarrow b$
- (3) $A \rightarrow cAd$
- (4) $A \rightarrow c$

**Step 2: Build the table (3 marks)**

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ | $\$$ | $S$ | $A$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|-----|-----|
| 0 | s2 | s3 | | | | 1 | |
| 1 | | | | | acc | | |
| 2 | | | s5 | | | | 4 |
| 3 | r2 | r2 | r2 | r2 | r2 | | |
| 4 | r1 | r1 | r1 | r1 | r1 | | |
| 5 | | | s5 | | | | 6 |
| 6 | | | | s7 | | | |
| 7 | r3 | r3 | r3 | r3 | r3 | | |

**Step 3: Parse input `b` (3 marks)**

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `0` | `b$` | Shift to 3 |
| 2 | `0 b 3` | `$` | Reduce by (2): $S \rightarrow b$ |
| 3 | `0 S 1` | `$` | **Accept** |

> **Conclusion:** The grammar is **LR(0)** (no conflicts in any state).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **State numbering must be consistent** — A single mismatch between your DFA diagram and your table costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Closure must be repeated** — students who apply closure only once lose **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Reduce action is on ALL terminals in LR(0)** — confusing this with SLR(1) (where it's restricted to FOLLOW) is a **3-mark deduction**.
> 4. **Pop count must be $2 \cdot \vert\alpha\vert$**, not $\vert\alpha\vert$ — another common **1-mark loss**.
> 5. **Accept action goes only into the cell corresponding to $\$$ in the state with $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$** — placing it elsewhere is a **2-mark error**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2: LR(0) Items and LR(0) Parsing**

- **Definition Checkpoint:**
  - LR(0) item = production + dot position (·). Total items per production = $\vert \alpha \vert + 1$.
  - Augmented production $S' \rightarrow S$ creates the unique accept state.

- **Operation Checkpoint:**
  - **CLOSURE($I$):** Add $[B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma]$ for every item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta]$ in $I$ and every production $B \rightarrow \gamma$.
  - **GOTO($I$, $X$):** Advance the dot past $X$, then apply CLOSURE.

- **Construction Checkpoint:**
  - Start with CLOSURE($\{S' \rightarrow \cdot S\}$) as $I_0$.
  - Recursively compute GOTO until no new states appear.
  - Result = the **canonical collection of LR(0) items** (the DFA).

- **Table-Building Checkpoint:**
  - **ACTION table:** terminals $\times$ states → shift, reduce, accept, or error.
    - Shift on terminal = GOTO transition.
    - Reduce by production $p$ on **every** terminal (and $\$$) if $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot] \in I_i$.
  - **GOTO table:** non-terminals $\times$ states → state number or blank.

- **Conflict Checkpoint:**
  - **Shift-reduce conflict:** state has both a shift item and a reduce item.
  - **Reduce-reduce conflict:** state has two or more reduce items.
  - Either conflict → grammar is **NOT LR(0)**.

- **Parsing Trace Checkpoint:**
  - Stack alternates: *symbol, state, symbol, state, …*
  - Reduction: pop $2 \cdot \vert\alpha\vert$ entries, push LHS, then push GOTO(top_state, LHS).
  - End of parsing: ACCEPT when state contains $S' \rightarrow S \cdot$ and look-ahead is $\$$.

- **LR(0) vs SLR(1) — Don't Confuse!**
  - LR(0): reduce on **ALL** terminals.
  - SLR(1): reduce on **FOLLOW($A$) only** — a refinement using 1-symbol look-ahead.

- **KTU Exam Frequency:** This topic is **high-yield** — expect 14-mark table-construction questions almost every semester. Master the worked example in §3 thoroughly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLR(1) Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SLR(1) Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: SLR(1) Parsing - Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SLR(1) Parsing** stands for **Simple LR(1) Parsing**. It is a deterministic bottom-up shift-reduce parsing technique that uses the **LR(0) canonical collection of items** to drive a Deterministic Finite Automaton (DFA), but resolves shift-reduce and reduce-reduce conflicts by consulting the **FOLLOW sets** of the left-hand side non-terminals with a **single lookahead token (1)**.

Formally, an SLR(1) parser is defined by the tuple:

$$M = (G', C, ACTION, GOTO)$$

Where:
- $G' = G \cup \{S' \rightarrow S\}$ is the **augmented grammar**
- $C = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, \ldots, I_n\}$ is the **canonical collection of LR(0) item sets**
- $ACTION$ is a mapping from $\text{State} \times \text{Terminal} \rightarrow \{\text{shift, reduce, accept, error}\}$
- $GOTO$ is a mapping from $\text{State} \times \text{Non\text{-}terminal} \rightarrow \text{State}$

The parser is "Simple" because it uses the **coarsest possible lookahead information** — namely the FOLLOW sets — rather than full LR(1) first sets. The **"1"** indicates exactly **one symbol** of lookahead is used during reductions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Construction Site Inspector

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Reading a Blueprint Backwards**

Imagine you are an inspector watching workers build a house (a sentence) from the foundation up. You can only see the topmost parts of the walls (the stack/parse stack) and the next brick to be laid (the lookahead symbol).

- **LR(0) Approach**: The inspector would try to complete a room **immediately** as soon as all its walls are visible on the stack — without looking at what the *next* blueprint instruction is. This leads to mistakes (conflicts).
- **SLR(1) Approach**: The inspector waits a moment and **glances at the next blueprint instruction (the lookahead)**. If that next instruction is something the completed room logically *expects* to follow (i.e., the symbol is in the FOLLOW set of the room's label), then the inspector gives the **"REDUCE"** command. Otherwise, the inspector **"SHIFTS"** more bricks onto the stack.

This "simple glance at what comes next" is precisely what differentiates SLR(1) from LR(0). It is computationally cheap and elegant — but not powerful enough for all grammars (a limitation that leads us to CLR(1) and LALR(1)).

## 1.3 Position in the Compiler Pipeline

| Phase | Component | Role |
|-------|-----------|------|
| Lexical Analysis | Lexer | Produces tokens |
| **Syntax Analysis** | **SLR(1) Parser** | **Validates grammatical structure using a parse table** |
| Semantic Analysis | Type Checker | Uses parse tree |
| Code Generation | Backend | Emits target code |

## 1.4 Why SLR(1) Matters in KTU 2024 Curriculum

> [!IMPORTANT]
> SLR(1) is the **foundational gateway** to the entire LR parsing family. Mastery of SLR(1) is mandatory before attempting CLR(1) or LALR(1) parsing. KTU examiners frequently test:
> 1. Construction of the **LR(0) automaton**
> 2. Computation of **FOLLOW sets**
> 3. Resolution of **shift-reduce conflicts** in the SLR table
> 4. Tracing the parser through a sample input string
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Algorithm (Step-by-Step Logic)

The SLR(1) parsing method operates in two distinct phases: an **offline compilation phase** (table construction) and an **online parsing phase** (input string analysis).

### Phase A: Offline SLR(1) Table Construction

1. **Grammar Augmentation**: Introduce a new start symbol $S'$ and the production $S' \rightarrow S$. This production is used **only** to signal successful parsing (the **ACCEPT** state).
2. **LR(0) Item Generation**: For every production $A \rightarrow \alpha$, generate items by inserting a dot ($\cdot$) at every possible position:
$$A \rightarrow \alpha \text{ yields items } A \rightarrow \cdot \alpha, \; A \rightarrow \alpha_1 \cdot \alpha_2, \; \ldots, \; A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot$$
3. **Canonical Collection Construction**: Apply the two closure operations:
   - **Closure(I)**: If $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta \in I$, add all items $B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ for every production $B \rightarrow \gamma$.
   - **Goto(I, X)**: For item set $I$ and grammar symbol $X$, construct the set of items $[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta]$ for every $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X\beta] \in I$.
4. **DFA Construction**: Build the DFA of item sets where each transition is a Goto edge labeled with a grammar symbol.
5. **FOLLOW Set Computation**: For every non-terminal $A$, compute $\text{FOLLOW}(A)$ — the set of terminals that can legally appear immediately after $A$ in any sentential form.
6. **Table Population**: Fill $ACTION$ and $GOTO$ tables.

### Phase B: Online Parsing (The Parsing Algorithm)

Maintain two structures:
- A **stack** containing pairs of (state, symbol): $s_0 X_1 s_1 X_2 s_2 \ldots X_m s_m$
- A **buffer** containing the input string followed by the end-marker $\$$.

At each step, let $a$ be the current lookahead (first symbol of the buffer):
- If $ACTION[s_m, a] = \text{shift } s$: push $a$ and $s$ onto the stack. Advance the input pointer.
- If $ACTION[s_m, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \beta$: pop $2 \cdot \vert \beta \vert$ symbols off the stack (where $\vert \beta \vert$ is the length of $\beta$). Let $s'$ be the new top state. Push $A$ and $GOTO[s', A]$.
- If $ACTION[s_m, a] = \text{accept}$: parsing is successful. **Halt**.
- If $ACTION[s_m, a] = \text{error}$: invoke the error recovery routine.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction**: The dot position in an item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta]$ encodes the parser's progress. When the dot is at the extreme right ($[A \rightarrow \beta \cdot]$), the item is called a **complete item** or **kernel item of reduction**. SLR(1) uses these complete items *combined with* FOLLOW($A$) to decide reduction.

## 2.2 Conflict Classification in SLR(1)

SLR(1) parsers can encounter two types of conflicts:

| Conflict Type | Formal Condition | Practical Meaning |
|---------------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Shift-Reduce (SR)** | $ACTION[s, a] = \text{shift } s'$ **AND** $ACTION[s, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \beta$ | Parser cannot decide whether to shift $a$ or reduce by $A \rightarrow \beta$ when seeing $a$. |
| **Reduce-Reduce (RR)** | $ACTION[s, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \beta$ **AND** $ACTION[s, a] = \text{reduce } B \rightarrow \gamma$ | Parser sees two different productions both eligible for reduction under the same lookahead. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The SLR(1) Resolution Rule**:
> A state $s$ containing complete item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ defines a **reduce action** on input $a$ if and only if $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.
> A shift action on $a$ is defined if $a$ is a terminal on the Goto transition from $s$.
> **No conflict exists** if for every state $s$ and every terminal $a$, at most one of these actions is defined.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Application Context |
|---------|----------------|---------------------|
| **Augmented Grammar** | $G' = G \cup \{S' \rightarrow S\}$ | Required for ACCEPT action definition |
| **Closure Operation** | $A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta \implies \forall (B \rightarrow \gamma) : \text{add } B \rightarrow \cdot \gamma$ | Completing item sets |
| **Goto Operation** | $\text{Goto}(I, X) = \text{Closure}(\{[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta] : [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X\beta] \in I\})$ | DFA transition |
| **FOLLOW Rule 1** | $\$ \in \text{FOLLOW}(S)$ | Start symbol initialization |
| **FOLLOW Rule 2** | $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta \implies \text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Propagation through productions |
| **FOLLOW Rule 3** | $A \rightarrow \alpha B \text{ or } A \rightarrow \alpha B\beta \text{ with } \varepsilon \in \text{FIRST}(\beta) \implies \text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(B)$ | Cascading through nullable derivations |
| **Shift Action** | If $a$ is a terminal and $\text{Goto}(I_s, a) = I_j$, then $ACTION[s, a] = \text{shift } j$ | Building ACTION table |
| **Reduce Action (SLR)** | If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot] \in I_s$, then $\forall a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A) : ACTION[s, a] = \text{reduce } A \rightarrow \alpha$ | SLR-specific rule |
| **Accept Action** | If $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot] \in I_s$, then $ACTION[s, \$] = \text{accept}$ | Termination |
| **GOTO Entry** | If $\text{Goto}(I_s, A) = I_j$ for non-terminal $A$, then $GOTO[s, A] = j$ | Building GOTO table |
| **Stack Depth** | $2 \cdot \vert \beta \vert$ symbols popped per reduction | Parser mechanics |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake**: Students frequently forget to **exclude $\varepsilon$ from FIRST sets** when computing FOLLOW (Rule 2). Use $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

SLR(1) parsers, despite their simplicity, are deployed in:
- **Educational compiler tools** (e.g., YACC/Bison when default options are used)
- **Domain-specific languages (DSLs)** for configuration files and lightweight scripting
- **Initial prototyping** of language front-ends before upgrading to LALR(1) or LR(1)
- **Hardware description languages** with constrained grammar designs (early Verilog subsets)
- **Mathematical expression evaluators** in CAS (Computer Algebra Systems) and IDE plugins
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Comprehensive Worked Example (KTU-Style Full Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement** (Modeled after KTU University Exam Pattern):
> Consider the following augmented grammar $G'$:
> - $S' \rightarrow S$
> - $S \rightarrow a A$
> - $S \rightarrow b$
> - $A \rightarrow c A$
> - $A \rightarrow d$
>
> **(a)** Construct the canonical collection of LR(0) items for $G'$.
> **(b)** Construct the SLR(1) parsing table.
> **(c)** Trace the parsing of the input string **$a c c d \$$**.

---

### Step 1: Grammar Augmentation

The grammar is already augmented. The start production is:

$$S' \rightarrow S$$

This is used to detect the **ACCEPT** state.

---

### Step 2: Enumeration of All LR(0) Items

For each production, we generate items by moving the dot ($\cdot$) from left to right:

| Production | Items Generated |
|------------|-----------------|
| $S' \rightarrow S$ | $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$, $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ |
| $S \rightarrow a A$ | $[S \rightarrow \cdot aA]$, $[S \rightarrow a \cdot A]$, $[S \rightarrow aA \cdot]$ |
| $S \rightarrow b$ | $[S \rightarrow \cdot b]$, $[S \rightarrow b \cdot]$ |
| $A \rightarrow c A$ | $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$, $[A \rightarrow c \cdot A]$, $[A \rightarrow cA \cdot]$ |
| $A \rightarrow d$ | $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$, $[A \rightarrow d \cdot]$ |

**Total items**: 11 distinct LR(0) items.

---

### Step 3: Computation of FOLLOW Sets

We apply the three rules systematically.

**Initialize**:
- $\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\}$ (Rule 1)
- $\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \emptyset$, $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \emptyset$

**Apply Rule 2** (Production $A \rightarrow \alpha B \beta$, add $\text{FIRST}(\beta) \setminus \{\varepsilon\}$ to $\text{FOLLOW}(B)$):
- $S' \rightarrow S$: $\beta = \varepsilon$, so $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S')$ (via Rule 3 effectively, since $\varepsilon$ is in $\text{FIRST}(\varepsilon)$)
- $S \rightarrow aA$: $\beta = \varepsilon$, so $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$
- $A \rightarrow cA$: $\beta = \varepsilon$, so $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ (no new info)

**Apply Rule 3** (Cascading through nullable $\beta$):
- From $S' \rightarrow S$ (with $\beta = \varepsilon$): $\text{FOLLOW}(S') \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S)$
- $\therefore \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\}$
- From $S \rightarrow aA$ (with $\beta = \varepsilon$): $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A)$
- $\therefore \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$

**Final FOLLOW Sets**:

$$\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\}$$
$$\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}$$
$$\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{\$\}$$

---

### Step 4: Construction of Canonical Collection $C = \{I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_9\}$

**Item Set $I_0$** (Initial state, computed by $\text{Closure}(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]\})$):

Apply closure: $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$ has $S$ after the dot, so add all $S$-productions with dot at start.
- $S \rightarrow aA$ has $A$ after the dot, so add all $A$-productions with dot at start.

$$I_0 = \begin{cases} [S' \rightarrow \cdot S] \\ [S \rightarrow \cdot aA] \\ [S \rightarrow \cdot b] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot cA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot d] \end{cases}$$

**Computing Goto Transitions from $I_0$**:

For each grammar symbol $X$ appearing after the dot, compute $\text{Goto}(I_0, X)$.

**$I_1 = \text{Goto}(I_0, S)$**:
- Find items in $I_0$ with $S$ after dot: $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$
- Move dot past $S$: $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$
- No further closure needed (no non-terminal after dot).

$$I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\}$$

**$I_2 = \text{Goto}(I_0, a)$**:
- Items in $I_0$ with $a$ after dot: $[S \rightarrow \cdot aA]$
- Move dot: $[S \rightarrow a \cdot A]$
- Closure: $A$ after dot → add $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$

$$I_2 = \begin{cases} [S \rightarrow a \cdot A] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot cA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot d] \end{cases}$$

**$I_3 = \text{Goto}(I_0, b)$**:
- Item $[S \rightarrow \cdot b]$ → $[S \rightarrow b \cdot]$

$$I_3 = \{[S \rightarrow b \cdot]\}$$

**$I_4 = \text{Goto}(I_0, c)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$ → $[A \rightarrow c \cdot A]$
- Closure: add $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$

$$I_4 = \begin{cases} [A \rightarrow c \cdot A] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot cA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot d] \end{cases}$$

**$I_5 = \text{Goto}(I_0, d)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$ → $[A \rightarrow d \cdot]$

$$I_5 = \{[A \rightarrow d \cdot]\}$$

**Computing Goto Transitions from $I_2$**:

**$I_6 = \text{Goto}(I_2, A)$**:
- Item $[S \rightarrow a \cdot A]$ → $[S \rightarrow aA \cdot]$

$$I_6 = \{[S \rightarrow aA \cdot]\}$$

**$I_7 = \text{Goto}(I_2, c)$** = $\text{Goto}(I_2, c)$ same structure as $I_4$:
- Item $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$ → $[A \rightarrow c \cdot A]$, plus closure

$$I_7 = I_4 = \begin{cases} [A \rightarrow c \cdot A] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot cA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot d] \end{cases}$$

**$I_8 = \text{Goto}(I_2, d)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$ → $[A \rightarrow d \cdot]$

$$I_8 = \{[A \rightarrow d \cdot]\}$$

**Computing Goto Transitions from $I_4$ (and equivalently $I_7$)**:

**$I_9 = \text{Goto}(I_4, A)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow c \cdot A]$ → $[A \rightarrow cA \cdot]$

$$I_9 = \{[A \rightarrow cA \cdot]\}$$

We observe $I_7 = I_4$ and $I_8 = I_5$ (set equality), so the DFA has **10 distinct states** $I_0$ through $I_9$.

---

### Step 5: DFA Summary of Item Sets

| State | Items | Transitions (Goto) |
|-------|-------|---------------------|
| $I_0$ | Initial (kernel: $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$) | $S \to I_1$, $a \to I_2$, $b \to I_3$, $c \to I_4$, $d \to I_5$ |
| $I_1$ | $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ | (terminal) |
| $I_2$ | $[S \rightarrow a \cdot A]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$ | $A \to I_6$, $c \to I_7$, $d \to I_8$ |
| $I_3$ | $[S \rightarrow b \cdot]$ | (terminal) |
| $I_4$ | $[A \rightarrow c \cdot A]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot cA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot d]$ | $A \to I_9$, $c \to I_4$, $d \to I_5$ |
| $I_5$ | $[A \rightarrow d \cdot]$ | (terminal) |
| $I_6$ | $[S \rightarrow aA \cdot]$ | (terminal) |
| $I_7$ | Same as $I_4$ | (same as $I_4$) |
| $I_8$ | Same as $I_5$ | (same as $I_5$) |
| $I_9$ | $[A \rightarrow cA \cdot]$ | (terminal) |

---

### Step 6: SLR(1) Parsing Table Construction

We populate the table using the rules from Section 2.3.

**ACTION Table Entries**:

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $c$ | $d$ | $\$$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|
| $0$ | s2 | s3 | s4 | s5 | |
| $1$ | | | | | **acc** |
| $2$ | | | s7 | s8 | |
| $3$ | | | | | r2 ($S \rightarrow b$) |
| $4$ | | | s4 | s5 | |
| $5$ | | | | | r4 ($A \rightarrow d$) |
| $6$ | | | | | r1 ($S \rightarrow aA$) |
| $7$ | | | s4 | s5 | |
| $8$ | | | | | r4 ($A \rightarrow d$) |
| $9$ | | | | | r3 ($A \rightarrow cA$) |

**Derivation of $I_1$**: Contains $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$, so $ACTION[1, \$] = \text{accept}$.
**Derivation of $I_3$**: Contains complete item $[S \rightarrow b \cdot]$, FOLLOW($S$) = $\{\$\}$, so $ACTION[3, \$] = r2$.
**Derivation of $I_5$**: Contains complete item $[A \rightarrow d \cdot]$, FOLLOW($A$) = $\{\$\}$, so $ACTION[5, \$] = r4$.
**Derivation of $I_6$**: Contains $[S \rightarrow aA \cdot]$, FOLLOW($S$) = $\{\$\}$, so $ACTION[6, \$] = r1$.
**Derivation of $I_8$**: Same as $I_5$, so $ACTION[8, \$] = r4$.
**Derivation of $I_9$**: Contains $[A \rightarrow cA \cdot]$, FOLLOW($A$) = $\{\$\}$, so $ACTION[9, \$] = r3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No conflicts arise** because every complete item maps to FOLLOW sets that **do not overlap** with the terminals defining shift actions in the same state. The grammar is **SLR(1)**.

**GOTO Table Entries** (Non-terminal columns):

| State | $S$ | $A$ |
|-------|-----|-----|
| $0$ | 1 | |
| $1$ | | |
| $2$ | | 6 |
| $3$ | | |
| $4$ | | 9 |
| $5$ | | |
| $6$ | | |
| $7$ | | 9 |
| $8$ | | |
| $9$ | | |

---

### Step 7: Tracing the Input String $a\,c\,c\,d\,\$$

The stack initially contains state $0$. We use the notation:
$$\text{Stack} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{Input} \;\;\vert\;\; \text{Action}$$

| Step | Stack | Input | Action |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | $0$ | $a\,c\,c\,d\,\$$ | $ACTION[0, a] = s2$; shift |
| 2 | $0\,a\,2$ | $c\,c\,d\,\$$ | $ACTION[2, c] = s7$; shift |
| 3 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7$ | $c\,d\,\$$ | $ACTION[7, c] = s4$; shift |
| 4 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7\,c\,4$ | $d\,\$$ | $ACTION[4, d] = s5$; shift |
| 5 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7\,c\,4\,d\,5$ | $\$$ | $ACTION[5, \$] = r4$ ($A \rightarrow d$); reduce |
| 6 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7\,c\,4$ | $\$$ | $GOTO[4, A] = 9$; push $A$ and state $9$ |
| 7 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7\,c\,4\,A\,9$ | $\$$ | $ACTION[9, \$] = r3$ ($A \rightarrow cA$); reduce |
| 8 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7$ | $\$$ | $GOTO[7, A] = 9$; push $A$ and state $9$ |
| 9 | $0\,a\,2\,c\,7\,A\,9$ | $\$$ | $ACTION[9, \$] = r3$ ($A \rightarrow cA$); reduce |
| 10 | $0\,a\,2$ | $\$$ | $GOTO[2, A] = 6$; push $A$ and state $6$ |
| 11 | $0\,a\,2\,A\,6$ | $\$$ | $ACTION[6, \$] = r1$ ($S \rightarrow aA$); reduce |
| 12 | $0$ | $\$$ | $GOTO[0, S] = 1$; push $S$ and state $1$ |
| 13 | $0\,S\,1$ | $\$$ | $ACTION[1, \$] = \text{accept}$ |

**Result**: The string $a\,c\,c\,d$ is **accepted** by the SLR(1) parser. ✓

---

## 3.2 Conflict Identification Pattern (Common KTU Trap)

> [!WARNING]
> **Illustrative Conflict Case** (for understanding, not solved in full here):
> Consider grammar: $S \rightarrow L = R \;\vert\; R$, $L \rightarrow * R \;\vert\; \text{id}$, $R \rightarrow L$.
> FOLLOW($L$) contains $=$ (from $S \rightarrow L = R$) and FOLLOW($R$) contains $=$ (from $S \rightarrow L = R$, $L \rightarrow *R$).
> In a state with $[R \rightarrow L \cdot]$ and a transition on $=$ (from $S \rightarrow L \cdot = R$), we have:
> - Shift $=$ (because $=$ is on a Goto edge)
> - Reduce $R \rightarrow L$ (because $= \in \text{FOLLOW}(R)$)
> This is a **shift-reduce conflict** → **not SLR(1)**.

The analytical pattern is always the same: **does any lookahead in the shift transition set also lie in the FOLLOW set of a complete item?**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SLR(1) Parser System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Augmented Grammar G prime] --> B[LR 0 Item Generator]
    B --> C[Closure and Goto Engine]
    C --> D[Canonical Collection C of Item Sets]
    D --> E[DFA of LR 0 States]
    F[Follow Set Computer] --> G[SLR Action Table Builder]
    E --> G
    D --> G
    G --> H[ACTION Table shift reduce accept]
    D --> I[GOTO Table Builder]
    E --> I
    I --> J[GOTO Table state transitions]
    H --> K[SLR 1 Parsing Engine]
    J --> K
    L[Input String with Dollar] --> K
    K --> M{Parse Stack Top and Lookahead}
    M -->|shift s| N[Push Terminal and State s]
    M -->|reduce A to beta| O[Pop 2 times beta]
    M -->|accept| P[Successful Parse Output]
    M -->|error| Q[Error Recovery Routine]
    N --> M
    O --> R[GOTO new top A]
    R --> M
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram: SLR(1) DFA for the Worked Example

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> I0
    I0 --> I1 : S
    I0 --> I2 : a
    I0 --> I3 : b
    I0 --> I4 : c
    I0 --> I5 : d
    I2 --> I6 : A
    I2 --> I7 : c
    I2 --> I8 : d
    I4 --> I9 : A
    I4 --> I4 : c
    I4 --> I5 : d
    I7 --> I9 : A
    I7 --> I4 : c
    I7 --> I5 : d
    I1 --> [*] : accept on Dollar
    I3 --> I3 : reduce S to b
    I5 --> I5 : reduce A to d
    I6 --> I6 : reduce S to aA
    I8 --> I8 : reduce A to d
    I9 --> I9 : reduce A to cA
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart: Parsing Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Read Top State s] --> B[Read Lookahead a]
    B --> C{Lookup ACTION s comma a}
    C -->|shift s prime| D[Push a then s prime]
    C -->|reduce A to beta| E[Pop 2 times beta entries]
    C -->|accept| F[Success Exit]
    C -->|error or empty| G[Invoke Error Recovery]
    E --> H[Lookup GOTO new top A]
    H --> I[Push A and target state]
    D --> A
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the State Diagram**:
> - $I_0$ is the **initial state** (always contains the augmented start item).
> - $I_1$ contains $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$, so on input $\$$ it triggers **ACCEPT**.
> - States $I_3, I_5, I_6, I_8, I_9$ are **reduction states** because all their items are complete (dot at extreme right).
> - States $I_2, I_4, I_7$ are **mixed states** with both shift possibilities and reduction potential (though in this grammar, the shift symbols $a, c$ do not intersect with FOLLOW sets, so no conflict).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**

**Q1.** Define an **LR(0) item**. How does the concept of the *dot* position help the SLR(1) parser in deciding its actions?

**Model Answer**:
An LR(0) item is a production of the grammar with a special marker (the dot $\cdot$) placed at some position on the right-hand side. Formally, for a production $A \rightarrow XYZ$, the items are:
$$A \rightarrow \cdot XYZ, \quad A \rightarrow X \cdot YZ, \quad A \rightarrow XY \cdot Z, \quad A \rightarrow XYZ \cdot$$

The dot position encodes the parser's progress in recognizing the right-hand side:
- A dot **at the beginning** ($[A \rightarrow \cdot \alpha]$) indicates the parser has not yet seen any part of $\alpha$ on the stack.
- A dot **in the middle** ($[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta]$) indicates that $\alpha$ has been matched and the parser expects to see $\beta$ next.
- A dot **at the end** ($[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$, called a *complete item*) indicates the entire RHS is on the stack, and the parser is ready to reduce $A \rightarrow \alpha$, provided the lookahead permits.

In SLR(1), complete items in a state $I_s$ trigger a *reduce by $A \rightarrow \alpha$* action on every input $a$ such that $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **LR(0), SLR(1), CLR(1), and LALR(1)** parsers in terms of lookahead and conflict resolution power.

**Model Answer**:

| Parser | Lookahead | Item Type | Conflict Resolution Basis | Power |
|--------|-----------|-----------|--------------------------|-------|
| **LR(0)** | 0 symbols | LR(0) items | None (no lookahead used) | Weakest |
| **SLR(1)** | 1 symbol | LR(0) items | FOLLOW($A$) sets | Modest |
| **CLR(1)** | 1 symbol | LR(1) items | Full first/follow lookahead sets per item | Strongest |
| **LALR(1)** | 1 symbol | LR(0) items with merged lookahead | Same as CLR(1) but with merged states | Between SLR and CLR |

The progression is: $\text{LR(0)} \subset \text{SLR(1)} \subset \text{LALR(1)} \subset \text{CLR(1)}$ in terms of the set of grammars accepted, and the reverse in terms of table size and construction cost. SLR(1) is the simplest enhancement to LR(0) — it adds just enough lookahead (via FOLLOW sets) to resolve most practical conflicts without the exponential blowup of full LR(1) tables.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question Paper, Module 2, Q-Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Consider the augmented grammar $G'$:
- $S' \rightarrow S$
- $S \rightarrow A A$
- $A \rightarrow a A \;\vert\; b$

Construct the **canonical collection of LR(0) item sets** (the DFA) for $G'$. Show the closure and Goto operations explicitly. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compute the **FOLLOW sets** for all non-terminals and construct the **SLR(1) parsing table**. Verify whether the grammar is SLR(1) by checking for any conflicts. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question A**:

#### Part (a): Canonical Collection Construction

**Step 1**: List all LR(0) items. The productions are:
- $S' \rightarrow S$ → 2 items
- $S \rightarrow AA$ → 4 items
- $A \rightarrow aA$ → 4 items
- $A \rightarrow b$ → 2 items

Total: 12 items.

**Step 2**: Compute $I_0 = \text{Closure}(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]\})$.

- Start: $[S' \rightarrow \cdot S]$
- Closure: $S$ after dot → add $[S \rightarrow \cdot AA]$
- Closure: First $A$ after dot → add $[A \rightarrow \cdot aA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot b]$
- Second $A$ has nothing after dot, so no further closure.

$$I_0 = \begin{cases} [S' \rightarrow \cdot S] \\ [S \rightarrow \cdot AA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot aA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot b] \end{cases}$$

**Step 3**: Compute Goto operations from $I_0$:

**$I_1 = \text{Goto}(I_0, S)$**:
$$I_1 = \{[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]\}$$

**$I_2 = \text{Goto}(I_0, A)$**:
- Item $[S \rightarrow \cdot AA]$ → $[S \rightarrow A \cdot A]$
- Closure: Second $A$ after dot → add $[A \rightarrow \cdot aA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot b]$

$$I_2 = \begin{cases} [S \rightarrow A \cdot A] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot aA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot b] \end{cases}$$

**$I_3 = \text{Goto}(I_0, a)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow \cdot aA]$ → $[A \rightarrow a \cdot A]$
- Closure: $A$ after dot → add $[A \rightarrow \cdot aA]$, $[A \rightarrow \cdot b]$

$$I_3 = \begin{cases} [A \rightarrow a \cdot A] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot aA] \\ [A \rightarrow \cdot b] \end{cases}$$

**$I_4 = \text{Goto}(I_0, b)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow \cdot b]$ → $[A \rightarrow b \cdot]$

$$I_4 = \{[A \rightarrow b \cdot]\}$$

**Step 4**: Continue from $I_2$:

**$I_5 = \text{Goto}(I_2, A)$**:
- Item $[S \rightarrow A \cdot A]$ → $[S \rightarrow AA \cdot]$

$$I_5 = \{[S \rightarrow AA \cdot]\}$$

**$I_6 = \text{Goto}(I_2, a) = \text{Goto}(I_3, a) = I_3$** (revisited).
**$I_7 = \text{Goto}(I_2, b) = I_4$** (revisited).

**Step 5**: From $I_3$:

**$I_8 = \text{Goto}(I_3, A)$**:
- Item $[A \rightarrow a \cdot A]$ → $[A \rightarrow aA \cdot]$

$$I_8 = \{[A \rightarrow aA \cdot]\}$$

**Final Canonical Collection** (9 distinct states):
$$C = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5, I_6, I_7, I_8\} = \{I_0, I_1, I_2, I_3, I_4, I_5, I_8\}$$

with $I_6 = I_3$ and $I_7 = I_4$.

---

#### Part (b): FOLLOW Sets and SLR(1) Table

**FOLLOW Computation**:
- $\text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\}$
- From $S' \rightarrow S$: $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(S') = \{\$\}$
- From $S \rightarrow AA$: First $A$ has second $A$ after it (no $\varepsilon$), so $\text{FIRST}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(\text{first } A)$
  - $\text{FIRST}(A) = \{a, b\}$
  - So $\{a, b\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A)$
- From $S \rightarrow AA$: Second $A$ has nothing after it, so $\text{FOLLOW}(S) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(\text{second } A)$
  - $\therefore \{\$\} \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(A)$
- From $A \rightarrow aA$: inner $A$ has nothing after it, so $\text{FOLLOW}(A) \subseteq \text{FOLLOW}(\text{inner } A)$

$$\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(S) = \{\$\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a, b, \$\}}$$

**SLR(1) Parsing Table**:

| State | $a$ | $b$ | $\$$ | $S$ | $A$ |
|-------|-----|-----|------|-----|-----|
| $0$ | s3 | s4 | | 1 | 2 |
| $1$ | | | **acc** | | |
| $2$ | s3 | s4 | | | 5 |
| $3$ | s3 | s4 | | | 8 |
| $4$ | r3 ($A \rightarrow b$) | r3 | r3 | | |
| $5$ | | | r1 ($S \rightarrow AA$) | | |
| $6$ (= $I_3$) | s3 | s4 | | | 8 |
| $7$ (= $I_4$) | r3 | r3 | r3 | | |
| $8$ | r2 ($A \rightarrow aA$) | r2 | r2 | | |

**Conflict Check**:
- $I_4$ (state 4): Complete item $[A \rightarrow b \cdot]$ with $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a, b, \$\}$. No shift transitions on $a, b, \$$ in $I_4$. **No conflict**.
- $I_8$ (state 8): Complete item $[A \rightarrow aA \cdot]$ with $\text{FOLLOW}(A) = \{a, b, \$\}$. No shift transitions. **No conflict**.

**Conclusion**: The grammar is **SLR(1)**. No shift-reduce or reduce-reduce conflicts exist. ✓

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **algorithm for SLR(1) parsing** with a clearly labeled **stack-frame diagram**. Describe the role of the **$ and FOLLOW sets** in detail. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** For the grammar $E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$, $T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$, $F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; \text{id}$:
   - (i) Augment the grammar and compute the **canonical LR(0) collection** (identify at least 6 item sets).
   - (ii) Compute FOLLOW sets and build the **SLR(1) table**. Show whether the grammar is SLR(1). **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution for Question B**:

#### Part (a): SLR(1) Algorithm and Stack Mechanics

> [!NOTE]
> **SLR(1) Parsing Algorithm**:
> **Input**: An input string $w$ and the SLR(1) parsing table for grammar $G$.
> **Output**: If $w \in L(G)$, a rightmost derivation; otherwise, an error indication.
>
> **Data Structures**:
> - Stack $S$ holding pairs $(s_i, X_i)$ where $s_i$ is a state and $X_i$ a grammar symbol. Initially $S = (0)$.
> - Buffer containing $w\$$.
>
> **Procedure**:
> Let $a$ be the first symbol of the buffer and $s$ the state on top of the stack.
> 1. If $ACTION[s, a] = s_j$: shift $a$ onto stack; push state $j$.
> 2. If $ACTION[s, a] = r_j$ where $A \rightarrow \beta$ is production $j$: pop $2 \cdot \vert \beta \vert$ symbols; let $s'$ be the new top; push $A$ and $GOTO[s', A]$.
> 3. If $ACTION[s, a] = \text{accept}$: halt successfully.
> 4. Else: error; invoke recovery.

**Role of $\$**:
The end-marker $\$$ is a **sentinel terminal** added to the input buffer. It plays two crucial roles:
- It enables parsing of the **complete** string by providing a definitive termination condition.
- The state containing $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ uses $ACTION[s, \$] = \text{accept}$ to signal successful completion. Without $\$$, the parser would have no way to know when the input ends.

**Role of FOLLOW Sets**:
In SLR(1), FOLLOW($A$) answers the question: *"After reducing the handle $\alpha$ to $A$, what terminals can legitimately appear next?"* If the lookahead $a$ is in FOLLOW($A$), then reducing $A \rightarrow \alpha$ is a valid action because $A$ can legally be followed by $a$ in some sentential form. This is the **central insight** that makes SLR(1) more powerful than LR(0).

**Stack-Frame Diagram (Conceptual)**:
```
| Top of Stack (TOS) |
|-------------------|
|      State s      |  <-- Current DFA state
|   Grammar Symbol  |  <-- Last symbol shifted/reduced
|      ...          |
|      State 0      |  <-- Bottom (initial state)
```

Each reduction operation removes $2 \cdot \vert \beta \vert$ entries (one state and one symbol per item in $\beta$) and replaces them with the LHS non-terminal and its GOTO state.

---

#### Part (b): Arithmetic Expression Grammar

**Augmented Grammar**:
- $E' \rightarrow E$
- $E \rightarrow E + T$
- $E \rightarrow T$
- $T \rightarrow T * F$
- $T \rightarrow F$
- $F \rightarrow (E)$
- $F \rightarrow \text{id}$

**Selected Item Sets (from 12 total states)**:

$$I_0 = \begin{cases} [E' \rightarrow \cdot E] \\ [E \rightarrow \cdot E + T] \\ [E \rightarrow \cdot T] \\ [T \rightarrow \cdot T * F] \\ [T \rightarrow \cdot F] \\ [F \rightarrow \cdot (E)] \\ [F \rightarrow \cdot \text{id}] \end{cases}$$

$$\text{Goto}(I_0, E) = I_1, \quad \text{Goto}(I_0, T) = I_2, \quad \text{Goto}(I_0, F) = I_3$$
$$\text{Goto}(I_0, () = I_4, \quad \text{Goto}(I_0, \text{id}) = I_5$$

$$I_1 = \begin{cases} [E' \rightarrow E \cdot] \\ [E \rightarrow E \cdot + T] \end{cases}$$

$$I_2 = \begin{cases} [T \rightarrow T \cdot * F] \\ [E \rightarrow T \cdot] \end{cases}$$

$$I_3 = \{[T \rightarrow F \cdot]\}$$

$$I_4 = \begin{cases} [F \rightarrow ( \cdot E)] \\ [E \rightarrow \cdot E + T] \\ [E \rightarrow \cdot T] \\ [T \rightarrow \cdot T * F] \\ [T \rightarrow \cdot F] \\ [F \rightarrow \cdot (E)] \\ [F \rightarrow \cdot \text{id}] \end{cases}$$

$$I_5 = \{[F \rightarrow \text{id} \cdot]\}$$

**FOLLOW Sets**:
- $\text{FOLLOW}(E') = \{\$\}$
- From $E' \rightarrow E$: $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \supseteq \{\$\}$
- From $F \rightarrow (E)$: $\text{FOLLOW}(E) \supseteq \{)\}$
- From $E \rightarrow E + T$: $\text{FIRST}(+T) = \{+\}$ → $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \supseteq \{+\}$
- From $E \rightarrow E + T$: nothing after second $T$ → $\text{FOLLOW}(T) \supseteq \text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, )\}$
- From $T \rightarrow T * F$: $\text{FOLLOW}(F) \supseteq \{*\}$ and $\text{FOLLOW}(F) \supseteq \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, \$, )\}$
- From $T \rightarrow T * F$: $\text{FOLLOW}(F) \supseteq \{*\}$

$$\boxed{\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, )\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(T) = \{+, *, \$, )\}, \quad \text{FOLLOW}(F) = \{+, *, \$, )\}}$$

**SLR(1) Table Excerpt (Critical States)**:

| State | $+$ | $*$ | $($ | $)$ | $\text{id}$ | $\$$ | $E$ | $T$ | $F$ |
|-------|-----|-----|-----|-----|-----|------|-----|-----|-----|
| $0$ | | | s4 | | s5 | | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| $1$ | s6 | | | | | acc | | | |
| $2$ | r2 | s7 | | r2 | | r2 | | | |
| $3$ | r4 | r4 | | r4 | | r4 | | | |
| $4$ | | | s4 | | s5 | | 8 | 2 | 3 |
| $5$ | r6 | r6 | | r6 | | r6 | | | |
| $6$ | | | s4 | | s5 | | | 9 | 3 |
| $7$ | | | s4 | | s5 | | | | 10 |
| $8$ | s6 | | | s11 | | | | | |
| $9$ | r1 | s7 | | r1 | | r1 | | | |
| $10$ | r3 | r3 | | r3 | | r3 | | | |
| $11$ | r5 | r5 | | r5 | | r5 | | | |

**Conflict Check (Critical States)**:
- **$I_2$** (state 2): Complete item $[E \rightarrow T \cdot]$ with $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, )\}$. Shift transitions: $*$ (on $[T \rightarrow T \cdot * F]$). Sets: $\{\$, )\}$ vs. $\{*\}$. **Disjoint → No conflict**.
- **$I_9$** (state 9): Complete item $[E \rightarrow E + T \cdot]$ with $\text{FOLLOW}(E) = \{\$, )\}$. Shift on $*$. Disjoint. **No conflict**.

**Conclusion**: The arithmetic expression grammar is **SLR(1)**. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Zones in SLR(1) Problems** (as observed in KTU valuation keys):
>
> 1. **Forgetting to augment the grammar**: The augmented production $S' \rightarrow S$ is **mandatory** for the ACCEPT action. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if omitted.
>
> 2. **Incorrect FOLLOW computation**: Students often:
>    - Forget to add $\$$ to FOLLOW($S$) via Rule 1.
>    - Confuse FIRST with FOLLOW.
>    - Miss the cascading Rule 3 when $\beta = \varepsilon$.
>
> 3. **Mixing up shift and reduce rules**: The shift action is based on **Goto transitions on terminals**; the reduce action is based on **complete items + FOLLOW sets**. Confusing these two yields 2–3 mark losses.
>
> 4. **Skipping the conflict check**: Even after building the table, students often **do not explicitly verify** whether conflicts exist. Always state: *"In state $I_k$, the complete item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ requires reduction on FOLLOW($A$) = $\{\ldots\}$, but the shift transitions on $\{\ldots\}$ are disjoint, so no conflict."*
>
> 5. **Incorrect stack-pop arithmetic in tracing**: When reducing by $A \rightarrow \beta$ where $\vert \beta \vert = n$, pop exactly $2n$ entries (not $n$ and not $n+1$). This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to renumber states**: The canonical collection $C$ is a **set of sets**. If $I_i = I_j$ as sets, they share a single state number. Missing this renumbering creates phantom states and incorrect tables.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — SLR(1) Parsing**

- **Definition Recap**: SLR(1) = LR(0) DFA + FOLLOW-set-driven reductions + one-symbol lookahead.
- **Augmentation**: Always add $S' \rightarrow S$ as production 0.
- **Item Form**: $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta]$ — the dot tracks parsing progress.
- **Closure Rule**: Items with a non-terminal after the dot trigger addition of all productions of that non-terminal with the dot at the start.
- **Goto Rule**: Move the dot past symbol $X$ in every applicable item, then take closure.
- **FOLLOW Set Initialization**: Start with FOLLOW($S'$) = $\{\$\}$. Apply three rules iteratively until convergence.
- **ACTION Table**:
  - **Shift**: On terminal $a$ with $\text{Goto}(I_s, a) = I_j$, set $ACTION[s, a] = s_j$.
  - **Reduce**: On complete item $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot]$ in $I_s$, set $ACTION[s, a] = r_k$ for all $a \in \text{FOLLOW}(A)$ where production $k$ is $A \rightarrow \alpha$.
  - **Accept**: On $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot]$ in $I_s$, set $ACTION[s, \$] = \text{accept}$.
- **GOTO Table**: On non-terminal $A$ with $\text{Goto}(I_s, A) = I_j$, set $GOTO[s, A] = j$.
- **Conflict Test**: A grammar is SLR(1) if and only if no state contains both a shift action and a reduce action on the same terminal, and no state contains two reduce actions on the same terminal.
- **Power Hierarchy**: $\text{LR(0)} \subset \text{SLR(1)} \subset \text{LALR(1)} \subset \text{CLR(1)}$.
- **When SLR(1) Fails**: When a non-terminal appears in multiple contexts with disjoint FOLLOW sets, the FOLLOW-based reduction is overly conservative. CLR(1) handles this by using **per-item lookahead sets**.
- **Stack Mechanic**: Each reduction pops $2 \cdot \vert \beta \vert$ entries and pushes the LHS non-terminal plus its GOTO state.
- **Real-World Tool**: **YACC** and **GNU Bison** generate LALR(1) tables by default but the underlying machinery is rooted in SLR(1) theory.
- **Exam Tip**: Always draw the DFA **before** building the table. Always compute FOLLOW **before** filling reductions. Always verify conflicts **explicitly** with a sentence like "the sets are disjoint".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: General LR(1) and LALR(1) Parsing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** General LR(1) and LALR(1) Parsing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 LR(1) Parsing — Formal Definition

**LR(1) Parsing** is a powerful, deterministic, bottom-up shift-reduce parsing technique in which the parsing decision (shift, reduce, accept, or error) at any step is governed by:
- The entire contents of the parsing **stack** (L for Left-to-right scan),
- The next **k** input symbols to be read, and
- For LR(1) specifically, exactly **k = 1** lookahead symbol.

An **LR(1) item** (also called an *LR(1) configuration*) is a pair:

$$[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \;\; a]$$

where:
- $A \rightarrow \alpha\beta$ is a production of the augmented grammar,
- $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are arbitrary string of grammar symbols,
- The dot $\cdot$ separates what has already been recognized ($\alpha$) from what is yet to be recognized ($\beta$),
- $a$ is a **terminal symbol called the lookahead**, drawn from $FIRST(\beta a)$ at construction time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from LR(0):** In an LR(0) item, the second component is absent. The lookahead $a$ in LR(1) carries contextual information about *which* terminal can validly follow $A$ in the current sentential form. This extra symbol dramatically increases the discriminative power of the parser.

## 1.2 LALR(1) Parsing — Formal Definition

**LALR(1) (Look-Ahead LR(1)) Parsing** is a practical refinement of LR(1) parsing. It is constructed by merging any two LR(1) item sets that have the **same core** — meaning the same set of productions with the same dot position, ignoring the lookaheads. The merged state's lookaheads are the **union** of the lookaheads from the original states.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Core of an LR(1) Item** is obtained by deleting the lookahead. Two items $[A \rightarrow \alpha\cdot\beta, \; a]$ and $[A \rightarrow \alpha\cdot\beta, \; b]$ share the same core, namely $[A \rightarrow \alpha\cdot\beta]$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Airport Security Checkpoint

Imagine an **airport security line** as a parser:
- **LR(0)** = A security guard who decides to flag your bag for inspection using **only the X-ray image of what's already on the conveyor belt** (the stack). With no extra context, the guard over-flags bags → many false alarms.
- **SLR(1)** = A smarter guard who also glances at the **arrival flight's destination country** (the FOLLOW set). Better, but still imperfect.
- **LR(1)** = A meticulous guard who combines the X-ray image with the **exact seat number of the passenger** (precise lookahead). Extremely accurate, but the guard requires a huge instruction manual (parsing table).
- **LALR(1)** = The meticulous guard's handbook is **compressed**: instructions for "passengers in row 12A" and "passengers in row 12B" are merged into one rule "passengers in row 12". Smaller handbook, almost the same accuracy.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LALR(1) is what production compilers like **YACC** and **GNU Bison** actually use. It is the sweet spot between power and table size.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics

- **Number of states** in a parser = number of item sets in the canonical collection.
- **Typical size ratio:** $\vert \text{LR(1)} \text{ states} \vert \approx 2 \text{ to } 10 \times \vert \text{SLR(1)} \text{ states} \vert$ for the same grammar.
- **State count invariant:** $\text{LALR(1) states} = \text{SLR(1) states} = \text{LR(0) states}$, since merging only affects lookaheads, not the cores.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Fundamental Operations on LR(1) Items

### 2.1.1 CLOSURE Operation
Given a set $I$ of LR(1) items, $CLOSURE(I)$ is the smallest set satisfying:
1. Every item in $I$ is in $CLOSURE(I)$.
2. If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B \beta, \; a] \in CLOSURE(I)$, and $B \rightarrow \gamma$ is a production, then for **every terminal** $b \in FIRST(\beta a)$, the item $[B \rightarrow \cdot\gamma, \; b]$ is added to $CLOSURE(I)$.

**Why?** Because once we may need to reduce $B$ in a context where $B$ is followed by $\beta$ and the next input is $a$, we must be prepared to derive any $b$ that can legally begin that derivation.

### 2.1.2 GOTO Operation
For a set $I$ of LR(1) items and a grammar symbol $X$:

$$GOTO(I, X) = CLOSURE\!\left( \{ [A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta, \; a] \;\vert\; [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X \beta, \; a] \in I \} \right)$$

**Why?** If the parser has shifted $X$ on top of the dot, the new configuration must reflect $X$ as recognized. The closure then brings in everything that could legally follow.

## 2.2 The LR(1) Construction Algorithm

**Input:** An augmented grammar $G'$.
**Output:** The canonical collection of LR(1) item sets $C = \{I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n\}$ and the LR(1) parsing table.

**Procedure:**
1. Augment the grammar with $S' \rightarrow S$.
2. Initialize $C = \{ CLOSURE(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]\}) \}$ and let this be $I_0$.
3. Repeat until no new item set can be added to $C$:
   - For each item set $I_i \in C$ and each grammar symbol $X$:
     - Compute $J = GOTO(I_i, X)$.
     - If $J \neq \emptyset$ and $J \notin C$, add $J$ to $C$.
4. Construct the parsing table: state $i$ corresponds to item set $I_i$.

## 2.3 LALR(1) Construction — Two Equivalent Methods

**Method A (Merge Method):**
1. Construct the full LR(1) collection $C = \{I_0, I_1, \ldots, I_n\}$.
2. For each pair $(I_i, I_j)$ with the **same core**, replace them with their union.
3. The merged collection is $C'$.

**Method B (Direct Construction):**
Use the *LALR(1) propagation table* technique (Aho, Sethi, Ullman), which directly computes lookaheads without ever materializing the full LR(1) table. Essential for large grammars.

## 2.4 Conflict Behavior in LALR(1)

- **No new shift/reduce conflicts** are ever introduced by LALR(1) merging.
- **Reduce/Reduce conflicts** *may* appear that did not exist in LR(1). These are called **spurious conflicts**.
- If LR(1) has no conflicts, LALR(1) *may* still be conflict-free — this is the common case.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | LR(1) Item | $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot \beta, \;\; a]$ where $a \in V_T \cup \{\$\}$ |
| 2 | CLOSURE | If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot B\beta, \; a] \in I$, add $[B \rightarrow \cdot\gamma, \; b]$ for all $b \in FIRST(\beta a)$ |
| 3 | GOTO | $GOTO(I,X) = CLOSURE(\{[A \rightarrow \alpha X \cdot \beta, \; a] \mid [A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot X\beta, \; a] \in I\})$ |
| 4 | Initial State | $I_0 = CLOSURE(\{[S' \rightarrow \cdot S, \; \$]\})$ |
| 5 | Shift action | If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot a\beta, \; b] \in I_i$ and $GOTO(I_i, a) = I_j$, then $ACTION[i, a] = \text{shift } j$ |
| 6 | Reduce action | If $[A \rightarrow \alpha \cdot, \; a] \in I_i$, then $ACTION[i, a] = \text{reduce by } A \rightarrow \alpha$ |
| 7 | Accept | $ACTION[i, \$] = \text{accept}$ for state containing $[S' \rightarrow S \cdot, \; \$]$ |
| 8 | LALR merge | $I_i \sim I_j$ iff $\text{core}(I_i) = \text{core}(I_j)$. Merged lookahead $= \text{union of lookaheads}$ |
| 9 | Power hierarchy | $LR(0) \subseteq SLR(1) \subseteq LALR(1) \subseteq LR(1)$ |
| 10 | State count | $\vert C_{LR(1)} \vert \geq \vert C_{LALR(1)} \vert = \vert C_{SLR(1)} \vert = \vert C_{LR(0)} \vert$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation:** $FIRST(\beta a)$ means: if $\beta$ derives $\varepsilon$, the lookahead is $a$; otherwise, it is $FIRST(\beta)$. Symbolically, $FIRST(\beta a) = FIRST(\beta)$ if $\varepsilon \notin FIRST(\beta)$, else $FIRST(\beta) \cup \{a\}$.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **YACC / GNU Bison** (used to build GCC, Clang front-ends, PHP engine) internally use LALR(1).
- **JavaCUP**, **Elkhound**, **GLR parsers** are all descendants of LALR(1) technology.
- LALR(1) is the standard for **compiler construction courses** worldwide and the de-facto choice for industrial programming languages (C, C++, Java, Python parsers).
