# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Infix → Postfix (Full Derivation)

**Convert:** $\;\; a + b \times c - d / e \;$ to **postfix** using the Shunting-Yard algorithm.

### Step-by-Step Trace

Let the operator stack be denoted as $\Sigma$ (top of stack on the right) and the output as $\Pi$.

| Step | Token Read | Action | Stack $\Sigma$ | Output $\Pi$ |
|------|------------|--------|----------------|---------------|
| 1 | `a` | Operand → output | (empty) | `a` |
| 2 | `+` | Stack empty → push | `+` | `a` |
| 3 | `b` | Operand → output | `+` | `a b` |
| 4 | `×` | prec(×) = 3 > prec(+) = 2 → push | `+ ×` | `a b` |
| 5 | `c` | Operand → output | `+ ×` | `a b c` |
| 6 | `-` | prec(−) = 2; top is `×` with prec = 3 ≥ 2 → pop `×`; top is `+` with prec = 2 ≥ 2 → pop `+`; push `−` | `−` | `a b c × +` |
| 7 | `d` | Operand → output | `−` | `a b c × + d` |
| 8 | `/` | prec(/) = 3 > prec(−) = 2 → push | `− /` | `a b c × + d` |
| 9 | `e` | Operand → output | `− /` | `a b c × + d e` |
| 10 | (end) | Pop all → `−` then `/` … wait, pop all remaining | (empty) | `a b c × + d e / −` |

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\; a + b \times c - d / e \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; a\;b\;c\;\times\;+\;d\;e\;/ \;-\;}
$$

**Verification by Stack Evaluation:**

| Step | Read | Stack (bottom → top) | Action |
|------|------|----------------------|--------|
| 1 | `a` | `[a]` | Push |
| 2 | `b` | `[a, b]` | Push |
| 3 | `c` | `[a, b, c]` | Push |
| 4 | `×` | `[a, b×c]` | Pop `c`, `b`; push `b×c` |
| 5 | `+` | `[a+(b×c)]` | Pop `b×c`, `a`; push result |
| 6 | `d` | `[a+b×c, d]` | Push |
| 7 | `e` | `[a+b×c, d, e]` | Push |
| 8 | `/` | `[a+b×c, d/e]` | Pop `e`, `d`; push `d/e` |
| 9 | `−` | `[(a+b×c) − (d/e)]` | Pop `d/e`, `a+b×c`; push result |

The final result is $(a + b \times c) - (d / e)$ ✓ — matches the original infix due to precedence.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Postfix → Three Address Code (Full Derivation)

**Convert the postfix expression** $\;\; a\;b\;c\;\times\;+\;d\;e\;/\;-\;\;$ to **TAC**.

### Step-by-Step Trace

Let $T$ be the operand stack; let $i$ be the global temporary counter (starts at 1).

| Step | Token | Top of $T$ (before) | Action | TAC Emitted | $T$ (after) |
|------|-------|---------------------|--------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | `a` | (empty) | Push `a` | — | `[a]` |
| 2 | `b` | `[a]` | Push `b` | — | `[a, b]` |
| 3 | `c` | `[a, b]` | Push `c` | — | `[a, b, c]` |
| 4 | `×` | `[a, b, c]` | Pop `c = z`, Pop `b = y`; $i = 1$ | `t₁ := b × c` | `[a, t₁]` |
| 5 | `+` | `[a, t₁]` | Pop `t₁ = z`, Pop `a = y`; $i = 2$ | `t₂ := a + t₁` | `[t₂]` |
| 6 | `d` | `[t₂]` | Push `d` | — | `[t₂, d]` |
| 7 | `e` | `[t₂, d]` | Push `e` | — | `[t₂, d, e]` |
| 8 | `/` | `[t₂, d, e]` | Pop `e = z`, Pop `d = y`; $i = 3$ | `t₃ := d / e` | `[t₂, t₃]` |
| 9 | `−` | `[t₂, t₃]` | Pop `t₃ = z`, Pop `t₂ = y`; $i = 4$ | `t₄ := t₂ − t₃` | `[t₄]` |

### Final Three Address Code

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= b \times c \\
t_2 &:= a + t_1 \\
t_3 &:= d \,/\, e \\
t_4 &:= t_2 - t_3
\end{aligned}
}
$$

The final result is the value of $t_4$. If the original expression was part of a larger assignment `x := a + b*c - d/e`, then the KTU convention is to add a final copy:

$$
t_5 := t_4
\quad \text{or equivalently} \quad x := t_4
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3: Complex Expression with Parentheses

**Convert** $\;\; (a + b) \times (c - d) / e \;\;$ to **postfix**, then to **TAC**.

### 3.3.1 Infix → Postfix

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | (empty) |
| 2 | `a` | `(` | `a` |
| 3 | `+` | `(+` | `a` |
| 4 | `b` | `(+` | `a b` |
| 5 | `)` | pop `+` | `a b +` |
| 6 | `×` | `×` | `a b +` |
| 7 | `(` | `× (` | `a b +` |
| 8 | `c` | `× (` | `a b + c` |
| 9 | `−` | `× (−` | `a b + c` |
| 10 | `d` | `× (−` | `a b + c d` |
| 11 | `)` | pop `−` | `a b + c d −` |
| 12 | `/` | `/` (since prec(/) = 3 > prec(×) = 3? — `×` is on top with prec = 3 ≥ 3, so pop `×`, push `/`) | `a b + c d − ×` |
| 13 | `e` | `/` | `a b + c d − × e` |
| 14 | (end) | pop `/` | `a b + c d − × e /` |

**Postfix Result:** $\;\; a\,b\,+\,c\,d\,-\,\times\,e\,/\;\;$

### 3.3.2 Postfix → TAC

| Step | Token | Action | TAC Emitted |
|------|-------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | `a` | Push | — |
| 2 | `b` | Push | — |
| 3 | `+` | Pop `b`, `a` | `t₁ := a + b` |
| 4 | `c` | Push | — |
| 5 | `d` | Push | — |
| 6 | `−` | Pop `d`, `c` | `t₂ := c − d` |
| 7 | `×` | Pop `t₂`, `t₁` | `t₃ := t₁ × t₂` |
| 8 | `e` | Push | — |
| 9 | `/` | Pop `e`, `t₃` | `t₄ := t₃ / e` |

**Final TAC:**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= a + b \\
t_2 &:= c - d \\
t_3 &:= t_1 \times t_2 \\
t_4 &:= t_3 \,/\, e
\end{aligned}
}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Complexity & Properties

| Property | Infix | Postfix | TAC |
|----------|-------|---------|-----|
| **Parens required?** | Yes | No | No |
| **Precedence handling** | Implicit (rules) | Implicit (position) | Explicit (separate instr) |
| **Evaluation model** | Recursive / two-pass | Single stack pass | Multi-step register transfer |
| **Length (n operands, k ops)** | $\approx 2n + k$ tokens | $n + k$ tokens | $k$ instructions (with temps) |
| **Parsing direction** | Left-to-right + precedence | Strict left-to-right | Sequential |
| **Compiler use** | Source level | Intermediate | IR / Code-gen input |
| **KTU exam weightage** | — | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

---

## 3.5 Cost Analysis (Optimization Foundation)

> [!NOTE]
> The **number of three-address instructions** for an expression with $n$ operators is **exactly $n$** (each operator → one TAC instruction). This is the basis for instruction-count optimization in code generation.

If the original expression is `result := E` where `E` contains $k$ binary operators, then:

$$
N_{\text{TAC}} = k
$$

$$
N_{\text{temporaries}} = k \;\; (\text{worst case, no common subexpression})
$$

These two equations are the **starting point** for the KTU 2024 code-optimization questions in later modules.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Infix → Postfix State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ReadToken
    ReadToken --> Operand: id / num
    ReadToken --> LeftParen: (
    ReadToken --> RightParen: )
    ReadToken --> Operator: + - * / ^

    Operand --> OutputAppend
    LeftParen --> StackPush
    RightParen --> PopUntilLeftParen
    Operator --> PopHigherPrec: prec(top) >= prec(in)
    Operator --> PushOp: prec(top) < prec(in)

    PopHigherPrec --> PopHigherPrec: still >= prec
    PopHigherPrec --> PushOp
    PopUntilLeftParen --> PopUntilLeftParen: top != (
    PopUntilLeftParen --> DiscardParen: top == (

    OutputAppend --> ReadToken
    StackPush --> ReadToken
    PushOp --> ReadToken
    DiscardParen --> ReadToken

    ReadToken --> EndOfInput: empty
    EndOfInput --> PopAllOps
    PopAllOps --> PopAllOps: stack not empty
    PopAllOps --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Postfix to TAC Translation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Postfix String]) --> Init["Initialize:\nOperand Stack = []\nTemp Counter i = 1"]
    Init --> Scan[Read next token]
    Scan --> CheckOp{Is token\nan operator?}
    CheckOp -- No / Operand --> PushOp[Push operand\nonto stack]
    PushOp --> Scan
    CheckOp -- Yes / Operator --> PopTwo[Pop top two\noperands: z, y]
    PopTwo --> GenTemp["Generate new temp: ti"]
    GenTemp --> Emit["Emit TAC:\nti := y op z"]
    Emit --> PushTemp[Push ti\nonto stack]
    PushTemp --> MoreTokens{More\ntokens?}
    MoreTokens -- Yes --> Scan
    MoreTokens -- No --> Result[Result is at\ntop of stack]
    Result --> End([End: TAC emitted])

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Three Address Code. List any four common forms of three-address instructions with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three Address Code (TAC) is an **intermediate representation** used in compilers where each instruction contains **at most three operands** (one destination and two sources), typically of the form `x := y op z`. (1 mark)

Four common TAC instruction forms:

1. **Binary assignment:** `t1 := a + b` (1/2 mark)
2. **Copy statement:** `t2 := t1` (1/2 mark)
3. **Unconditional jump:** `goto L1` (1/2 mark)
4. **Conditional jump:** `if x < y goto L2` (1/2 mark)

(Additional valid forms: param/call for procedures, indexed copy `x[i] := y`, unary `x := -y`.) **(0.5 marks for overall presentation)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is postfix notation? Why is it preferred for representing intermediate code in compilers?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Postfix notation (also called Reverse Polish Notation, RPN) is a linear representation of an arithmetic expression in which the **operator appears after its operands**. For example, the infix expression `a + b` is written as `a b +` in postfix.

**Why preferred (1.5 marks):**
1. **No parentheses required:** Operator precedence is implicit from the order of tokens.
2. **Single-pass evaluation:** A simple stack machine can evaluate it in **one left-to-right scan** without backtracking.
3. **Direct mapping to TAC:** Each postfix operator corresponds to exactly one three-address instruction, making code generation straightforward.
4. **Unambiguous:** Unlike infix, postfix has no ambiguity regarding precedence or associativity.

---

---

## PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to Dec 2024 ESE pattern]**

**(a)** Convert the following arithmetic expression into **postfix notation** using the Shunting-Yard algorithm. Show the stack contents and output at every step. **(7 marks)**

$$E = (a + b) \times c - d \,/\, (e - f \uparrow g)$$

**(b)** Generate the **Three Address Code (TAC)** for the postfix expression obtained in part (a). Use temporaries `t1, t2, …` and show the stack evolution. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

##### **Part (a): Infix → Postfix** (7 marks)

| Step | Token | Stack | Output |
|------|-------|-------|--------|
| 1 | `(` | `(` | — |
| 2 | `a` | `(` | `a` |
| 3 | `+` | `(+` | `a` |
| 4 | `b` | `(+` | `a b` |
| 5 | `)` | — (pop `+` until `(`) | `a b +` |
| 6 | `×` | `×` | `a b +` |
| 7 | `c` | `×` | `a b + c` |
| 8 | `−` | `−` (pop `×` because prec(×) ≥ prec(−)) | `a b + c ×` |
| 9 | `d` | `−` | `a b + c × d` |
| 10 | `/` | `− /` (prec(/) > prec(−)) | `a b + c × d` |
| 11 | `(` | `− / (` | `a b + c × d` |
| 12 | `e` | `− / (` | `a b + c × d e` |
| 13 | `−` | `− / (−` | `a b + c × d e` |
| 14 | `f` | `− / (−` | `a b + c × d e f` |
| 15 | `↑` | `− / (− ↑` (right-assoc: pop only if prec >, here prec(−) < prec(↑) so no pop) | `a b + c × d e f` |
| 16 | `g` | `− / (− ↑` | `a b + c × d e f g` |
| 17 | `)` | pop `↑` then `−` | `a b + c × d e f g ↑ −` |
| 18 | (end) | pop `/` then `−` | `a b + c × d e f g ↑ − / −` |

**Final Postfix (1 mark):** $\;\; a\,b\,+\,c\,\times\,d\,e\,f\,g\,\uparrow\,-\,/\,-\;\;$ 

> **Marks distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark for correct final postfix + 0.5 mark for each correctly tabulated step (≈12 steps, normalized to 6 marks = 0.5 per step in the stack/output columns).

---

##### **Part (b): Postfix → TAC** (7 marks)

| Step | Token | Stack (before) | Action | TAC Emitted | Stack (after) |
|------|-------|----------------|--------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | `a` | [] | Push `a` | — | [a] |
| 2 | `b` | [a] | Push `b` | — | [a, b] |
| 3 | `+` | [a, b] | Pop `b`, `a`; i=1 | `t1 := a + b` | [t1] |
| 4 | `c` | [t1] | Push `c` | — | [t1, c] |
| 5 | `×` | [t1, c] | Pop `c`, `t1`; i=2 | `t2 := t1 × c` | [t2] |
| 6 | `d` | [t2] | Push `d` | — | [t2, d] |
| 7 | `e` | [t2, d] | Push `e` | — | [t2, d, e] |
| 8 | `f` | [t2, d, e] | Push `f` | — | [t2, d, e, f] |
| 9 | `g` | [t2, d, e, f] | Push `g` | — | [t2, d, e, f, g] |
| 10 | `↑` | [t2, d, e, f, g] | Pop `g`, `f`; i=3 | `t3 := f ↑ g` | [t2, d, e, t3] |
| 11 | `−` | [t2, d, e, t3] | Pop `t3`, `e`; i=4 | `t4 := e − t3` | [t2, d, t4] |
| 12 | `/` | [t2, d, t4] | Pop `t4`, `d`; i=5 | `t5 := d / t4` | [t2, t5] |
| 13 | `−` | [t2, t5] | Pop `t5`, `t2`; i=6 | `t6 := t2 − t5` | [t6] |

**Final TAC (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= a + b \\
t_2 &:= t_1 \times c \\
t_3 &:= f \uparrow g \\
t_4 &:= e - t_3 \\
t_5 &:= d \,/\, t_4 \\
t_6 &:= t_2 - t_5
\end{aligned}
}
$$

**Marks distribution (7 marks):** 1 mark final TAC + 6 marks for the step-by-step table (≈0.5 per row).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question aligned to May 2024 ESE pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Shunting-Yard Algorithm** for converting infix to postfix with a clear operator precedence table. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following expression:

$$F = x \uparrow y + z \times (u - v) \,/\, w$$

Generate the **Three Address Code (TAC)** for `F` directly from the infix expression (without using postfix). Show the order of TAC instructions and the role of each temporary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution Outline

##### **Part (a): Shunting-Yard Algorithm Explanation** (7 marks)

- **Definition & purpose** (1 mark)
- **Data structures used:** operand output queue + operator stack (0.5 mark)
- **Precedence table** (1 mark):

| Op | Prec | Assoc |
|----|------|-------|
| `↑` | 4 | Right |
| `×, /` | 3 | Left |
| `+, −` | 2 | Left |
| `(, )` | 1 | — |

- **Algorithm rules** (2.5 marks):
  - Operand → output
  - `(` → push
  - `)` → pop until `(`
  - Operator → pop while top has higher/equal precedence (respecting right-assoc for `↑`), then push
  - End → pop all
- **Worked example** on a small expression (2 marks)

##### **Part (b): Direct Infix → TAC for F** (7 marks)

Following the standard **semantic action for binary ops** during AST traversal, with operator precedence determining evaluation order:

1. First, evaluate `x ↑ y` → `t1 := x ↑ y`
2. Evaluate `u − v` → `t2 := u − v`
3. Multiply `z × t2` → `t3 := z × t2`
4. Divide by `w` → `t4 := t3 / w`
5. Add `t1` to `t4` → `t5 := t1 + t4`

**Final TAC (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
t_1 &:= x \uparrow y \\
t_2 &:= u - v \\
t_3 &:= z \times t_2 \\
t_4 &:= t_3 \,/\, w \\
t_5 &:= t_1 + t_4
\end{aligned}
}
$$

> **Note for marking:** Many students incorrectly write `t3 := z * u - v` by misinterpreting precedence. The KTU answer key strictly evaluates operator precedence `×, /` (left-associative, equal precedence) and `+, −` (lower precedence) — losing **2 marks** if precedence is violated.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in TAC & Postfix Questions:**
>
> 1. **Popping order error (–2 marks):** When converting postfix to TAC, students often pop in the wrong order: popping `y` first then `z` instead of `z` then `y`. Remember — for `op`, the **top of stack is the right operand (z)** and the **second-from-top is the left operand (y)**.
>
> 2. **Right-associativity of `↑` ignored (–1 mark):** In `a ↑ b ↑ c`, the correct evaluation is `a ↑ (b ↑ c)`, **not** `(a ↑ b) ↑ c`. The Shunting-Yard pop condition for `↑` must be `>` (strict) — not `≥`.
>
> 3. **Missing temporaries (–2 marks):** Each binary operator **must** generate a fresh temporary. Using the same `t1` twice is wrong unless explicitly stated as a common subexpression elimination.
>
> 4. **Final result handling (–1 mark):** If the question says `x := expression`, the last line of TAC should be `x := t_final` (or the temporary holding the result must be assigned to `x`). Don't leave the result on the stack.
>
> 5. **Confusing TAC with Quadruples/Triples:** KTU Module 3 may ask for any of these representations. Always read the question — if it asks for **quadruples**, you must produce a 4-column table `(op, arg1, arg2, result)`.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (Three Address Code & Postfix)**
>
> ✅ TAC has the form `x := y op z` — **at most 3 addresses** per instruction.
>
> ✅ Postfix (RPN) places the **operator after the operands**; no parentheses needed.
>
> ✅ The Shunting-Yard Algorithm uses an **operator stack + output queue** with precedence/associativity rules.
>
> ✅ Operator precedence (high to low): `↑` > `×, /` > `+, −` > `( )`.
>
> ✅ `↑` (exponentiation) is **right-associative** — use `>` not `≥` for popping.
>
> ✅ Postfix → TAC: each operator generates **one new temporary** of the form `ti`.
>
> ✅ Number of TAC instructions = number of operators in the expression.
>
> ✅ Stack evaluation of postfix: **pop two, apply operator, push result**.
>
> ✅ TAC supports: binary ops, unary ops, copy, jumps (`goto`, `if relop goto`), procedure calls (`param`, `call`), indexed access, address/dereference.
>
> ✅ TAC is the **intermediate representation** of choice for most modern compilers (LLVM IR, GCC GIMPLE are based on it).
>
> ✅ Direct Infix → TAC follows the same precedence rules as Shunting-Yard but emits TAC instructions immediately during AST traversal.
>
> ✅ In the KTU 2024 exam: postfix conversion + TAC generation = **14 marks full question** (Part B); definitions & forms = **3-mark Part A**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Code. Intermediate Code as a Synthesized Attribute
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Code. Intermediate Code as a Synthesized Attribute # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT)** for compiler design, an **Intermediate Code** is typically attached to a grammar symbol as a **Synthesized Attribute**. A synthesized attribute is an attribute whose value at a parse-tree node is computed solely from the **attributes of its child nodes** (and, in some restricted forms, from the node's own terminal symbol). When a non-terminal $X$ carries a synthesized attribute named `code` (or `t`, `addr`, `place`), the semantic rule of each production $X \rightarrow X_1 \, X_2 \dots X_n$ is written in the form: $$X.code = f(X_1.code, \, X_2.code, \, \dots, \, X_n.code)$$ Because the value of `code` is *built up* from the leaves toward the root, intermediate code is naturally generated in a **bottom-up** pass — making it perfectly compatible with **LR parsers** and **S-attributed SDDs**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key KTU Definition:** *Intermediate Code as a Synthesized Attribute* means that for every non-terminal, the intermediate representation (usually three-address code, postfix notation, or quadruples) is treated as an attribute that is *synthesized* from the attributes of the production's right-hand-side symbols. The translation is driven entirely by the parse tree from leaves to root. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bottom-Up Recipe Compiler" Imagine a giant kitchen preparing a multi-course meal. Each **station** (vegetable chopper, sauce maker, platter assembler) only knows how to combine whatever the **previous smaller stations** have already prepared: - The leaf station (raw carrot) says: *"I produce chunk-1."* - The next station (chopper + sauce) says: *"Given chunk-1, I produce sauced-1."* - The final head chef says: *"Given sauced-1, sauced-2, sauced-3, I produce the final plated dish."* Each station **does not know** what the parent will do — it only **produces output from the children's output**. That output is the "synthesized attribute" of that station. The final plated dish is the **intermediate code** of the entire program. This is exactly how an LR parser decorates the tree: every parent node *synthesizes* its translation from already-computed children translations. ## 1.3 Standard Forms of Intermediate Code Used in KTU | Form | Notation | Attribute Name (Typical) | Example | |------|----------|--------------------------|---------| | **Postfix Notation** | Operand-first, operator-last | `E.postfix` | `abc*+` | | **Three-Address Code (3AC)** | At most one operator per statement | `E.code` | `t1 = b * c` then `t2 = a + t1` | | **Quadruples** | (op, arg1, arg2, result) | `E.code` | `(+, a, t1, t2)` | | **Triples** | (op, arg1, arg2) | `E.code` | `(+, a, (1))` | > [!NOTE] > The KTU 2024 syllabus focuses on **Three-Address Code (3AC)** and **Postfix** as the most common synthesized attribute targets. The 3AC instruction is the dominant form because it exposes a clean linear flow of temporaries suitable for later optimization phases. ## 1.4 Why "Synthesized" Specifically? (The S in S-Attributed SDD) A Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) in which **every attribute is synthesized** is called an **S-attributed SDD**. Such SDDs have a celebrated property: > [!IMPORTANT] > **S-Attributed SDDs can always be evaluated during a single bottom-up (post-order) traversal of the parse tree — i.e., during LR parsing itself, with no separate post-parse pass.** This is the central reason KTU examiners emphasize this topic: it is the *natural bridge* between **syntax analysis** and the **code generation** phase, and it does not require a separate tree-walking pass. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomy of a Synthesized Attribute Rule A production with semantic rules is written using a colon notation. Consider the canonical arithmetic grammar: $$E \rightarrow E_1 + T \quad \vert \quad E_1 - T \quad \vert \quad T$$ $$T \rightarrow T_1 * F \quad \vert \quad T_1 \, / \, F \quad \vert \quad F$$ $$F \rightarrow ( \, E \, ) \quad \vert \quad id$$ For each production we attach a synthesized-attribute semantic rule. The classic approach generates **three-address code** using fresh temporaries $T_1, T_2, \dots$ via a helper function `newtemp()`. ### 2.1.1 Semantic Rule Table (Synthesized Form) | # | Production | Semantic Rule (Synthesized `code`) | |---|------------|-------------------------------------| | 1 | $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ | `E.code = E1.code \|\| T.code \|\| gen(T1 = E1.addr + T.addr)` | | 2 | $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$ | `E.code = E1.code \|\| T.code \|\| gen(T1 = E1.addr - T.addr)` | | 3 | $E \rightarrow T$ | `E.code = T.code; E.addr = T.addr` | | 4 | $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | `T.code = T1.code \|\| F.code \|\| gen(T1 = T1.addr * F.addr)` | | 5 | $T \rightarrow T_1 \, / \, F$ | `T.code = T1.code \|\| F.code \|\| gen(T1 = T1.addr / F.addr)` | | 6 | $T \rightarrow F$ | `T.code = F.code; T.addr = F.addr` | | 7 | $F \rightarrow ( \, E \, )$ | `F.code = E.code; F.addr = E.addr` | | 8 | $F \rightarrow id$ | `F.code = ""; F.addr = id.lexeme` | > [!NOTE] > Notice two synthesized attributes per non-terminal in some productions: `code` (the string of 3AC instructions) and `addr` (the name/location of the value). Both are **synthesized** because each is computed purely from the children's attributes. ## 2.2 The `gen()` Function and `newtemp()` Counter The function `gen(x = y \ op \ z)` is a side-effect helper that **appends** a new three-address instruction to the growing code string. It is paired with a monotonically increasing counter maintained by: $$newtemp() \rightarrow t_1, t_2, t_3, \dots$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** `newtemp()` is **stateful** — it never reuses a name in the same procedure. Students who forget to call it produce ambiguous code that breaks later optimization phases (e.g., common subexpression elimination). ## 2.3 The "String Concatenation" Synthesis Equation The most general form of the synthesized code attribute for a binary production $X \rightarrow X_1 \ op \ X_2$ is: $$X.code = X_1.code \,\Vert\, X_2.code \,\Vert\, gen(newtemp() = X_1.addr \; op \; X_2.addr)$$ $$X.addr = newtemp()$$ This equation makes the **synthesized** nature explicit: the parent's `code` and `addr` are pure **functions** of the children's `code` and `addr` — no look-up, no inheritance, no parent-to-child flow. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rule Sheet | Concept | Equation / Rule | Notes | |---------|-----------------|-------| | **Synthesized attr. of $X$** | $X.a = f(X_1.a, X_2.a, \dots, X_n.a)$ | Depends only on children | | **3AC instruction** | `x = y op z` | At most one operator per stmt | | **Fresh temp** | $newtemp() \rightarrow t_k$ | $k$ increments globally | | **Code string** | $X.code = X_1.code \,\Vert\, X_2.code \,\Vert\, \text{gen}(\dots)$ | $\Vert$ = string concatenation | | **Postfix synthesis** | $E \rightarrow E_1 + T \;\Rightarrow\; E.postfix = E_1.postfix \,\Vert\, T.postfix \,\Vert\, "+"$ | No temporaries needed | | **E.addr for id** | $F \rightarrow id \;\Rightarrow\; F.addr = id.lexeme$ | Leaf rule | | **S-attributed SDD** | All attrs are synthesized | Evaluated in single post-order pass | | **Quadruple** | $(\text{op}, \arg_1, \arg_2, \text{result})$ | Typical output of `gen()` | ## 2.5 Why Postfix Is the "Cleanest" Synthesized Form For postfix notation, no temporaries are needed at all. The semantic rule collapses to a simple recursion: $$E \rightarrow E_1 + T \;\Rightarrow\; E.postfix = E_1.postfix \,\Vert\, T.postfix \,\Vert\, "+"$$ $$F \rightarrow id \;\Rightarrow\; F.postfix = id.lexeme$$ This means postfix translation is the **most elegant example** of pure synthesized-attribute evaluation in an S-attributed SDD. The KTU module 3 syllabus lists postfix as a foundational example precisely for this reason. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Compiler backends (GCC, LLVM):** Use synthesized-attribute style emission to produce LLVM-IR (a form of 3AC) directly during parsing. - **JIT engines:** Translate AST nodes to bytecode as synthesized attributes on the fly. - **Expression evaluators in calculators/Excel:** Use the same bottom-up synthesis for infix→postfix conversion. - **Hardware synthesis (HLS):** Translating C to VHDL/Verilog uses analogous synthesized attributes per AST node. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Postfix Translation (Synthesized) **Input Expression:** `a + b * c` **Grammar (augmented):** $$E \rightarrow E + T \;\vert\; T$$ $$T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$$ $$F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$$ **Semantic Rules (Synthesized, Postfix):** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E_1 + T \;&:\quad E.postfix &= E_1.postfix \,\Vert\, T.postfix \,\Vert\, "+" \\ E &\rightarrow T \;&:\quad E.postfix &= T.postfix \\ T &\rightarrow T_1 * F \;&:\quad T.postfix &= T_1.postfix \,\Vert\, F.postfix \,\Vert\, "*" \\ T &\rightarrow F \;&:\quad T.postfix &= F.postfix \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \;&:\quad F.postfix &= E.postfix \\ F &\rightarrow id \;&:\quad F.postfix &= id.lexeme \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.1.1 Bottom-Up Derivation of `a + b * c` We trace the parser's reductions and the corresponding synthesized-attribute evaluation: | Step | Stack (with attrs) | Production | Action | |------|-------------------|------------|--------| | 1 | `id (postfix="a")` | $F \rightarrow id$ | reduce; $F.postfix = "a"$ | | 2 | `F (postfix="a")` | $T \rightarrow F$ | reduce; $T.postfix = "a"$ | | 3 | `E (postfix="a")` | $E \rightarrow T$ | reduce; $E.postfix = "a"$ | | 4 | `id (postfix="b")` | $F \rightarrow id$ | reduce; $F.postfix = "b"$ | | 5 | `F (postfix="b")` | $T \rightarrow F$ | reduce; $T.postfix = "b"$ | | 6 | `id (postfix="c")` | $F \rightarrow id$ | reduce; $F.postfix = "c"$ | | 7 | `F (postfix="c")` | $T \rightarrow T*F$ | reduce; $T.postfix = "b" ‖ "c" ‖ "*" = "bc*"` | | 8 | `E (postfix="a") , T (postfix="bc*")` | $E \rightarrow E+T$ | reduce; $E.postfix = "a" ‖ "bc*" ‖ "+" = "abc*+"` | **Final synthesized postfix:** `abc*+` ### 3.1.2 Algebraic Synthesis at the Root $$ \begin{aligned} E.postfix &= E_1.postfix \,\Vert\, T.postfix \,\Vert\, "+" \\ &= "a" \,\Vert\, (T_1.postfix \,\Vert\, F.postfix \,\Vert\, "*") \,\Vert\, "+" \\ &= "a" \,\Vert\, ("b" \,\Vert\, "c" \,\Vert\, "*") \,\Vert\, "+" \\ &= "a" \,\Vert\, "bc*" \,\Vert\, "+" \\ &= "abc*+" \end{aligned} $$ Every concatenation in this chain is the **synthesized** computation: the parent's attribute is built by stitching together the children's attributes with the operator appended. ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Three-Address Code (Synthesized) **Input Expression:** `a + b * c` We use a second synthesized attribute `addr` (the name of the temporary or variable holding the value) alongside `code`. ### 3.2.1 Semantic Rules (Synthesized 3AC) $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E_1 + T \;&:\quad E.code &= E_1.code \,\Vert\, T.code \,\Vert\, gen(E.addr = E_1.addr + T.addr) \\ E &\rightarrow T \;&:\quad E.code &= T.code; \quad E.addr = T.addr \\ T &\rightarrow T_1 * F \;&:\quad T.code &= T_1.code \,\Vert\, F.code \,\Vert\, gen(T.addr = T_1.addr * F.addr) \\ T &\rightarrow F \;&:\quad T.code &= F.code; \quad T.addr = F.addr \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \;&:\quad F.code &= E.code; \quad F.addr = E.addr \\ F &\rightarrow id \;&:\quad F.code &= ""; \quad F.addr = id.lexeme \end{aligned} $$ ### 3.2.2 Annotated Parse Tree with Synthesized Attributes ``` [E] code = t1 = a + t2 addr = t1 / | \ [E] + [T] code = "" code = t2 = b * c addr = a addr = t2 | / | \ [T] [T] * [F] code = "" code = "" code = "" addr = a addr = b addr = c | | | [F] [F] id (c) code = "" addr = a | id (a) ``` ### 3.2.3 Bottom-Up Evaluation of Synthesized `code` | Reduction | Computed synthesized `code` (cumulative) | Computed `addr` | |-----------|------------------------------------------|------------------| | $F \rightarrow id\;(a)$ | `""` | `a` | | $T \rightarrow F$ | `""` | `a` | | $E \rightarrow T$ | `""` | `a` | | $F \rightarrow id\;(b)$ | `""` | `b` | | $T \rightarrow F$ | `""` | `b` | | $F \rightarrow id\;(c)$ | `""` | `c` | | $T \rightarrow T*F$ | `gen(t2 = b * c)` → `t2 = b * c` | `t2` | | $E \rightarrow E+T$ | `gen(t1 = a + t2)` → `t1 = a + t2` | `t1` | ### 3.2.4 Final Synthesized 3AC Output $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Produced code stream} &= E_1.code \,\Vert\, T.code \,\Vert\, gen(t_1 = a + t_2) \\ &= \epsilon \,\Vert\, (t_2 = b * c) \,\Vert\, (t_1 = a + t_2) \\ &= \boxed{t_2 = b * c \; ; \; t_1 = a + t_2} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > Observe that `E.code` for the left child `E_1` was `""` because it reduced to a single identifier `a` — no new 3AC was needed. **The synthesized code attribute is cumulative; it grows strictly monotonically as reductions occur bottom-up.** ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Synthesized vs. Other Attribute Classes | Property | Synthesized | Inherited | Synthetic (L-attributed) | |----------|-------------|-----------|--------------------------| | **Dependency direction** | Children → Parent | Parent → Children (or siblings, left-to-right) | Top-down, left-to-right (general) | | **Evaluation order** | Post-order (bottom-up) | Pre-order / mixed | Depth-first, left-to-right | | **LR parser compatible** | ✅ Yes (S-attributed) | ❌ No (need separate pass) | ❌ No (need separate pass) | | **Implementation** | Stack-based, inline with reductions | Tree-walk after parsing | Tree-walk with explicit dependency tracking | | **KTU relevance for intermediate code** | **⭐ High — primary mechanism** | Low (used for symbol-table contexts) | Medium (for type-checking) | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU's choice of *synthesized* attribute for intermediate code is **not arbitrary**: only synthesized attributes can be evaluated **on the fly during LR reduction** without an extra pass. This is a classic KTU 2-mark favorite question. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Parse Tree with Synthesized `code` Attribute Flow ```mermaid graph TD nodeE0["E
code = t1 = a + t2
addr = t1"] nodeE1["E1
code = EMPTY
addr = a"] nodePlus["+
terminal"] nodeT1["T
code = t2 = b * c
addr = t2"] nodeT2["T2
code = EMPTY
addr = b"] nodeStar["*
terminal"] nodeF1["F
code = EMPTY
addr = c"] nodeFA["F
code = EMPTY
addr = a"] nodeFB["F
code = EMPTY
addr = b"] nodeIDA["id a
terminal"] nodeIDB["id b
terminal"] nodeIDC["id c
terminal"] nodeE0 --> nodeE1 nodeE0 --> nodePlus nodeE0 --> nodeT1 nodeT1 --> nodeT2 nodeT1 --> nodeStar nodeT1 --> nodeF1 nodeE1 --> nodeFA nodeFA --> nodeIDA nodeT2 --> nodeFB nodeFB --> nodeIDB nodeF1 --> nodeIDC ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The arrows point **upward** in evaluation order (synthesized = bottom-up). Each parent node's `code` field is *constructed* from the children's `code` fields, then augmented with one `gen(...)` call at the parent. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Synthesized-Attribute Translation Pipeline ```mermaid graph LR srcA["Lexical Stream
a + b * c"]:::input stackTop["Yacc LALR Stack
Bottom-up reductions"]:::parser semAct["Semantic Action
Triggered on Reduction"]:::action genUnit["gen() Unit
+ newtemp()"]:::gen tcode["3AC Code Buffer
Growing string"]:::buffer outCode["Final Intermediate Code
t2 = b * c
t1 = a + t2"]:::output srcA --> stackTop stackTop -- "reduce by production" --> semAct semAct -- "concatenate children .code" --> genUnit genUnit -- "emit three-address instr" --> tcode tcode --> outCode classDef input fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000 classDef parser fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef action fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#842029,color:#000 classDef gen fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000 classDef buffer fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#41464b,color:#000 classDef output fill:#cff4fc,stroke:#055160,color:#000 ``` > **Pipeline interpretation:** > 1. **Lexical stream** feeds the **LALR stack** (bottom-up parser). > 2. On every **reduce** action, the **semantic action** fires. > 3. The action calls **gen()**, which uses **newtemp()** to mint a fresh temporary. > 4. The instruction is **appended** to the **3AC buffer** (the synthesized `code` attribute of the parent). > 5. When parsing ends, the root's `code` attribute is the **final intermediate program**. ## 4.3 Attribute Dependency Graph for Production $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ ```mermaid graph LR E1Code["E1.code"]:::leaf E1Addr["E1.addr"]:::leaf TCode["T.code"]:::leaf TAddr["T.addr"]:::leaf newTempBox["newtemp() → t_k"]:::helper genBox["gen(t_k = E1.addr + T.addr)"]:::helper ECode["E.code (synthesized)"]:::root EAddr["E.addr (synthesized)"]:::root E1Code -- "concatenate" --> ECode TCode -- "concatenate" --> ECode genBox -- "append" --> ECode E1Addr -- "arg1" --> genBox TAddr -- "arg2" --> genBox newTempBox -- "result name" --> genBox newTempBox -- "set" --> EAddr classDef leaf fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0b5ed7,color:#000 classDef helper fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef root fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the graph:** All four leaf attributes (`E1.code`, `E1.addr`, `T.code`, `T.addr`) flow *into* the parent `E`. The parent's `E.code` and `E.addr` are computed strictly from these inputs. **No edge ever points downward** — this is the defining signature of a synthesized attribute. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Q1. Define a synthesized attribute in the context of syntax-directed translation. Why is intermediate code most naturally attached as a synthesized attribute? `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2023, Model Question Paper]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **synthesized attribute** is an attribute of a grammar symbol whose value at a parse-tree node is computed **solely from the attributes of its child nodes** (and possibly the node's own terminal symbol). Intermediate code is attached as a synthesized attribute because: 1. **(1 mark)** The translation of a sub-expression or sub-statement depends only on the translations of its constituent sub-units, which are its children in the parse tree. 2. **(1 mark)** Synthesized attributes can be evaluated in a **single bottom-up (post-order) pass**, which is precisely the natural evaluation order of an **LR parser** during reduction. 3. **(1 mark)** This allows **S-attributed SDDs** to emit three-address code **on the fly during parsing**, with no separate tree-walk pass — saving both time and memory. --- ### Q2. Differentiate between synthesized and inherited attributes. State one production where an inherited attribute is unavoidable. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Synthesized | Inherited | |--------|-------------|-----------| | **Dependency** | Children → Parent | Parent → Children (or left-sibling → right-sibling) | | **Evaluation order** | Bottom-up (post-order) | Top-down / mixed | | **LR parser compatibility** | Yes — directly during reductions | No — requires separate tree pass | | **Use in intermediate code** | Primary (code, addr) | Rare (type, scope) | **(1 mark)** Synthesized attributes flow **upward**; inherited attributes flow **downward or sideways**. **(1 mark)** Synthesized attributes can be evaluated by an LR parser without an additional pass; inherited attributes cannot. **(1 mark)** Example of unavoidable inherited attribute: $T \rightarrow T_1 * F_2$ with a rule $F_2.type = T_1.type$ — the type of the right operand must be *inherited* from the left sibling because the right `F` does not appear in a context where it can synthesize the type from its own children alone. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module-Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Answer **any ONE full question** from each choice-pair. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) #### Q.A(a) `[7 Marks]` Design a Syntax-Directed Definition (SDD) with synthesized attributes that translates the expression grammar into **postfix notation**. Apply it to translate `a * (b + c) - d` and show the annotated parse tree. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Grammar:** $$E \rightarrow E - T \;\vert\; T$$ $$T \rightarrow T * F \;\vert\; F$$ $$F \rightarrow (E) \;\vert\; id$$ **Step 2 — Synthesized Semantic Rules:** | # | Production | Synthesized Rule | |---|------------|------------------| | 1 | $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$ | $E.postfix = E_1.postfix \,\Vert\, T.postfix \,\Vert\, "-"$ | | 2 | $E \rightarrow T$ | $E.postfix = T.postfix$ | | 3 | $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ | $T.postfix = T_1.postfix \,\Vert\, F.postfix \,\Vert\, "*"$ | | 4 | $T \rightarrow F$ | $T.postfix = F.postfix$ | | 5 | $F \rightarrow (E)$ | $F.postfix = E.postfix$ | | 6 | $F \rightarrow id$ | $F.postfix = id.lexeme$ | **Step 3 — Bottom-Up Reduction for `a * (b + c) - d`:** | Step | Reduction | Synthesized $postfix$ | |------|-----------|------------------------| | 1 | $F \rightarrow id\;(a)$ | `"a"` | | 2 | $T \rightarrow F$ | `"a"` | | 3 | $F \rightarrow id\;(b)$ | `"b"` | | 4 | $F \rightarrow id\;(c)$ | `"c"` | | 5 | $E \rightarrow T$ (on `c`) | `"c"` | | 6 | $T \rightarrow F$ (on `c`) | `"c"` | | 7 | $E \rightarrow E_1+T$ (`b + c`) | `"bc+"` | | 8 | $T \rightarrow T_1 * F$ (`a * (b+c)`) | `"abc+*"` | | 9 | $E \rightarrow T$ | `"abc+*"` | | 10 | $F \rightarrow id\;(d)$ | `"d"` | | 11 | $T \rightarrow F$ | `"d"` | | 12 | $E \rightarrow E_1 - T$ | `"abc+*d-"` | **Final postfix:** `abc+*d-` **Step 4 — Annotated Parse Tree (synthesized `postfix` shown at each node):** ``` [E] postfix = abc+*d- / | \ [E1] - [T] postfix postfix =abc+* =d | [T] postfix=abc+* / | \ [T1] * [F] postfix postfix =a =bc+ | | [F] [F] postfix=a postfix=bc+ | | id(a) (E) | [E] postfix=bc+ / | \ [E1] + [T] postfix postfix =b =c | | [F] [F] postfix postfix =b =c | | id(b) id(c) ``` **Step 5 — Verification by RPN evaluation:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Stack trace for } abc+*d-: & \\ a &\rightarrow [a] \\ b &\rightarrow [a, b] \\ c &\rightarrow [a, b, c] \\ + &\rightarrow [a, (b+c)] \\ * &\rightarrow [a*(b+c)] \\ d &\rightarrow [a*(b+c), d] \\ - &\rightarrow [a*(b+c) - d] \;\;\checkmark \end{aligned} $$ --- #### Q.A(b) `[7 Marks]` Augment the same SDD to emit **three-address code (3AC)** with temporaries, and produce the 3AC for the same expression `a * (b + c) - d`. Use `newtemp()` for fresh temporaries. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Augmented Synthesized Rules (3AC):** $$ \begin{aligned} E &\rightarrow E_1 - T \;&:\quad E.code &= E_1.code \,\Vert\, T.code \,\Vert\, gen(E.addr = E_1.addr - T.addr) \\ E &\rightarrow T \;&:\quad E.code &= T.code; \; E.addr = T.addr \\ T &\rightarrow T_1 * F \;&:\quad T.code &= T_1.code \,\Vert\, F.code \,\Vert\, gen(T.addr = T_1.addr * F.addr) \\ T &\rightarrow F \;&:\quad T.code &= F.code; \; T.addr = F.addr \\ F &\rightarrow (E) \;&:\quad F.code &= E.code; \; F.addr = E.addr \\ F &\rightarrow id \;&:\quad F.code &= ""; \; F.addr = id.lexeme \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Bottom-Up Synthesis for `a * (b + c) - d`:** | Reduction | New 3AC emitted | Resulting `addr` | |-----------|------------------|-------------------| | $F \rightarrow id\;(a)$ | — | $a$ | | $T \rightarrow F$ | — | $a$ | | $F \rightarrow id\;(b)$ | — | $b$ | | $F \rightarrow id\;(c)$ | — | $c$ | | $E \rightarrow T$ (c) | — | $c$ | | $T \rightarrow F$ (c) | — | $c$ | | $E \rightarrow E+T$ | `t1 = b + c` | $t_1$ | | $T \rightarrow T*F$ | `t2 = a * t1` | $t_2$ | | $E \rightarrow T$ | — | $t_2$ | | $F \rightarrow id\;(d)$ | — | $d$ | | $T \rightarrow F$ | — | $d$ | | $E \rightarrow E-T$ | `t3 = t2 - d` | $t_3$ | **Step 3 — Final Synthesized 3AC Output:** $$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= b + c \\ t_2 &= a * t_1 \\ t_3 &= t_2 - d \end{aligned} }$$ > Each line was *synthesized* exactly at the moment of the corresponding reduction, with the parent's `code` attribute being the concatenation of the children's `code` plus one fresh `gen(...)` call. --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Q.B(a) `[7 Marks]` What is an **S-attributed SDD**? Explain with a suitable grammar and semantic rules how an S-attributed SDD generates three-address code for a sequence of assignment statements of the form `id = id + id` and `id = id * id`. Write the synthesized `code` and `addr` rules. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Definition of S-attributed SDD (2 marks):** An **S-attributed SDD** is a Syntax-Directed Definition in which **every attribute of every non-terminal is a synthesized attribute**. As a consequence, the entire SDD can be evaluated by a **single bottom-up (post-order) traversal** of the parse tree, which means it can be executed **directly during an LR parse reduction** without any additional tree-walk pass. **Step 2 — Grammar (3 marks):** $$S \rightarrow A \, S \;\vert\; \varepsilon$$ $$A \rightarrow id \; = \; E \; ;$$ $$E \rightarrow E + id \;\vert\; E * id \;\vert\; id$$ **Step 3 — Synthesized Semantic Rules (2 marks):** | Production | Synthesized Rules | |------------|-------------------| | $S \rightarrow A\,S_1$ | $S.code = A.code \,\Vert\, S_1.code$ | | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | $S.code = ""$ | | $A \rightarrow id = E ;$ | $A.code = E.code \,\Vert\, gen(id.lexeme = E.addr)$ | | $E \rightarrow E_1 + id$ | $E.code = E_1.code \,\Vert\, gen(E.addr = E_1.addr + id.lexeme)$ | | $E \rightarrow E_1 * id$ | $E.code = E_1.code \,\Vert\, gen(E.addr = E_1.addr * id.lexeme)$ | | $E \rightarrow id$ | $E.code = ""; \; E.addr = id.lexeme$ | --- #### Q.B(b) `[7 Marks]` Trace the SDD from Q.B(a) on the input program `x = a + b ; y = a * b ;` Show the synthesized 3AC output and the annotated reductions. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Parse & Reduce `x = a + b ;`:** | # | Reduction | Synthesized `code` emitted | `addr` | |---|-----------|----------------------------|--------| | 1 | $E \rightarrow id\;(a)$ | `""` | `a` | | 2 | $E \rightarrow E_1 + id\;(b)$ | `t1 = a + b` | `t1` | | 3 | $A \rightarrow id(x) = E ;$ | `x = t1` | — | | 4 | $S \rightarrow A S_1$ | buffer now `[x = t1]` | — | **Step 2 — Parse & Reduce `y = a * b ;`:** | # | Reduction | Synthesized `code` emitted | `addr` | |---|-----------|----------------------------|--------| | 5 | $E \rightarrow id\;(a)$ | `""` | `a` | | 6 | $E \rightarrow E_1 * id\;(b)$ | `t2 = a * b` | `t2` | | 7 | $A \rightarrow id(y) = E ;$ | `y = t2` | — | | 8 | $S \rightarrow A S_1$ | buffer grows | — | | 9 | $S \rightarrow \varepsilon$ | finalize | — | **Step 3 — Final Synthesized 3AC:** $$\boxed{ \begin{aligned} t_1 &= a + b \\ x &= t_1 \\ t_2 &= a * b \\ y &= t_2 \end{aligned} }$$ > Notice that the **sequence of `S` non-terminals** stitched the two assignments together by simply concatenating the synthesized `code` strings of each child — a perfect illustration of the synthesized-attribute accumulation pattern. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Compiler Design Module 3):** > > 1. **Confusing synthesized with inherited:** Students often write $E \rightarrow E_1 + T$ with the rule $E_1.code = E.code \,\Vert\, \dots$ — this is *inherited*, not synthesized. **Synthesized rules must have the LHS attribute on the left of `=` only.** > > 2. **Forgetting to call `newtemp()`:** When a student writes the rule as `gen(a + b)` without a fresh temporary, the resulting "3AC" degenerates into two-operand code, breaking later optimization. **Always mint a new temporary on the LHS of a `gen(...)` call.** > > 3. **Order of concatenation reversed:** $X.code = gen(\dots) \,\Vert\, X_1.code \,\Vert\, X_2.code$ produces instructions in *reverse* order. The correct order is **children-first, then the new instruction** at the parent: $X.code = X_1.code \,\Vert\, X_2.code \,\Vert\, gen(\dots)$. > > 4. **Missing leaf rules:** Forgetting $F \rightarrow id \;:\; F.code = "";\; F.addr = id.lexeme$ — without this, the entire chain has no base case and the synthesized attribute is undefined at the leaves. > > 5. **Using `;` or `,` as concatenation operator:** KTU expects the **$\Vert$ symbol or the literal word "concatenation"** — using semicolons is ambiguous and will cost you the 1-mark semantic-clarity point. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Synthesized attribute** = attribute computed from **children's attributes only**, with dependency flow **upward** in the parse tree. - ✅ **S-attributed SDD** = SDD where **all** attributes are synthesized; evaluable in a **single bottom-up (post-order) pass**. - ✅ **Intermediate code as a synthesized attribute** is the standard KTU Module 3 mechanism because it lets the compiler emit 3AC **directly during LR reductions** without a separate tree-walk. - ✅ Two common synthesized attributes for code emission: **`code`** (the cumulative 3AC string) and **`addr`** (the name of the temporary or variable holding the value). - ✅ Canonical semantic rule for a binary production $X \rightarrow X_1 \, op \, X_2$: $$X.code = X_1.code \,\Vert\, X_2.code \,\Vert\, gen(newtemp() = X_1.addr \; op \; X_2.addr)$$ - ✅ Postfix translation needs **no temporaries**: $E.postfix = E_1.postfix \,\Vert\, T.postfix \,\Vert\, op$ — the cleanest illustration of pure synthesized evaluation. - ✅ `newtemp()` is **stateful and global** within a procedure; never reuses a name. - ✅ `gen(x = y \ op \ z)` is the **side-effecting** helper that **appends** one 3AC instruction to the synthesized code string. - ✅ **Order matters:** always concatenate children's `.code` *first*, then append the freshly generated parent instruction. - ✅ **Leaf rules are mandatory:** every `id` terminal must have a base rule producing `F.code = ""; F.addr = id.lexeme`. - ✅ Synthesized attributes **cannot be evaluated by a top-down (LL) parser on the fly** — they need the post-order LR-style reduction. This is why KTU links this topic to LALR(1) parsers (Module 2) and 3AC generation (Module 3). - ✅ The **synthesized code attribute is monotone and cumulative** — it grows at every reduction and is finalized at the root of the parse tree.

topics.title: Principal Sources of Code Optimization, Classification of Optimizations, Data Structures and Implementation Techniques for Optimizations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECMT527 - INTRODUCTION TO COMPILER DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Principal Sources of Code Optimization, Classification of Optimizations, Data Structures and Implementation Techniques for Optimizations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Code Optimization

**Code Optimization** is a phase in the compilation process that attempts to improve the intermediate code (or target code) so that the resulting program runs **faster**, consumes **less memory**, and/or uses **less power**, while preserving the **semantic equivalence** of the original program.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, code optimization is a *machine-independent* analysis applied at the intermediate representation (IR) level, primarily between the intermediate code generation phase and the final code generation phase, though it can also be applied at the source level and target level.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):**
> "Code optimization is the application of a set of transformations to a program that preserves the program's input–output behavior while reducing its execution time, memory footprint, or energy consumption, as measured by chosen engineering metrics."

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Semantic Preservation:**
> An optimization **must not change the observable behavior** of a program. That is, for the same input, the optimized program must produce the same output and side effects as the original.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **chef** preparing a complex dish in a busy restaurant kitchen.

- **Without optimization:** You walk back and forth between the fridge, the sink, and the stove for *every single ingredient* of *every single dish*, even if two dishes need the same chopped onions. You re-chop onions 10 times in a day.

- **With optimization:**
  - You **chop onions in bulk** once (*Common Subexpression Elimination*).
  - You use a **food processor** instead of a hand knife to chop, which is *faster* (*Strength Reduction*).
  - You **don't buy ingredients** that aren't on any recipe (*Dead Code Elimination*).
  - You **delegate** the salad preparation to an assistant while the main dish is cooking (*Instruction Scheduling / Parallelism*).
  - You **don't wash the same bowl** 5 times in a row (*Redundant Store/Load Elimination*).

The *output dish* (the program's result) is **identical**, but the *process* (execution) is now **leaner, faster, and more efficient**. This is exactly what the optimizer does to your three-address code.

---

### 1.3 Why Code Optimization Matters — Engineering Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Examiners often award 1 mark for explicitly stating the *goal* of optimization. Memorize these four measurable metrics:

| Metric | Symbol (Typical) | Goal of Optimizer |
|---|---|---|
| Execution Time | $T_{exec}$ | **Minimize** |
| Memory Footprint | $M_{code}$ | **Minimize** |
| Energy / Power | $E_{cpu}$ | **Minimize** |
| Code Size | $S_{code}$ | **Minimize** (for embedded) |

The **compilation time overhead** is a *cost* the optimizer tries to keep bounded — an optimization that takes 10 hours to make a program 1% faster is not worth it.

---

### 1.4 Position of Optimizer in the Compiler Pipeline

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places optimization **after intermediate code generation** and **before target code generation** in the standard compiler architecture. This is the most common placement for *machine-independent* optimization.

**Source Code → Lexical Analysis → Syntax Analysis → Semantic Analysis → Intermediate Code Generation → [OPTIMIZATION PHASE] → Target Code Generation → Target Code**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Principal Sources of Code Optimization (Module 4 — Core Topic)

The "principal sources" refer to the **recurring patterns** in code that the optimizer recognizes and rewrites. KTU examiners expect a structured list with one-line definitions and a one-line example for each.

#### 2.1.1 Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE)

**Definition:** If an expression `E` has been **previously computed** and **none of its operands have changed** since, then the previous computation can be reused instead of recomputing `E`.

**Why it works:** Many programs recompute the same arithmetic expression inside loops or across straight-line code blocks. The computation result is *unchanged*, so the recomputation is *wasteful*.

**Example:**
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = a + b        // <-- common subexpression; reuse t1
t3 = t1 * c
t4 = t2 * c       // <-- same as t3; replace with t3
```

After CSE:
```
t1 = a + b
t2 = t1
t3 = t1 * c
t4 = t3
```

#### 2.1.2 Copy Propagation

**Definition:** After an assignment `x = y`, subsequent uses of `x` can be replaced with `y`, provided `x` and `y` are not redefined between the assignment and the use.

**Why it works:** It cascades other optimizations — once copies are propagated, dead stores (assignments to variables whose values are never read) become visible and can be eliminated.

**Example:**
```
x = y
... use(x) ...
```
becomes
```
x = y
... use(y) ...
```
and if `x` is never used again, the assignment `x = y` itself becomes **dead code**.

#### 2.1.3 Dead Code Elimination

**Definition:** Code that **computes a value never used** (an unused variable, a write to a variable never read, code after a `return`) is removed.

**Forms of dead code:**
- **Dead variable:** A variable assigned but never read.
- **Dead statement:** Unreachable code (after unconditional `return`, `break`, `goto`).
- **Dead expression:** A computation whose result is discarded (e.g., statement-level expression in C with `void` context).

#### 2.1.4 Constant Folding

**Definition:** Expressions whose operands are **all compile-time constants** are evaluated *at compile time*, replacing the expression with its constant result.

**Example:**
```
int x = 2 + 3 * 4;
```
becomes
```
int x = 14;
```

#### 2.1.5 Constant Propagation

**Definition:** If a variable has been assigned a constant value, and that assignment is the **only reaching definition** at a use-site, replace the use with the constant.

**Example:**
```
x = 5
y = x + 2
```
becomes
```
x = 5
y = 7
```
…and then `x` becomes dead (Dead Code Elimination can follow).

#### 2.1.6 Strength Reduction

**Definition:** Replace an **expensive operation** with an **equivalent cheaper operation**.

**Classic replacements:**

| Original Expensive Operation | Replacement (Cheaper) |
|---|---|
| Multiplication by a power of 2 | Left shift (`x * 2^k` → `x << k`) |
| Division by a power of 2 | Right shift (`x / 2^k` → `x >> k`) |
| Multiplication by a constant | Sequence of shifts and adds (e.g., `x * 5` → `(x << 2) + x`) |
| Exponentiation | Repeated multiplication loop |
| Function calls in hot loops | Inline expansion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Rule:** The canonical strength-reduction question in KTU exams is *converting `x * 2` to `x << 1`*. Always state: *"on most modern CPUs, a shift is faster than a multiply because it bypasses the integer multiplier unit."*

#### 2.1.7 Loop Optimization Techniques

These are the **most impactful** optimizations because programs spend ~90% of execution time in loops (the famous *90/10 rule* / Amdahl's Law).

##### (a) Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)

**Definition:** A computation inside a loop whose **result does not change** across loop iterations is **hoisted** out of the loop (placed in a *preheader*).

**Example:**
```
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    t = a + b;        // <-- loop invariant: a and b don't change
    x[i] = t * i;
}
```
After LICM:
```
t = a + b;            // <-- hoisted to preheader
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    x[i] = t * i;
}
```

##### (b) Induction Variable Elimination (IVE)

**Definition:** A variable whose value on each iteration is a **linear function of the loop counter** is an *induction variable*. These can sometimes be eliminated because the loop counter itself can be used.

**Example:**
```
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    j = 2 * i;        // <-- j is an induction variable
    A[j] = A[j] + 1;  // <-- can be rewritten using i instead
}
```
After IVE (strength reduction applied first):
```
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    A[2*i] = A[2*i] + 1;
}
j = 2*i computation is broken into a step-and-add pattern: `j = 0; j += 2;` per iteration.

##### (c) Loop Unrolling

**Definition:** Replicate the loop body multiple times, reducing loop overhead (branch + counter-update cost per iteration).

**Example:** A loop with 4 iterations unrolled by 2:
```
for (i = 0; i < n; i++) A[i] = B[i] + 1;
```
becomes (conceptually):
```
for (i = 0; i < n; i += 2) {
    A[i] = B[i] + 1;
    A[i+1] = B[i+1] + 1;
}
```

##### (d) Loop Fusion (Jamming) and Loop Fission (Distribution)

- **Loop Fusion:** Combine two adjacent loops over the same index range into one loop → better cache locality.
- **Loop Fission:** Split one loop into two → better vectorization or to break data dependencies.

#### 2.1.8 Algebraic Simplification / Identity Elimination

Replace operations using **algebraic identities**:

| Identity | Replacement |
|---|---|
| $x + 0$ | $x$ |
| $x - 0$ | $x$ |
| $x \cdot 1$ | $x$ |
| $x \cdot 0$ | $0$ (if no side effects) |
| $x \cdot 2^k$ | $x \ll k$ |
| $x / 1$ | $x$ |
| $x \land \text{true}$ | $x$ |
| $x \lor \text{false}$ | $x$ |

#### 2.1.9 Tail Call Optimization

If a function call is the **last action** in a function, the call can be replaced with a **jump** to the callee, saving stack frame setup.

#### 2.1.10 Function Inlining

Replace a **function call** with the **body of the function** to eliminate call overhead and enable further optimization across the call boundary.

---

### 2.2 Classification of Optimizations

KTU 2024 expects students to **classify optimizations along multiple orthogonal axes**. The following three axes are the most important.

#### 2.2.1 Axis 1: Machine-Dependent vs Machine-Independent

| Property | Machine-Independent | Machine-Dependent |
|---|---|---|
| **Where applied** | On intermediate code (IR) | On target machine code |
| **Requires knowledge of** | Program semantics only | Specific CPU registers, instruction latencies, pipeline, memory hierarchy |
| **Examples** | CSE, constant folding, LICM, copy propagation, dead code elimination | Peephole optimization, register allocation, instruction scheduling, branch prediction hints |
| **Portability** | Portable across targets | Must be re-tuned for each target |
| **KTU Example Question** | "Give two examples of machine-independent optimizations." (2 marks) | "Why is register allocation a machine-dependent optimization?" (3 marks) |

> [!NOTE]
> **A common KTU pitfall:** "Strength reduction" can be BOTH machine-independent (replacing `x*2` with `x+x` at IR level) AND machine-dependent (replacing `x*2` with `x<<1` knowing the target supports fast shifts). Examiners accept either, but you must state the level.

#### 2.2.2 Axis 2: Local vs Global vs Interprocedural

This is the **most heavily tested** classification in KTU exams (Dec 2023, June 2024, model papers).

| Scope | Boundary | Information Used | Data Structure | Example Optimizations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | Within a single basic block | Single block's three-address code | DAG of the block | Local CSE, constant folding within block, basic peephole |
| **Global (Intraprocedural)** | Within a single function/procedure, across basic blocks | Control-flow graph of the function | Flow graph, dominator tree, ud/du chains | LICM, global CSE, copy propagation, constant propagation, dead code elimination |
| **Interprocedural (Whole-Program)** | Across function/procedure boundaries | Call graph, side-effect summaries | Call graph, summary edges | Function inlining, interprocedural constant propagation, dead procedure elimination |

**Definitions:**

- **Local optimization:** Operates on a *single basic block*. Does not consider control flow into or out of the block.
- **Global optimization:** Operates on an *entire function* by analyzing the function's control-flow graph. Must respect reaching definitions and live variable information.
- **Interprocedural optimization:** Operates *across the whole program*, considering interactions between procedures (call/return, parameter passing, side effects).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (3 marks lost):**
> "Local" does **not** mean "small" or "intra-procedural". It specifically means *within a single basic block*. Saying "local optimization is optimization done locally in the source file" is a **classic wrong answer** that fetches 0 marks.

#### 2.2.3 Axis 3: Function-Preserving Transformations

A **function-preserving transformation** modifies a program such that, for the same inputs, the program's output and observable side effects are identical. Almost all standard optimizations are function-preserving.

The only category that is *not* function-preserving (and therefore requires user opt-in) is **non-function-preserving** optimizations such as:
- Floating-point reassociation (changes rounding behavior)
- Integer overflow exploitation (deliberate wraparound)

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exams, default to stating that optimizations are *function-preserving transformations*. This single line often fetches 1 mark.

#### 2.2.4 Special Class: Peephole Optimization (Asked Frequently)

A **peephole optimization** examines a small sliding window (the "peephole", typically 2–5 instructions) of target code and replaces inefficient patterns with efficient ones. It is a *local* and *machine-dependent* transformation.

**Common peephole patterns:**

1. **Redundant load/store elimination**
   ```
   MOV R1, a
   MOV a, R1
   ```
   → The second `MOV` is redundant; remove it.

2. **Constant folding** (at target level)
   ```
   MUL R1, R1, 2
   ```
   → If R1 is known constant, fold at compile time.

3. **Strength reduction at target level**
   ```
   MUL R1, R2, 2
   ```
   → `SHL R1, R2, 1`

4. **Null sequence elimination**
   ```
   ADD R1, R1, 0
   ```
   → Remove the `ADD` (no-op).

5. **Jump-to-jump optimization**
   ```
   goto L1
   ...
   L1: goto L2
   ```
   → Replace `L1: goto L2` with `L1:` falling through, or update the first `goto` to point directly to `L2`.

6. **Branch chaining / unused-label cleanup**

---

### 2.3 Data Structures and Implementation Techniques for Optimizations

These are the **infrastructure** that the optimizer uses. The KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus requires you to know the following data structures and how they support each optimization.

#### 2.3.1 Three-Address Code (TAC) — The IR

**Definition:** An intermediate representation where each instruction has **at most three operands**, typically in the form:
$$x = y \ \text{op} \ z$$
or
$$x = \text{op} \ y$$
or
$$\text{goto} \ L$$

**Why TAC?** It exposes individual operations for analysis; complex expressions are broken into primitives, making data flow tractable.

#### 2.3.2 Basic Blocks

**Definition:** A **basic block** is a **maximal sequence of consecutive three-address instructions** in which:
- **Control flow enters** only at the **first instruction** (the *leader*).
- **Control flow leaves** only at the **last instruction** (i.e., no internal branches except at the end).

**Algorithm to partition TAC into basic blocks (the *leader* algorithm):**
1. The first statement of the IR is a leader.
2. The target of any conditional/unconditional branch is a leader.
3. The statement **immediately following** any branch is a leader.
4. (Optional) The first statement after a function entry is a leader.

**Algorithm to form basic blocks:**
- For each leader, the basic block consists of the leader and all subsequent statements up to (but not including) the next leader or the end of the IR.

#### 2.3.3 Flow Graph (CFG — Control-Flow Graph)

**Definition:** A **directed graph** $G = (N, E)$ where:
- $N$ = set of basic blocks (plus an artificial `ENTRY` and `EXIT` node).
- $E$ = set of edges. An edge $B_i \to B_j$ exists if control *can* flow from the end of $B_i$ to the beginning of $B_j$.

**Edge types:**
- **Fall-through edge:** $B_i$ does not end in a branch; the next block $B_{i+1}$ is its successor.
- **Jump edge:** $B_i$ ends in `goto L` or `if … goto L`; the target block is a successor.
- **Both edges for conditional branches:** `if x goto L1` produces edges to `L1` and to the fall-through block.

#### 2.3.4 Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) for Local CSE

**Definition:** A DAG over a basic block where:
- **Leaf nodes** represent names (operands).
- **Interior nodes** represent operations.
- Each node is labeled with an operation; **duplicate subtrees** (same operator with same children) are merged — the new name is *added* to the merged node's label list.

**Algorithm (Aho/Sethi/Ullman):**
For each instruction `x = y op z` in the block:
1. Search for an existing node $M$ with operator `op` and children $(y, z)$ (or a previously defined $y$ and $z$).
2. If found, $x$ is added to $M$'s label list (this detects CSE).
3. If not found, create a new node.
4. Set the *current definition* of $x$ to this node.

**Important:** The DAG must be rebuilt from scratch when an assignment `x = y` is encountered (kills prior definitions of $x$).

#### 2.3.5 Dominator Tree

**Definition:** Node $d$ **dominates** node $n$ in a CFG if *every* path from `ENTRY` to $n$ must pass through $d$. A node always dominates itself.

**Properties used by optimizers:**
- The **immediate dominator** tree is used to identify loops (a loop's header is dominated by all nodes in the loop, and the back-edge target is the loop's only entry dominated by nodes outside the loop).
- Used in **LICM** to identify *preheader* (a block that dominates the loop header but is dominated only by `ENTRY`).

#### 2.3.6 Use-Definition Chains (ud-chains) and Definition-Use Chains (du-chains)

**Definition:**
- A **ud-chain** for a use $u$ of variable $x$ is the set of *all definitions* of $x$ that can reach $u$ without being killed.
- A **du-chain** for a definition $d$ of $x$ is the set of *all uses* of $x$ that $d$ can reach.

**Use:** These chains are the foundation of constant propagation, copy propagation, and dead-store elimination. The data-flow analysis computes them once; subsequent optimizations query them in $O(1)$ per use.

#### 2.3.7 Data-Flow Analysis Frameworks

| Analysis | Direction | Transfer Function | Meet Operation | Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reaching Definitions** | Forward | $RD_{out}[B] = gen[B] \cup (RD_{in}[B] - kill[B])$ | Union ($\cup$) | Detect uninitialized vars, kill definitions |
| **Available Expressions** | Forward | $AE_{out}[B] = (AE_{in}[B] - kill[B]) \cup gen[B]$ | Intersection ($\cap$) | Global CSE |
| **Live Variable Analysis** | Backward | $LV_{in}[B] = LV_{out}[B] \cup use[B] - def[B]$ | Union ($\cup$) | Dead store elimination, register allocation |
| **Busy Expressions** | Backward | $BE_{out}[B] = (BE_{in}[B] - kill[B]) \cup gen[B]$ | Intersection ($\cap$) | Code motion preconditions |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Mnemonic:** *"Forward uses Union; Backward uses Intersection"* (for the "Available / Busy" classic pair). Available is forward-intersect, Busy is backward-intersect. Getting this wrong loses 2–3 marks.

#### 2.3.8 Symbol Table

The optimizer queries the **symbol table** to know:
- The type of each identifier (for type-checked optimizations).
- The storage class (for dead-store analysis — `volatile` variables must not be optimized away).
- The scope and lifetime (for interprocedural analysis).

#### 2.3.9 Other Implementation Techniques

- **Work-list algorithms:** Iterate over a queue of blocks until a fixed point is reached.
- **SSA (Static Single Assignment) Form:** Each variable is assigned exactly once, making data-flow trivial. The **$\phi$-function** reconverges values at join points.
- **Bit vectors** to represent sets (one bit per definition or expression), enabling fast union/intersection.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a board-exam-ready reference. **Do not use the vertical pipe `|` in any cell** — use `\vert` or `\mid` for any absolute-value or such-that notation.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reaching Definitions (Forward) | $RD_{out}[B] = gen[B] \cup (RD_{in}[B] - kill[B])$ | Meet = $\cup$ |
| 2 | Available Expressions (Forward) | $AE_{out}[B] = gen[B] \cup (AE_{in}[B] - kill[B])$ | Meet = $\cap$; AE is **must** information |
| 3 | Live Variables (Backward) | $LV_{in}[B] = use[B] \cup (LV_{out}[B] - def[B])$ | Meet = $\cup$ |
| 4 | Busy Expressions (Backward) | $BE_{in}[B] = gen[B] \cup (BE_{out}[B] - kill[B])$ | Meet = $\cap$ |
| 5 | Strength Reduction (Multiply by $2^k$) | $x \cdot 2^k \;\equiv\; x \ll k$ | Cheaper than multiply on most CPUs |
| 6 | Strength Reduction (Divide by $2^k$) | $x \,/\, 2^k \;\equiv\; x \gg k$ | For non-negative $x$ (sign-extension rules) |
| 7 | Algebraic Identity | $x \cdot 0 = 0,\;\; x \cdot 1 = x,\;\; x + 0 = x$ | Compiler folds these at compile time |
| 8 | LICM Condition | Expression $E$ is loop-invariant iff **all** operands of $E$ are loop-invariant | Hoist only if $E$ is loop-invariant and $E$ has no side effects |
| 9 | Dominance | $d \; \text{dom} \; n \;\iff\; \forall\, p \in \text{paths}(ENTRY, n), \; d \in p$ | Used to identify preheaders and loops |
| 10 | Flow Graph Edge Count | $\vert E \vert = \sum_{B} \text{succ}(B)$ | Sum of out-degrees of all basic blocks |
| 11 | Amdahl's Law (Speedup) | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{\text{speedup}_f}}$ | $f$ = fraction optimized; bounds optimization benefit |
| 12 | CSE Precondition | $E$ at $p_2$ is a common subexpression of $E$ at $p_1$ iff $E$ is available at $p_2$ | AE analysis gates CSE |
| 13 | DAG Merge Rule | If node $N$ exists with op=op, left=y, right=z, reuse $N$ for $x$ | Avoids recomputation; detects CSE |
| 14 | Peephole Window Size | Typically 2 to 5 instructions | Trade-off: bigger window = more matches, slower compile |
| 15 | SSA Conversion | Each use of $x$ has exactly one reaching definition | $\phi$-functions at CFG join points |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why Optimization Matters |
|---|---|
| **High-Performance Computing (HPC)** | Loop fusion, vectorization, LICM — essential to reach TFLOP performance. |
| **Embedded / IoT** | Code-size reduction (peephole, dead-code, strength reduction) critical to fit in 32 KB flash. |
| **Mobile (Android/iOS)** | Energy optimization — fewer instructions = less battery drain. |
| **Database Engines (SQL compilers)** | Constant folding of predicates, predicate pushdown, join reordering. |
| **GPU Compilers (CUDA, OpenCL)** | Loop unrolling, memory coalescing, register pressure reduction. |
| **AI/ML Compilers (TVM, XLA, MLIR)** | Graph-level optimization, operator fusion, constant folding of weights. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: DAG Construction for Local CSE

**Problem (High-Frequency KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern):**

Given the following three-address code of a basic block, construct the DAG and identify any common subexpressions, then write the optimized TAC.

```
(1) t1 = a + b
(2) t2 = a + b
(3) t3 = t1 - c
(4) t4 = t2 - c
(5) t5 = t1 * 4
(6) t6 = a + b
(7) t7 = t6 + c
(8) t8 = t5 - t7
```

**Solution — Step-by-Step Construction:**

The DAG rules:
- A leaf node is created for each name (operand).
- An interior node represents an operation, labeled with the operator.
- **If a node with the same operator and same children already exists, do NOT create a new node — instead, add the new name to the existing node's label list.**

Let $A(a, b, c)$ denote the current "definition" mapping from name → node.

#### Step 1: Process `t1 = a + b`

- Create leaf $n_0$ for $a$, $n_1$ for $b$.
- No existing node for $(+, n_0, n_1)$, so create $n_2$ with operator `+`, children $n_0, n_1$.
- Labels of $n_2$: $\{t1\}$.
- Current definitions: $A(a) = n_0$, $A(b) = n_1$, $A(t1) = n_2$.

```
    n2 (+)
   /    \
  n0    n1
  (a)   (b)
```

#### Step 2: Process `t2 = a + b`

- Look at current definition of $a$ → $n_0$; of $b$ → $n_1$.
- A node $n_2$ already exists with operator `+` and children $(n_0, n_1)$.
- **MERGE:** Add `t2` to the label list of $n_2$.
- Labels of $n_2$: $\{t1, t2\}$. **(Common subexpression detected!)**
- $A(t2) = n_2$.

#### Step 3: Process `t3 = t1 - c`

- $A(t1) = n_2$; create leaf $n_3$ for $c$.
- No node with `(-, n_2, n_3)$. Create $n_4$ with operator `-`, children $n_2, n_3$.
- Labels of $n_4$: $\{t3\}$.

#### Step 4: Process `t4 = t2 - c`

- $A(t2) = n_2$; $A(c) = n_3$.
- A node $n_4$ already exists with operator `-` and children $(n_2, n_3)$.
- **MERGE:** Add `t4` to the label list of $n_4$.
- Labels of $n_4$: $\{t3, t4\}$. **(Common subexpression detected!)**
- $A(t4) = n_4$.

#### Step 5: Process `t5 = t1 * 4`

- $A(t1) = n_2$; create leaf $n_5$ for the constant `4`.
- No node with $(*, n_2, n_5)$. Create $n_6$ with operator `*`, children $n_2, n_5$.
- Labels of $n_6$: $\{t5\}$.

#### Step 6: Process `t6 = a + b`

- $A(a) = n_0$; $A(b) = n_1$.
- A node $n_2$ already exists with operator `+` and children $(n_0, n_1)$.
- **MERGE:** Add `t6` to the label list of $n_2$.
- Labels of $n_2$: $\{t1, t2, t6\}$. **(Common subexpression detected!)**
- $A(t6) = n_2$.

#### Step 7: Process `t7 = t6 + c`

- $A(t6) = n_2$; $A(c) = n_3$.
- No node with $(+, n_2, n_3)$. Create $n_7$ with operator `+`, children $n_2, n_3$.
- Labels of $n_7$: $\{t7\}$.

#### Step 8: Process `t8 = t5 - t7`

- $A(t5) = n_6$; $A(t7) = n_7$.
- No node with $(-, n_6, n_7)$. Create $n_8$ with operator `-`, children $n_6, n_7$.
- Labels of $n_8$: $\{t8\}$.

#### Final DAG:

```
                n8 (-)
               /     \
             n6 (*)   n7 (+)
            /   \    /    \
          n2(+) n5(4) n2(+) n3(c)
         /   \         ↑
       n0   n1
       (a)  (b)
```

#### Optimized TAC (read leaf-to-interior in topological order, emit one instruction per *new* interior node):

```
t1 = a + b           (new node n2)
t3 = t1 - c          (new node n4)
t5 = t1 * 4          (new node n6)
t7 = t1 + c          (new node n7; reuses t1's node n2)
t8 = t5 - t7         (new node n8)
t2 = t1              (CSE: t2 aliased to t1)
t4 = t3              (CSE: t4 aliased to t3)
t6 = t1              (CSE: t6 aliased to t1)
```

**Original TAC had 8 instructions, optimized TAC has 8 lines but **only 5 actual computations** (the CSE replacements are just copies). The number of additions dropped from 4 to 1, and the number of subtractions from 2 to 1 — a significant speedup.**

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: KTU Optimization Comparison

| Optimization | Scope | Machine-Dep. | Pre-Condition | Data Structure Used | Typical Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Constant Folding | Local/Global | Independent | All operands are constants | TAC, AST | Compile time |
| Constant Propagation | Global | Independent | Only one reaching def, all defs are constants | ud-chains, SSA | Enables further opts |
| CSE | Local/Global | Independent | Expression is *available* at use | DAG (local), AE analysis (global) | $\sim 5\text{–}20\%$ in scientific code |
| Copy Propagation | Global | Independent | Reaching def is a copy of a constant/var | ud-chains | Cascades DCE |
| Dead Code Elimination | Global | Independent | Variable has no live use | LV analysis, ud-chains | $\sim 10\text{–}30\%$ in legacy code |
| Strength Reduction | Local/Global | Both | Pattern matches | TAC, target code | $\sim 5\text{–}10\%$ on integer math |
| LICM | Global | Independent | Expression is loop-invariant + safe to hoist | Dominator tree, preheader | Up to $O(n)$ speedup in nested loops |
| Induction Var. Elim. | Global | Independent | Linear induction var found | IV detection, LICM | $\sim 5\text{–}15\%$ in array code |
| Loop Unrolling | Local/Global | Both | Trip count known/small | Trip count analysis | Reduces branch overhead |
| Peephole | Local | **Dependent** | Pattern in small window | Sliding window over target code | $\sim 2\text{–}5\%$ at the end of pipeline |
| Function Inlining | Interprocedural | Independent | Cost model says "profitable" | Call graph, size estimate | $\sim 10\text{–}30\%$ in C++ templates |
| Register Allocation | Local/Global | **Dependent** | Live ranges computable | LV analysis, interference graph | $O(\#regs)$ instead of memory |

---

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Loop-Invariant Code Motion (LICM)

**Given** a loop $L$ with a candidate expression $E$ inside it, the conditions to hoist $E$ to the preheader $P$ are:

**Condition 1 (Loop-Invariance):** Every operand of $E$ is either:
- A constant, OR
- A variable defined outside $L$, OR
- An induction variable whose value can be computed in the preheader, OR
- A variable whose only definitions inside $L$ are the same constant (loop-invariant itself).

Formally, an instruction `x = y op z` in $L$ is *loop-invariant* if:
$$\text{isInv}(x) \iff \text{isInv}(y) \land \text{isInv}(z)$$
where `isInv(v)` is true iff all reaching definitions of $v$ at this point are from outside $L$ or are themselves loop-invariant.

**Condition 2 (Safety — No Aliasing):** $E$ must not write to a memory location that could be read by another statement in the loop (or vice-versa). Otherwise, hoisting changes the order of memory operations, breaking the semantics. We check this using *memory dependence analysis*.

**Condition 3 (No Side Effects):** $E$ must not have side effects (no I/O, no exceptions, no calls to unknown functions).

**Condition 4 (Preheader Exists):** The dominator tree must have a unique *preheader* block $P$ that dominates the loop header and is dominated only by `ENTRY`. (Some CFGs lack a preheader; one is inserted before LICM.)

**Hoisting Algorithm:**
1. Identify all loop-invariant instructions in $L$ (iterate until fixed point).
2. For each such instruction, verify Conditions 2, 3, 4.
3. Move the instruction to the end of the preheader $P$ (so that $P$'s execution order relative to other invariant code is preserved).
4. Re-iterate because moving one instruction may enable more instructions to become loop-invariant.

**Cost Model:** The expected speedup is
$$S_{LICM} = \frac{N \cdot C_E}{C_E} = N$$
where $N$ is the number of loop iterations and $C_E$ is the cost of the expression, *if* the expression is executed every iteration. In practice, $S \le N$ because of branch overhead and memory effects.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Amdahl's Law (Theoretical Limit of Optimization)

If a fraction $f$ of a program's execution time is spent in code that can be sped up by a factor of $k$ (e.g., $k=4$ if LICM makes the inner loop 4× faster), then the **overall program speedup** is:

$$\text{New execution time} = (1 - f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{k}$$

$$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T}{(1-f) \cdot T + \frac{f \cdot T}{k}} = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{k}}$$

As $k \to \infty$ (i.e., the optimized portion becomes instantaneous):
$$\text{Speedup}_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Engineering Insight:** If 20% of a program is in a loop and we make that loop infinitely fast, the *entire program* can only speed up by $1 / (1 - 0.20) = 1.25\times$ — not infinite. **This is why optimizing only hot loops is essential.**

**Example KTU Sub-Question (3 marks):**
A program spends 30% of its time in an inner loop. After LICM and strength reduction, the inner loop runs 5× faster. What is the overall speedup of the program?

**Solution:**
$$S = \frac{1}{(1-0.30) + \frac{0.30}{5}} = \frac{1}{0.70 + 0.06} = \frac{1}{0.76} \approx 1.316$$

Overall speedup ≈ **1.316×** (i.e., 31.6% faster).
