## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Optimizer Placement in the Compiler Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Source Code] --> B[Lexical Analyzer]
    B --> C[Syntax Analyzer]
    C --> D[Semantic Analyzer]
    D --> E[Intermediate Code Generator]
    E --> F[MACHINE INDEPENDENT OPTIMIZER]
    F --> G[Target Code Generator]
    G --> H[Target Code]
    F -.peephole.-> G
    F --> F1[Constant Folding]
    F --> F2[Common Subexpression Elimination]
    F --> F3[Dead Code Elimination]
    F --> F4[Loop Invariant Code Motion]
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> F4
```

### 4.2 Control-Flow Graph (CFG) Example

Consider a function with the following TAC:

```
L1: t1 = a + b
    if t1 > 0 goto L2
    t2 = a - b
    goto L3
L2: t2 = a * b
L3: print t2
```

The corresponding CFG (nodes are basic blocks):

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ENTRY[ENTRY] --> B1
    B1[Block B1: t1 = a + b; if t1 > 0 goto L2] --> B2
    B1 --> B3
    B2[Block B2: t2 = a - b; goto L3] --> B3
    B3[Block B3: t2 = a * b] --> B4
    B4[Block B4: print t2] --> EXIT[EXIT]
```

Note: In the actual layout, B1's conditional `goto L2` jumps to B3 (the `L2` block), while the fall-through is B2.

### 4.3 DAG Schematic (Section 3.1 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    n0["n0: a (leaf)"] --> n2["n2: + (t1, t2, t6)"]
    n1["n1: b (leaf)"] --> n2
    n2 --> n4["n4: - (t3, t4)"]
    n3["n3: c (leaf)"] --> n4
    n2 --> n6["n6: * (t5)"]
    n5["n5: 4 (leaf)"] --> n6
    n2 --> n7["n7: + (t7)"]
    n3 --> n7
    n6 --> n8["n8: - (t8)"]
    n7 --> n8
```

### 4.4 Classification of Optimizations (Top-Level Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OPT[Code Optimization]
    OPT --> A1[Machine Independent]
    OPT --> A2[Machine Dependent]
    OPT --> B1[Local: Within Basic Block]
    OPT --> B2[Global: Intraprocedural]
    OPT --> B3[Interprocedural]
    OPT --> C1[Function Preserving]
    OPT --> C2[Non Function Preserving]

    A1 --> A1X[Constant Folding, CSE, LICM, DCE, Copy Prop]
    A2 --> A2X[Peephole, Reg Alloc, Instr Scheduling]
    B1 --> B1X[Local CSE, Basic Peephole, Algebraic Simplification]
    B2 --> B2X[Global CSE, LICM, IV Elim, Const Prop]
    B3 --> B3X[Inlining, Interprocedural Const Prop]
    C1 --> C1X[All standard opts]
    C2 --> C2X[FP reassociation, deliberate overflow]
```

### 4.5 Peephole Optimizer (Sliding-Window View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    I1[Instr 1] --> I2[Instr 2] --> I3[Instr 3] --> I4[Instr 4] --> I5[Instr 5]
    I2 -.peephole.-> P1[Pattern: redundant load]
    I3 -.peephole.-> P2[Pattern: mult by 2]
    I4 -.peephole.-> P3[Pattern: add 0]
    P1 --> R1[Replace with nop]
    P2 --> R2[Replace with shift left 1]
    P3 --> R3[Remove instruction]
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Optimizer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IR[Intermediate Code TAC] --> SA[Static Analyzer]
    SA --> DFA[Data Flow Analysis]
    DFA --> CSA[Constant and Subexpression Analysis]
    CSA --> RA[Reaching Definitions]
    RA --> LV[Live Variable Analysis]
    LV --> DAG[DAG Builder for Local CSE]
    DAG --> LICM[Loop Invariant Code Motion Module]
    LICM --> DCE[Dead Code Elimination Module]
    DCE --> CP[Copy Propagation Module]
    CP --> SRP[Strength Reduction Module]
    SRP --> PEEP[Peephole Optimizer]
    PEEP --> OUT[Optimized IR / Target Code]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question says *"Draw the block diagram of the optimization phase"*, the above block diagram is the *board-expected answer*. Add arrows from `DFA` back into `CSA`, `RA`, `LV` (indicating **iteration until fixed point**) for full marks.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**Define code optimization. List any four principal sources of code optimization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Code optimization is the phase of compilation that applies a set of semantic-preserving transformations to the intermediate code of a program in order to improve its execution time, memory usage, or both, while preserving the program's observable input–output behavior.

**Four principal sources (1/2 mark each, total 2 marks):**
1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (CSE):** Reuse of an already-computed expression whose operands are unchanged.
2. **Copy Propagation:** Replacing uses of a copied variable with the original source variable.
3. **Dead Code Elimination:** Removal of code whose computed value is never used.
4. **Strength Reduction:** Replacing an expensive operation (e.g., `x * 2`) with a cheaper equivalent (e.g., `x << 1`).

*(Acceptable alternatives: Constant Folding, Constant Propagation, Loop-Invariant Code Motion, Algebraic Simplification, Induction Variable Elimination.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplementary Paper]**
**Differentiate between machine-dependent and machine-independent optimization. Give one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Machine-Independent | Machine-Dependent |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Optimizations performed on the intermediate representation that depend only on the program's semantics, not on the target CPU. | Optimizations that exploit specific features of the target machine (registers, instruction latencies, pipeline). |
| **Applied to** | Three-address code / IR | Target code (or IR with target info) |
| **Example (1 mark each)** | Common Subexpression Elimination, Constant Folding, LICM, Dead Code Elimination. | Peephole optimization, Register Allocation, Instruction Scheduling. |
| **Example given (1 mark)** | Example: `x * 2` → `x + x` (IR level) | Example: `x * 2` → `x << 1` (target supports fast shift) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Part B questions carry **14 marks** each and offer an **internal choice** (the student must answer either OR). Each Part-B question has **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks**. We provide two full alternative 14-mark questions.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **principal sources of code optimization** in detail. For each source, give the **three-address code transformation** that the optimizer performs.

**(b) [7 marks]** Construct the **DAG** for the following basic block and identify all common subexpressions. Rewrite the optimized TAC.

```
(1) t1 = a * b
(2) t2 = a * b
(3) t3 = 4 * c
(4) t4 = t1 + t3
(5) t5 = a * b
(6) t6 = t2 + t3
(7) t7 = t4 - t6
(8) t8 = 2 * t7
```

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Principal Sources — 7 marks (1 mark for definition, 1/2 mark per source with example, max 7)**

1. **Common Subexpression Elimination (1 mark):** If expression `E` is computed earlier and operands unchanged, replace with reuse. Example: `t2 = a + b; t4 = a + b` → `t2 = a + b; t4 = t2`.

2. **Copy Propagation (1 mark):** Replace use of copied variable with the source. Example: `x = y; z = x * 2` → `x = y; z = y * 2`.

3. **Dead Code Elimination (1 mark):** Remove unused code. Example: `x = 5; y = 10;` if `x` is unused → remove `x = 5;`.

4. **Constant Folding (1/2 mark):** Evaluate constant expressions at compile time. Example: `x = 2 + 3` → `x = 5`.

5. **Constant Propagation (1/2 mark):** Replace variable uses with the constant value. Example: `x = 5; y = x + 2` → `x = 5; y = 7`.

6. **Strength Reduction (1 mark):** Replace expensive ops with cheap equivalents. Example: `x * 2` → `x << 1`; `x * 4` → `x << 2`.

7. **Loop Optimization — LICM (1 mark):** Hoist loop-invariant code. Example:
   ```
   for(i=0;i<n;i++) { t = a+b; x[i] = t*i; }
   ```
   →
   ```
   t = a+b;
   for(i=0;i<n;i++) x[i] = t*i;
   ```

8. **Algebraic Simplification (1/2 mark):** Use identities like `x*1 = x`, `x+0 = x`. Example: `y = x * 1` → `y = x`.

**(b) DAG Construction — 7 marks (DAG diagram 3 marks, CSE list 2 marks, optimized TAC 2 marks)**

**Step-by-step DAG construction (similar to Section 3.1 methodology):**

1. `t1 = a * b` → new node $n_2$ with op `*`, children $n_0(a), n_1(b)$. Label $n_2 = \{t1\}$.
2. `t2 = a * b` → node $n_2$ already exists with op `*`, children $(n_0, n_1)$. **MERGE** $t2$ into $n_2$. Label $n_2 = \{t1, t2\}$.
3. `t3 = 4 * c` → new node $n_4$ with op `*`, children $n_3(4), n_5(c)$. Label $n_4 = \{t3\}$.
4. `t4 = t1 + t3` → $A(t1)=n_2, A(t3)=n_4$. New node $n_6$ with op `+`, children $n_2, n_4$. Label $n_6 = \{t4\}$.
5. `t5 = a * b` → node $n_2$ already exists. **MERGE** $t5$ into $n_2$. Label $n_2 = \{t1, t2, t5\}$.
6. `t6 = t2 + t3` → $A(t2)=n_2, A(t3)=n_4$. Node $n_6$ already exists with op `+`, children $(n_2, n_4)$. **MERGE** $t6$ into $n_6$. Label $n_6 = \{t4, t6\}$.
7. `t7 = t4 - t6` → $A(t4)=n_6, A(t6)=n_6$. **Special rule:** both operands refer to the same node $n_6$. No existing `-` node with $(n_6, n_6)$. Create $n_7$ with op `-`, children $(n_6, n_6)$. Label $n_7 = \{t7\}$.
   *(Note: $t7$ is not a CSE; it is a self-referential subtraction, computable as 0. Most optimizers emit a `t7 = 0` via algebraic simplification.)*
8. `t8 = 2 * t7` → $A(2)=n_8$ (new leaf for 2), $A(t7)=n_7$. New node $n_9$ with op `*`, children $n_8, n_7$. Label $n_9 = \{t8\}$.

**DAG diagram (textual):**
```
                n9 (* t8)
               /     \
             n8(2)   n7 (- t7)
                      |
                     n6 (+ t4, t6)
                    /    \
                  n2(*)   n4(* t3)
                 /   \   /   \
               n0   n1 n3(4) n5(c)
               (a)  (b)
```

**Common Subexpressions Identified (2 marks):**
- $a * b$ computed **three times** ($t1, t2, t5$) → only **one** multiplication needed.
- $t1 + t3$ and $t2 + t3$ are **identical** ($t4, t6$) → only **one** addition needed.
- $t7 = t4 - t6$ — operands are the same, so $t7 = 0$ (algebraic simplification).

**Optimized TAC (2 marks):**
```
t1 = a * b
t3 = 4 * c
t4 = t1 + t3
t7 = 0           (algebraic simplification: t4 - t4 = 0)
t8 = 2 * t7      (or t8 = 0 after constant folding)
t2 = t1          (CSE: t2 aliased)
t5 = t1          (CSE: t5 aliased)
t6 = t4          (CSE: t6 aliased)
```

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]*

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the **block-level functional architecture of the optimization phase** of a compiler. Briefly state the role of each block.

**(b) [7 marks]** Discuss **peephole optimization** in detail. List any **six peephole transformations** with example three-address / target code snippets.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Block Diagram + Roles — 7 marks (diagram 4 marks, role descriptions 3 marks = 1/2 mark each for 6 blocks)**

**Diagram (4 marks):** Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.6 of these notes (Control flow: IR → Static Analyzer → Data Flow Analysis → Reaching Definitions → Live Variable Analysis → DAG Builder → LICM → DCE → Copy Propagation → Strength Reduction → Peephole → Optimized IR).

**Role descriptions (3 marks, 1/2 mark each):**
1. **Static Analyzer:** Parses IR; identifies loops, branches, procedure boundaries.
2. **Data Flow Analysis Module:** Iteratively computes reaching definitions, available expressions, and live variables using fixed-point iteration.
3. **DAG Builder (Local Optimizer):** Builds a DAG per basic block to detect local common subexpressions and apply constant folding and algebraic simplification.
4. **LICM Module:** Hoists loop-invariant code to the preheader.
5. **Dead Code Elimination Module:** Removes assignments to variables that are not live at that point.
6. **Peephole Optimizer:** Operates on small windows of target/IR code, applying local rewrite rules.

**(b) Peephole Optimization — 7 marks (definition 1 mark, 6 transformations × 1 mark each = 6 marks)**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Peephole optimization is a local, machine-dependent code optimization technique that examines a small set of consecutive instructions (a "peephole," typically 2–5 instructions) and replaces them with a more efficient equivalent sequence, preserving program semantics.

**Six Transformations (1 mark each):**

1. **Redundant load/store elimination**
   ```
   MOV R1, a
   MOV a, R1          <-- redundant; a is already in R1
   ```
   → Remove the second `MOV a, R1`.

2. **Strength reduction (multiply to shift)**
   ```
   MUL R1, R2, 2      <-- expensive
   ```
   → `SHL R1, R2, 1` (faster on most CPUs).

3. **Null sequence elimination**
   ```
   ADD R1, R2, 0      <-- adds 0, no effect
   ```
   → Remove the `ADD`.

4. **Algebraic identity**
   ```
   MUL R1, R2, 1      <-- multiply by 1
   ```
   → `MOV R1, R2`.

5. **Constant folding (target level)**
   ```
   MUL R1, R1, 4
   ```
   → If `R1` is known constant, fold at compile time. (More common at IR: `t1 = 2 * 3` → `t1 = 6`.)

6. **Jump-to-jump optimization**
   ```
   goto L1
   ...
   L1: goto L2
   ...
   L2: ...
   ```
   → Replace `L1: goto L2` with fall-through or update the first `goto` to point directly to `L2`.

7. **Unreachable code elimination** (bonus)
   ```
   goto L2
   <unreachable>
   L2: ...
   ```
   → Remove the unreachable code block.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each in KTU 2024 Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "local" with "intraprocedural":** Local optimization = within ONE basic block. Intraprocedural = within one procedure (could be many basic blocks). Mixing these terms is the **#1 mistake** in classification questions.
>
> 2. **Forgetting "semantic preservation":** Every optimization answer should include the phrase *"without changing the program's observable behavior."* Examiners allot 1 mark for this line; missing it = 1 mark lost.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction in data-flow equations:** Constantly mixing up *forward* vs *backward* for Available vs Busy. Mnemonic: **"FABU" → Forward-Available-Backward-Busy (intersection).** Re-derive the equation on the answer sheet — do not blindly copy.
>
> 4. **DAG mistake — missing the `kill` on reassignment:** In some questions, a variable is reassigned inside a block. You must **forget** its previous node in the DAG. Skipping this is a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 5. **Strength reduction sign errors:** `x / 4` for *negative* `x` cannot be `x >> 2` due to arithmetic vs logical shift. State the assumption (non-negative) explicitly in the answer.
>
> 6. **Omitting the preheader in LICM answers:** LICM *requires* a preheader. If the question does not give one, you should **insert one** explicitly. Examiners deduct 1 mark for implicitly assuming it.
>
> 7. **Mixing up the formula for `gen` and `kill`:** In available expressions, $gen[B]$ contains expressions computed in $B$ that are *not* killed later in $B$; $kill[B]$ contains expressions whose operands are redefined. Mixing these is a common 1–2 mark loss.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — print this section before the exam.**

- [ ] **Definition to memorize:** Code optimization is a semantic-preserving transformation of IR that minimizes $T_{exec}$, $M_{code}$, $E_{cpu}$, $S_{code}$.
- [ ] **Position in pipeline:** IR generation → **[Optimization]** → Target code generation.
- [ ] **10 principal sources:** CSE, Copy Propagation, DCE, Constant Folding, Constant Propagation, Strength Reduction, LICM, IV Elimination, Algebraic Simplification, Function Inlining / Peephole.
- [ ] **Three classification axes:**
  - Machine-Dependent vs Machine-Independent
  - Local (one block) vs Global (one procedure) vs Interprocedural (whole program)
  - Function-Preserving vs Non-Function-Preserving
- [ ] **Local ≠ small:** Local = one basic block.
- [ ] **Peephole** is local + machine-dependent.
- [ ] **DAG construction rules:** Merge identical (op, left, right) nodes; reset definition on reassignment; output one instruction per *new* interior node.
- [ ] **Data-flow direction:** Reaching Defs (Forward, $\cup$); Available Expr (Forward, $\cap$); Live Var (Backward, $\cup$); Busy Expr (Backward, $\cap$).
- [ ] **LICM preconditions:** Loop-invariant + safe (no aliasing, no side effects) + preheader exists.
- [ ] **Strength reduction table:** `*2 → <<1`, `/2 → >>1`, `*4 → <<2`, `*8 → <<3`.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{k}}$ — use to bound the *maximum* benefit of any optimization.
- [ ] **Dominance definition:** $d$ dom $n$ iff every path from ENTRY to $n$ contains $d$.
- [ ] **ud-chains / du-chains:** Foundation of constant propagation, copy propagation, DCE.
- [ ] **Key data structures:** Basic blocks, CFG, DAG, dominator tree, ud/du chains, call graph, SSA form with $\phi$-functions.
- [ ] **Engineering metrics to state in answers:** Execution time, memory footprint, energy, code size — always frame optimizations in these terms.
- [ ] **Always state the 4-quadrant matrix** in classification answers: scope × machine-dependence.
- [ ] **LICM benefit upper bound:** $S \le N$ where $N$ is the iteration count (Amdahl's Law).
- [ ] **Final exam mantra:** *"The optimizer must NEVER change the program's observable behavior. It only changes the path taken to compute the result."*
